Key Idea: The core insight is that a decoder-only transformer trained from scratch with modality-specific input/output interfaces but a fully shared backbone, combined with a text-heavy unimodal batch curriculum (92/8), can develop representations suitable for both modalities simultaneously without catastrophic interference. Cross-modal capability emerges passively from shared parameter updates across modalities rather than from architecturally engineered cross-attention or early explicit alignment supervision. Methodology: The architecture is a 16-layer decoder-only transformer (d=1024, 8 GQA query heads, 4 KV heads, SwiGLU, pre-norm RMSNorm, RoPE, 324M parameters total). Time series input: each series is arsinh-normalized (statistics computed over visible values only), segmented into non-overlapping patches of length P=32, and each patch represented by a 4P-dimensional feature vector [time ramp r; normalized values v; binary validity mask m; channel ramp c], projected to d via a single bias-free linear layer + RMSNorm. Text input uses a 131,072-entry BPE vocabulary with tied embedding/LM-head. Output heads are modality-specific: a tied LM head with logit soft-capping (α=15) for text (cross-entropy loss LCE); a 21-quantile head over τ∈[0.05, 0.95] for time series (masked quantile loss LQL). Combined objective: L = LCE + 2.5·LQL (asymmetric weighting to balance gradient magnitudes). Stage 1 (47,683 steps, seq len 2048): 92% text-only batches (FineWeb-Edu + Dolmino-mix), 8% time-series-only batches (GiftEvalPretrain + KernelSynth augmentation); ~150B total tokens. Stage 2 (extended seq len 4096): injects 5% interleaved alignment data from ChatTS synthetic pairs and Merrill et al. descriptions into the TS stream. Optimizer: Muon for transformer block weight matrices; AdamW for embeddings, heads, and norms. Critical design decisions: (1) unimodal batches during bulk pretraining rather than interleaved from the start — justified as preventing modality interference early; (2) 92/8 text-heavy mix — acknowledged as a compute constraint rather than an optimal ratio; (3) channel ramp encoding for multivariate identity — ablated in Appendix E.2 and shown to improve UEA classification by +0.039 accuracy on average. Key Results: NLU (19 tasks, DCLM suite, zero/few-shot): Chronicle Stage 2 achieves 0.406 average, matching Gemma-3-270M-PT (0.406) at comparable scale despite seeing only ~138B text tokens vs. Gemma-3's 6T. On ARC-Easy, Stage 2 scores 0.651 vs. LLaMA-3.2-1B's 0.678 (a model 4× larger trained exclusively on text). GIFT-Eval zero-shot forecasting (97 tasks): Stage 1 = 0.978 MASE / 0.690 CRPS, below all dedicated TSFMs (best: TimesFM-2.5 at 0.705 MASE, PatchTST-FM-r1 at 0.483 CRPS) but above N-BEATS (0.938 MASE), DLinear (1.061), and Auto-ARIMA (0.912 CRPS). Stage 2 regresses to 1.053 MASE. UCR/UEA classification (24 datasets, frozen linear probe, 5 seeds): Stage 1 = 0.736 accuracy / 0.712 F1 vs. Moirai-2 at 0.714/0.692, TimesFM at 0.611/0.563, Chronos-2 at 0.376/0.230. Exceeds all per-dataset supervised DL baselines except FEDformer (0.723). Time-MMD multimodal forecasting (9 domains, LP head on frozen backbone): Stage 2 (LP) = 0.514 NMAE / avg rank 2.56, outperforming BERT+TNet (0.621 NMAE) and GPT2+Moi2 (0.588 NMAE). TimeCAP multimodal classification: Stage 2 LoRA (r=64) = 0.613 F1; Stage 1 LoRA (r=64) = 0.763 AUC — both best across all baselines over 3 seeds. Baselines on GIFT-Eval and UCR are drawn from the current public leaderboard and are strong and up-to-date. Evaluation scale: standard benchmarks, 324M parameters; no stress-scale experiments at larger model sizes. Novelty Classification: NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The from-scratch joint training paradigm is genuinely novel — no prior work has trained a single model on both modalities from random initialization. The cross-domain evaluation protocol against dedicated specialists in each modality is also new to the multimodal TS literature. The architecture itself (decoder-only transformer with patch embedding, quantile output head) is a straightforward combination of PatchTST-FM and GPT-2 style components; the novelty is in the training strategy and evaluation discipline, not architectural invention. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are present but incomplete: the Stage 1 vs. Stage 2 trade-off is cleanly documented across all benchmarks; the TS-token repetition sweep (Figure 3) is thorough; the channel-aware vs. mean-channel pooling ablation (Figure 4) covers all 10 UEA datasets. However, the critical missing ablation is a Chronicle-architecture model trained on 100% time series — without it, the cost of joint training for TS quality cannot be isolated from architecture and objective differences relative to PatchTST-FM or TimesFM. Error bars are reported for TimeCAP (3 seeds) and UCR/UEA (5 seeds) but not for GIFT-Eval or NLU (single runs). Code and checkpoints are stated as publicly available on Hugging Face and GitHub, but URLs in the preprint are placeholder strings ([HUGGINGFACE_LINK], [GITHUB_LINK]) — not accessible at paper submission time. The paper is a preprint (arXiv:2605.20268v1); results have not undergone peer review. Training data (FineWeb-Edu, Dolmino-mix) and evaluation suite (DCLM) partially overlap in distribution, which is standard practice but may inflate NLU comparisons against models trained on less curated text. Deployability (EMPIRICAL): - Practical: Stage 1+2 training runs on 2×H100 80GB GPUs in roughly one week; inference is standard autoregressive decoding runnable on a single GPU. - Efficient: 324M parameters is compact relative to the 7–14B baselines it surpasses on multimodal tasks; however, autoregressive next-patch forecasting is slower than one-shot bidirectional TSFMs for long-horizon prediction. - Effective: Evidence is strong for frozen-embedding classification and head-trained multimodal forecasting; zero-shot forecasting is substantially weaker than dedicated specialists, a limitation the paper explicitly measures and explains. - Scalable: No scaling experiments beyond 324M parameters; the paper asserts the forecasting gap is addressable with more compute or a curriculum strategy but provides no evidence at larger scale. The paper delivers stated model checkpoints (URLs pending release) and evaluation code as software artifacts. Strengths: - The finding that 8% time series compute in a 324M joint model does not cause catastrophic interference — quantified against scale-matched LLMs across 19 NLU tasks — is the first clean empirical proof of this claim and directly refutes the implicit assumption underlying all prior post-hoc LLM adaptation work. - The evaluation protocol is the most rigorous in the multimodal TS literature: evaluating against the public GIFT-Eval leaderboard, dedicated TSFM frozen probes, and scale-matched LLMs simultaneously closes a longstanding gap that prior work had not addressed. - The frozen-embedding UCR/UEA classification result (0.736 accuracy, beating Moirai-2 at 0.714 and per-dataset supervised DL baselines with a single backbone) is a substantive empirical contribution, not just a tie. - The TS-token repetition trick (Section 5.4) is a simple, architecture-agnostic solution to the modality-imbalance problem in shared autoregressive backbones, with a clean ablation showing ~3 accuracy and ~4 AUC points of headroom — practically useful for downstream classification tasks with short time series. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors directly confront the forecasting gap to dedicated TSFMs, attributing it to two principled design choices: the 8% compute allocation and the causal next-patch objective vs. contiguous patch masking used by PatchTST-FM. They explicitly suggest curriculum strategies (TS-only pretraining followed by joint continued pretraining) and hybrid attention objectives as remedies, and note that Stage 2 covers only 5% of TS tokens with cross-modal supervision — a larger alignment stage is called out as the most promising direction. They also acknowledge the scope restriction to frozen representation learning vs. conversational agents. Overall the limitations section is unusually candid. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The most consequential missing ablation is a Chronicle-architecture model trained on 100% time series at the same compute budget as Stage 1 — without this, it is impossible to determine whether the gap to PatchTST-FM is due to (a) joint training hurting TS quality, (b) the causal next-patch objective, (c) the lower TS compute, or some combination; the paper attributes it to (b) and (c) but cannot isolate them. The Time-MMD LP comparison is partially apples-to-oranges: Chronicle's LP head is trained with joint text+TS input (backbone sees both), while FM Fusion baselines freeze both encoders and only train the head — Chronicle thus benefits from cross-modal conditioning during fine-tuning in a way the baselines do not. The NLU comparison favors Chronicle on data quality: Chronicle uses FineWeb-Edu (highly filtered) while GPT-2's ~10B tokens are much lower quality Common Crawl — the 43× fewer token count comparison is accurate but potentially misleading about the actual information advantage. No per-domain analysis on Time-MMD breaks down which domains benefit from text conditioning vs. not, which would be critical for understanding when joint training actually helps rather than just matching well-tuned unimodal baselines. The paper does not evaluate retrieval quality of the time series embeddings or robustness to domain shift. Bottom Line: Chronicle establishes the right evaluation methodology for multimodal time series models and produces the first credible evidence that joint from-scratch training is feasible at compact scale without catastrophic interference; practitioners building systems where time series and text co-occur should read it, but the large zero-shot forecasting gap to dedicated specialists means it is not yet a viable replacement for production forecasting pipelines.

Self-Improving Language Models with Bidirectional Evolutionary Search

arxiv.org · 📄 Open PDF
**TL;DR** - **What it does:** This paper introduces Bidirectional Evolutionary Search (BES), a search framework that escapes the model's autoregressive probability shell by recombining partial trajectories via four biological-evolution-inspired operators and by recursively decomposing problems into verifiable sub-goals that supply dense intermediate feedback. - **Why it's important:** On hard tasks, correct solutions lie in low-probability regions of the model's distribution; standard search methods (best-of-N, tree search) are confined to the high-probability entropy shell, causing mainstream post-training algorithms to flatline or degrade on challenging benchmarks. - **How it does it:** Forward search applies trajectory recombination (combination, translocation, crossover, deletion) to splice segments from different rollouts, while backward search constructs a goal tree by recursively decomposing the root task into leaf sub-goals, scoring partial trajectories against how many sub-goals they address. - **The good:** On multi-hop reasoning (MuSiQue), BES achieves +3.0% and +3.8% absolute accuracy over the base model on 3B and 8B models respectively, where GRPO degrades (-1.0 to -1.9%) and Tree-GRPO barely improves; on three open mathematical optimization benchmarks it outperforms all open-source frameworks with substantially lower run-to-run variance. - **The missing:** The backward search component is never ablated independently from the evolution operators — a condition of evolution-only (no sub-goal decomposition) does not appear — so the relative contribution of the two core innovations remains unquantified. - **Read it or not:** Researchers working on test-time compute, post-training data generation, or agentic search should read this; the entropy-shell theorem and sub-goal complexity result are independently useful, and the empirical results specifically on regimes where baselines fail are the most credible part of the case.
Full summary
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Bidirectional Evolutionary Search (BES), a search framework that escapes the model's autoregressive probability shell by recombining partial trajectories via four biological-evolution-inspired operators and by recursively decomposing problems into verifiable sub-goals that supply dense intermediate feedback. - Why it's important: On hard tasks, correct solutions lie in low-probability regions of the model's distribution; standard search methods (best-of-N, tree search) are confined to the high-probability entropy shell, causing mainstream post-training algorithms to flatline or degrade on challenging benchmarks. - How it does it: Forward search applies trajectory recombination (combination, translocation, crossover, deletion) to splice segments from different rollouts, while backward search constructs a goal tree by recursively decomposing the root task into leaf sub-goals, scoring partial trajectories against how many sub-goals they address. - The good: On multi-hop reasoning (MuSiQue), BES achieves +3.0% and +3.8% absolute accuracy over the base model on 3B and 8B models respectively, where GRPO degrades (-1.0 to -1.9%) and Tree-GRPO barely improves; on three open mathematical optimization benchmarks it outperforms all open-source frameworks with substantially lower run-to-run variance. - The missing: The backward search component is never ablated independently from the evolution operators — a condition of evolution-only (no sub-goal decomposition) does not appear — so the relative contribution of the two core innovations remains unquantified. - Read it or not: Researchers working on test-time compute, post-training data generation, or agentic search should read this; the entropy-shell theorem and sub-goal complexity result are independently useful, and the empirical results specifically on regimes where baselines fail are the most credible part of the case. Problem & Motivation LLMs and agents need high-quality candidate trajectories both for post-training (as training data) and at inference time. On difficult problems — multi-hop reasoning requiring multiple retrievals, logic puzzles with large variable counts, combinatorial optimization — the model assigns extremely small probability mass to correct solutions. Best-of-N sampling improves coverage only linearly with sample count, while tree search builds candidates one lineage at a time, remaining confined to the model's own distribution. This creates a practical ceiling: on challenging variants of MuSiQue and Knights-and-Knaves, GRPO and MaxRL consistently fail to discover sufficient training positives and either flatline or degrade from the base model. The gap between what existing search can find and what hard tasks demand is the central problem. Key Idea Autoregressive extension of a trajectory stays within a narrow entropy shell because every generated token conditions on the same single prefix lineage — the distribution never mixes across independent rollouts. Recombining blocks from distinct rollouts (analogous to chromosomal crossover in sexual reproduction) produces candidates whose log-probability under the model exceeds the shell boundary by an amount proportional to the block total correlation of the trajectory distribution. This is the entropy-shell escape insight. The second insight, for backward search, is that replacing a single binary terminal verifier with m independent sub-goal verifiers transforms the sample requirement from Ω(1/∏p_i) — exponential in the number of sub-goals — down to O(p_min^{-1} log(m/δ)), because each sub-goal can be satisfied independently and then combined. Methodology BES maintains a candidate pool of partial trajectories and alternates between forward search steps and backward scoring, re-triggering backward decomposition every K_dec forward steps. The forward search applies five operators sampled with fixed probabilities (expansion 0.70, combination 0.10, translocation 0.075, crossover 0.075, deletion 0.05). Expansion samples K ~ Uniform{1,...,K_max} new steps from the policy autoregressively. Combination concatenates the distinct suffixes of two trajectories beyond their shared prefix. Deletion removes one interior step. Translocation transplants one step from trajectory B into trajectory A at position r. Crossover splices the prefix of A with the tail of B at a cut point. Single-parent operators select parents via Boltzmann distribution over backward scores; two-parent operators use a pair score that favors complementary parents maximizing joint sub-goal coverage. Temperature is annealed linearly from τ_0 to τ_end. The backward goal tree starts at a root goal and recursively decomposes unsatisfied leaves; node score s(n,g) = α·V_g(x,n) + (1-α)·mean_{g'∈ch(g)} s(n,g'), with α balancing coarse and fine signals. Verifiers are task-specific: rule-based exact match for K&K, embedding cosine similarity (all-MiniLM-L6-v2, threshold 0.6) for MuSiQue sub-questions, and Python expression evaluators returning [0,1] for open problem solving. For executable-program tasks (open problem solving), evolution operators cannot concatenate tokens directly and are instead realized as LLM-prompted rewrites with operator-specific instructions. Critical design decisions: (1) The 70/30 expansion/evolution split is fixed and not ablated individually per operator — empirically motivated but not justified from first principles; (2) pair score uses max over parents' sub-goal scores, incentivizing complementarity rather than quality, which is well-motivated but sensitive to backward tree quality; (3) for Gemma-3-1B-it (too small for open-ended decomposition), the backward search degrades to template-based scheduling — a significant deviation from the general framework not prominently flagged. Key Results Logical Reasoning (Knights-and-Knaves, Gemma-3-1B-it, 5K training problems): BES improves steadily across 1,000 gradient steps while GRPO and MaxRL show little to no improvement (EMA-smoothed curves in log-accuracy space; exact percentages not reported in text). Multi-Hop Reasoning (MuSiQue, 3-4 hop subset): BES reaches 7.0% (+3.0% over base) on Llama-3.2-3B and 10.4% (+3.8%) on Llama-3.1-8B; GRPO degrades to 2.1%/5.6%; Tree-GRPO (ICLR 2026 baseline, cited as state-of-the-art) reaches 3.9%/7.4%. BES finish ratios (0.97/0.94) substantially exceed Tree-GRPO (0.64/0.71), indicating trained agents learn to actually search rather than guess. Open Problem Solving (GPT-5 backbone, 3 runs each): Circle Packing Square — BES avg 2.623±0.014 vs. GEPA 2.613±0.022; Circle Packing Rect — BES avg 2.349±0.012 vs. GEPA 2.326±0.023; Heilbronn Convex — BES avg 0.026±0.001 vs. GEPA/OpenEvolve 0.025±0.002. BES exhibits dramatically lower standard deviation than all open-source baselines. Best-case scores remain slightly below AlphaEvolve (which uses significantly more compute and is closed-source). BES incurs ~29% more wall-clock time than Tree-GRPO per training step (309s vs. 240s) and 30-43% more API cost than ShinkaEvolve at inference. Baselines are current (GRPO, Tree-GRPO from ICLR 2026; ShinkaEvolve, GEPA from 2025-2026). Novelty Classification NEW METHOD. The specific coupling of biological recombination operators with backward sub-goal decomposition applied to LLM trajectory search is novel. Individually, the ingredients are known: genetic crossover operators trace to Holland (1992), bidirectional search is classical AI (Hart 1968), and MCTS-guided sub-goal decomposition appears in prior work. The genuine contribution is (a) the entropy-shell formalization proving confinement of autoregressive expansion and the escape guarantee for recombination, and (b) the exponential sample-complexity advantage theorem for backward sub-goal search — neither of which appeared in prior search-for-LLM work. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present but partial: Figure 4 shows that removing evolution operators or answer reweighting each degrades performance on K&K, confirming both components contribute. However, the ablation is conducted only on logical reasoning, not on multi-hop or open problem solving, and does not isolate individual evolution operators or the backward search independently. Multi-hop results are single-number accuracy values (no standard deviation, no multiple seeds). Open problem solving reports mean ± std across 3 runs. Open-source baseline results for open problem solving are taken from SkyDiscover [22] rather than rerun — claimed to use the same backbone and budget but from a third-party system, introducing implementation uncertainty. Code and trained models are released at https://github.com/Embodied-Minds-Lab/BES. This is a preprint (arXiv:2605.28814v1); results are not peer-reviewed. The K&K backward search silently falls back to template-based scheduling because the policy model is too small for open-ended decomposition — this is disclosed in Appendix D.1 but not flagged in the main text. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL - Practical: Post-training uses 2 H200 GPUs plus 1-2 auxiliary GPUs for the backward decomposer and retriever; inference uses GPT-5 via API — commercially accessible configurations. - Efficient: BES adds ~29% wall-clock overhead vs. Tree-GRPO in post-training and 30-43% API cost overhead vs. ShinkaEvolve at inference; the dominant cost is LLM calls for backward decomposition and pair-score computation (quadratic in pool size) — moderate but not prohibitive. - Effective: On post-training, BES demonstrates large gains specifically in the hard regime where baselines degrade; on open problem solving, improvements over best open-source are small in absolute terms (< 0.013 objective units on circle packing) but consistent and with meaningfully lower variance, suggesting better reliability rather than lucky best-case runs. - Scalable: Post-training experiments cap at 8B parameters; no experiments at 70B+ scale. Training collapse at >2 epochs for multi-hop is unresolved and suggests fragility at longer training horizons. Open problem solving uses a single GPT-5 backend with no scaling analysis. The work delivers a software artifact (code + trained models) and a reusable framework applicable to any task with an objective verifier. Strengths - Theorem 4.4 provides a non-trivial, formally rigorous explanation for why expansion-only search is fundamentally limited, going beyond empirical observation to pinpoint the mechanism (block total correlation and entropy-shell confinement). - Theorem 4.5 gives a clean exponential sample-complexity separation between terminal-only and backward-guided search, independently useful for reasoning about verifier design. - The empirical strategy of testing specifically on regimes where baselines fail or degrade is methodologically sound — it isolates the conditions under which BES provides genuine value rather than marginal improvement on already-solved settings. - Substantially lower variance on open problem solving (σ=0.001 for Heilbronn vs. σ=0.002-0.005 for competitors) is a concrete reliability advantage beyond raw performance, and is the most striking open-problem result. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly state: (1) BES requires an objective reward signal and has not been tested on subjective tasks like academic writing; (2) backward search depends on the policy's decomposition capability and degrades for weak models; (3) post-training experiments are limited to models ≤8B parameters due to resource constraints. These are stated directly in Appendix H and constitute credible self-assessment. Analyst-Identified Gaps - Individual evolution operator ablations are absent: the paper shows evolution operators collectively matter but not which of the four contribute, or whether the 70% expansion probability is tuned or principled — given that translocation is what solves the case study example, its individual contribution deserves isolation. - The backward search is never ablated independently: a condition of evolution operators alone (without sub-goal decomposition, using only terminal verification) would directly test whether the two theoretical claims translate into separable empirical contributions. - The K&K backward search falls back to template scheduling with Gemma-3-1B-it, meaning the backward decomposition result in that experiment is not the same mechanism described in the theory; the boundary conditions for when the general framework applies are unstated. - Open problem solving baselines (OpenEvolve, GEPA, ShinkaEvolve) come from a third-party benchmark system rather than being directly rerun, which introduces implementation heterogeneity that is not quantified. - Assumption 4.3 (linear block total correlation) is theoretically justified but not empirically verified for the Llama or Gemma models used; the shell-escape bound (Eq. 9) only guarantees a strictly positive fraction escapes — the actual fraction is not quantified, leaving the practical exploitation rate of escaped candidates unclear. - Multi-hop training collapses after 2 epochs for all methods, suggesting reward hacking or instability that BES does not fully address; this dynamics question goes unanalyzed. Bottom Line Any practitioner designing search-based post-training pipelines or inference-time compute strategies for hard reasoning tasks will find concrete, implementable contributions here — the entropy-shell analysis sharpens intuition about why evolutionary recombination helps and the MuSiQue results on models where GRPO fails are the strongest evidence — though the partial ablations and small-scale post-training experiments mean this should be read as a strong empirical proof-of-concept rather than a definitive characterization of the method's properties.

Frontier: Towards Comprehensive and Accurate LLM Inference Simulation

arxiv.org · 📄 Open PDF
**TL;DR** - **What it does:** This paper introduces Frontier, a discrete-event simulator for modern LLM inference serving that models disaggregated architectures (Prefill-Decode Disaggregation and Attention-FFN Disaggregation), runtime optimizations (CUDA Graph, speculative decoding, prefix caching), and stateful workloads (multi-round reasoning, agentic tool calls, RL rollouts). - **Why it's important:** Existing simulators make configuration recommendations that violate real SLAs in practice — average-case models produce up to 51.7% end-to-end latency error and can select configurations that fail SLA constraints on real hardware, undermining the core purpose of simulation-driven capacity planning. - **How it does it:** The simulator replaces the monolithic-replica abstraction with role-specific cluster workers whose cross-cluster dependencies (KV-cache transfer, activation ping-pong) are materialized as explicit discrete events, and replaces token-count proxies with class-specific ML predictors (random forests over distributional batch features for attention, load-balance statistics for MoE GroupedGEMM). - **The good:** Frontier reduces E2E latency error from 51.7% to 2.6% under PDD disaggregation and from 44.9% to 6.4% under co-location, compared to the prior state-of-the-art simulators, validated on a real 16-GPU H800 testbed with vLLM v0.10.2. - **The missing:** Use-case results — including the 30.4% aTTFT reduction from the phase-aware H2Q-BR scheduler and the 2.04× RL rollout throughput gain from dynamic layout switching — are demonstrated entirely in simulation and never validated on actual hardware, which is a significant credibility gap for a tool whose primary value is replacing expensive hardware experiments. - **Read it or not:** Systems researchers and infrastructure engineers who configure, evaluate, or build disaggregated LLM serving stacks should read this; practitioners looking for validated scheduling algorithms should treat the §6 use cases as design hypotheses, not confirmed results.
Full summary
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Frontier, a discrete-event simulator for modern LLM inference serving that models disaggregated architectures (Prefill-Decode Disaggregation and Attention-FFN Disaggregation), runtime optimizations (CUDA Graph, speculative decoding, prefix caching), and stateful workloads (multi-round reasoning, agentic tool calls, RL rollouts). - Why it's important: Existing simulators make configuration recommendations that violate real SLAs in practice — average-case models produce up to 51.7% end-to-end latency error and can select configurations that fail SLA constraints on real hardware, undermining the core purpose of simulation-driven capacity planning. - How it does it: The simulator replaces the monolithic-replica abstraction with role-specific cluster workers whose cross-cluster dependencies (KV-cache transfer, activation ping-pong) are materialized as explicit discrete events, and replaces token-count proxies with class-specific ML predictors (random forests over distributional batch features for attention, load-balance statistics for MoE GroupedGEMM). - The good: Frontier reduces E2E latency error from 51.7% to 2.6% under PDD disaggregation and from 44.9% to 6.4% under co-location, compared to the prior state-of-the-art simulators, validated on a real 16-GPU H800 testbed with vLLM v0.10.2. - The missing: Use-case results — including the 30.4% aTTFT reduction from the phase-aware H2Q-BR scheduler and the 2.04× RL rollout throughput gain from dynamic layout switching — are demonstrated entirely in simulation and never validated on actual hardware, which is a significant credibility gap for a tool whose primary value is replacing expensive hardware experiments. - Read it or not: Systems researchers and infrastructure engineers who configure, evaluate, or build disaggregated LLM serving stacks should read this; practitioners looking for validated scheduling algorithms should treat the §6 use cases as design hypotheses, not confirmed results. Problem & Motivation LLM inference has become the dominant operational cost for service providers, and the configuration space for modern serving systems is enormous — over 1,000 combinations for a 200B-parameter MoE model, with sweeping 100 configurations on a 64-GPU H100 cluster costing $128,000–$180,000. Simulation is the natural tool for design-space exploration, but existing simulators (Vidur, AIConfigurator, APEX, LLMServingSim2.0) fail in two ways that make them unreliable for production decisions. First, they assume a homogeneous set of monolithic replicas, which cannot represent disaggregated serving architectures (PDD, AFD) where different GPU pools play distinct roles connected by explicit KV-cache or activation transfers. Second, they smooth serving dynamics into average-case models: token-count proxies cause 32.6% mean FlashAttention latency error and 21.0% mean MoE GroupedGEMM error, while "total minus weights" KV-cache budget estimation overestimates the admissible budget by 8.1%–39.73% across configurations — errors large enough to shift TTFT/TPOT estimates across SLA boundaries and recommend configurations that fail on real systems. Key Idea The core insight is that LLM serving is a closed-loop system in which operator runtime errors, KV-cache budget errors, and scheduler admission decisions compound: a 0.1 ms per-operator error propagates across hundreds of decode steps, the scheduler uses the KV-cache budget to bound concurrent decode slots, and batch composition is determined online, making the execution space infeasible to profile exhaustively. The key technical move is to resolve all three sources of error together — materializing disaggregated dependencies as explicit events in the DES graph (so the event ordering enforces correctness), and classifying operators by what actually determines their runtime (token count, per-request length distribution, or expert routing load) so that the right ML predictor feature set is applied to each class. Methodology Frontier is organized into four modules. The Control Plane compiles a serving specification into role-specific Cluster Workers: co-location yields a monolithic cluster C; PDD yields prefill cluster P and decode cluster D; AFD yields P plus separate attention cluster A and FFN cluster F. Parallelism is expressed via a two-domain decomposition with attention sharding tuple (tp_attn, dp_attn) and FFN sharding tuple (tp_ffn, ep_ffn). Five dependency classes are materialized as explicit events: (i) pipeline stage chaining, (ii) PDD KV-cache transfer (KVCacheTransferStart/End causal edges), (iii) AFD per-layer M2N activation transfer ping-pong between A and F, (iv) MoE EP synchronization (AllToAllCombineReady fires only after the slowest rank), and (v) stateful reasoning requeue (ThinkingRequeue event re-admits the request with session affinity for prefix-cache continuity). The Execution Plane lifts production scheduler logic from vLLM and SGLang unchanged, rewiring only the I/O layer; Runtime Adapters inject CUDA Graph (padding to next bin, switching between kernel-only and launch-inclusive timing families), speculative decoding MTP (per-request planned/verified/accepted/committed token tracking), prefix caching (block-hash index), and chunked prefill. The Fidelity Plane applies three operator classes with distinct predictors: token-count operators (linear ops) use linear regression or small random forest on num_tokens; sequence-dependent operators (attention) use random forest over aggregate counts plus distributional statistics of per-request lengths (min, max, percentiles of prefill and decode lengths); routing-dependent operators (MoE GroupedGEMM) use random forest over load-balance statistics (variance and max of token-to-expert counts, expert selection ratio). Memory capacity is estimated via a vLLM-style dummy profile run on a single GPU, measuring weight memory, torch peak increase, and non-torch residency (NCCL workspace, CUDA Graph capture regions); the KV-cache block budget subtracts all three from the requested GPU budget. The Comm. Backend is pluggable (ASTRA-Sim or HTSim), selected by domain scale. The system is implemented in approximately 70K lines of Python, with 808 typed CLI options. Key Results All fidelity experiments use vLLM v0.10.2 (V1 engine) as ground truth on two 8×H800-SXM GPU servers. Operator-level: BF16 p50/p95 relative error is 3.5%/14.2% for attention, 3.3%/6.4% for linear ops, 1.4%/5.3% for MoE GroupedGEMM; Vidur's token-only predictor reaches 55.4%/376.1% for attention and is undefined for GroupedGEMM. FP8 shifts attention p95 to 8.8%. Memory: Initial KV-cache block budget is within 1.89% of vLLM across all four (PP,TP,DP,EP) configurations tested; the analytical baseline overestimates by 14.10%–39.73%. Runtime optimizations: CUDA Graph decode speedup is matched within 1.7% (co-location) and 6.1% (PDD); prefix-cache final hit ratios match exactly (36.98% and 37.11%); MTP speculative decoding p95 errors stay within 11.28% across six configurations (verify lengths {2,8,32} × acceptance ratios {0.3,0.7}). End-to-end: Under co-location, Frontier tracks vLLM within 9.37% across 32 cases (dense and MoE, four workload patterns), with TPOT-p95 and E2E inside 6.4% in all but one; AIConfigurator produces E2E makespan errors up to 170% on dense decode-heavy. Under PDD, all 32 entries are within 10.99% with 20 of 32 inside 3%; AIConfigurator reaches 200% E2E error on both dense and MoE decode-heavy (architectural failure due to missing KV-cache transfer dependency). Under AFD (Step3-316B, 16 H800s), TPOT is within 6.4% and throughput within 7.0% for both FFN-TP and FFN-EP layouts. Frontier scales to 1K+ GPU simulations on commodity CPU hardware (wall-clock time vs. cluster size is not benchmarked). Four use cases are presented using ex-situ simulation only: SLA-aware Pareto frontier comparison shows PDD outperforms at loose TTFT ≤3000ms (137.4K tok/s) while AFD is better at tight TTFT ≤500ms (116.2K tok/s vs. PDD 100.7K tok/s); heterogeneous GPU allocation shows H20 decode pool in PDD at P:D=1:1 passes SLA (CE=1.33) but P:D=2:6 fails SLA despite SR=1.69; a phase-aware H2Q-BR scheduler achieves 30.4% p95 aTTFT reduction over vLLM default; dynamic RL rollout reconfiguration halves makespan from 528.8 s to 259.1 s with 2.04× decode throughput. Novelty Classification NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW METHOD. The disaggregated abstraction — compiling serving specifications into role-specific cluster workers with explicit event-graph dependencies for PDD/AFD/MoE-EP — is genuinely novel as a simulator architecture. The class-specific operator predictor design (distributional batch features for attention, load statistics for MoE routing) is a real methodological contribution to simulation fidelity. Individual components (DES simulation, random forests, vLLM scheduler logic) are not new, but the specific co-design of architectural completeness and decision-grade fidelity with quantified evidence for each failure mode is original and practically significant. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are partial: each fidelity component (operator accuracy, memory budget, CUDA Graph, prefix cache, speculative decoding) is evaluated separately before the end-to-end comparison, which supports attribution. However, error bars and confidence intervals are absent throughout — all numbers are point estimates from single runs. The testbed is 16 H800 GPUs; the claimed 1K+ GPU scalability is stated but not benchmarked (simulation wall-clock as a function of cluster size is not reported). AFD calibration uses an in-house Step3-316B implementation with no publicly available ground truth, limiting independent verification. Code is not announced as publicly released; the paper describes 70K LoC Python but provides no repository. The use-case experiments (§6) are simulation-only with no hardware validation — the H2Q-BR scheduler (30.4% aTTFT reduction) and RL reconfiguration (2.04× throughput) are presented as findings of the simulator, but the simulator itself is the artifact being evaluated, creating a circularity. Comparisons are against current open-source simulator versions as of April 2026, with discrepancies resolved against repositories rather than papers, which is methodologically reasonable but creates an asymmetric characterization of competitors. Deployability (SYSTEMS) Profile: SYSTEMS — a built simulation tool evaluated through deployment fidelity comparisons. - Practical: Frontier can simulate 1K+ GPU clusters on commodity CPUs; single-GPU profiling is sufficient to generate operator and memory predictors for any cluster scale. - Efficient: Single-GPU dummy profile runs replace full-cluster hardware sweeps; the 70K LoC Python implementation may impose non-trivial simulation wall-clock for very large-scale or long-horizon traces, but this is uncharacterized. - Effective: Evidence of solving the stated problem is strong — sub-10% TTFT/TPOT errors across co-location and PDD on real hardware for two model families and four workload patterns; the SLA decision drift experiment (Figure 5) concretely demonstrates that prior simulators recommend configurations that violate SLA on real hardware. - Scalable: 1K+ GPU simulation on commodity CPU is claimed but not measured; the single-representative-rank PP profiling approach is acknowledged to contribute residual errors in pipeline-parallel configurations, and no results above 256 GPU equivalents are presented. The work delivers a software artifact (Python simulator), though no public release is announced. Strengths - The motivation section (§2.2) establishes each failure mode of existing simulators with real vLLM measurements — CUDA Graph padding inflation (22.6%–57.2% extra tokens), KV budget overestimation (up to 39.73%), and MTP sign-mismatches (4 of 5 low-acceptance batches predicted to slow down when they actually speed up) — grounding the design choices in quantified evidence rather than intuition. - The operator classification into token-count / sequence-dependent / routing-dependent classes, each with appropriate ML predictor feature sets, is well-motivated: Vidur's single token-count regressor has 376.1% p95 attention error precisely because attention runtime is shaped by per-request length distribution inside a batch, not aggregate token count, and the paper correctly identifies and fixes this. - The disaggregated abstraction correctly identifies that PDD/AFD are not expressible by adding latency terms to a monolithic-replica model — the event graph changes fundamentally, and AIConfigurator's 200% PDD error is a direct consequence of the missing KV-cache transfer causal edge, not parameter miscalibration. - The four use cases demonstrate analysis that is not reproducible with any prior simulator: the SLA-conditional Pareto frontier reversal (PDD preferred at loose TTFT, AFD preferred at tight TTFT) is a decision that would be made incorrectly with prior tools, and the gate-based heterogeneous GPU analysis (H20 on FFN fails, H20 on attention passes) is a role-level finding that requires the disaggregated cost model. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly acknowledge: (1) Frontier is primarily calibrated around vLLM, with SGLang and TensorRT-LLM support deferred to future work; (2) CPU overhead modeling may lack robustness as it fluctuates with system scale and architectural updates, and CUDA API interception is discussed as a more accurate alternative but debated for scalability across diverse CUDA versions; (3) profiling tools introduce intrusive overhead, requiring strict isolation of fine-grained metrics from system-level metrics; (4) AFD calibration uses an in-house implementation because no public AFD implementation exists. The authors confront these limitations directly, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The use-case scheduling and reconfiguration results (§6.3 H2Q-BR: 30.4% aTTFT reduction; §6.4 layout switch: 2.04× throughput) are validated entirely within the simulator whose fidelity is the subject of the paper — no hardware deployment validates these policies. The paper's own motivation (Figure 5) shows that prior simulators recommend configurations that fail on real hardware; the same risk applies here, yet the use cases present simulation outcomes as findings without this caveat. - The testbed (16 H800 GPUs) is small relative to the claimed use cases (256-GPU and 1K-GPU scenarios), meaning all large-scale results are produced by a tool validated only at 16-GPU scale — extrapolation risk is substantial and unquantified. - Simulation wall-clock time as a function of cluster size, trace length, and workload complexity is not reported, making it impossible to assess whether 1K-GPU simulation is practically feasible for realistic production traces. - The H2Q-BR scheduler comparison is thin: only vLLM default and skip-join MLFQ are compared; no other stateful scheduling baselines are evaluated. - Operator predictor training data size and train/test split methodology are not reported; it is unclear whether predictors generalize to operators and batch compositions outside the profiling distribution. - Results are limited to two MoE model families and one dense model; generalizability to other architectures (different expert counts, routing strategies, dense models at scale) is assumed. Bottom Line A practitioner designing disaggregated LLM serving infrastructure or trying to understand why their simulator's configuration recommendations fail on real hardware will get substantial, quantified value from this paper — but should treat the §6 use-case policy results as simulation-validated hypotheses requiring hardware confirmation before deployment.

SkillOpt: Executive Strategy for Self-Evolving Agent Skills

arxiv.org · 📄 Open PDF
**TL;DR** - **What it does:** This paper introduces SkillOpt, a text-space optimizer that trains a frozen agent's natural-language skill document using rollout batches, bounded add/delete/replace edits, a held-out validation gate, a rejected-edit buffer, and an epoch-wise slow/meta update — applying deep-learning training discipline to a portable text artifact. - **Why it's important:** Weight fine-tuning is unavailable for closed frontier models and expensive for open ones, yet hand-crafted or one-shot-generated skills are brittle; agents operating on procedural tasks (spreadsheet manipulation, embodied navigation, enterprise QA) need reliable iterative domain adaptation without touching model weights. - **How it does it:** A separate optimizer model reads scored trajectory batches and proposes structured edits; a textual learning-rate budget caps how many edits are applied per step; a held-out selection gate accepts only candidates that strictly improve validation score; rejected edits feed back as negative evidence in the same epoch. - **The good:** SkillOpt is best or tied-best across all 52 evaluated (model, benchmark, harness) cells, achieving average +23.5 absolute points over no-skill on GPT-5.5 direct chat and demonstrating Codex→Claude Code cross-harness transfer of +59.7 on SpreadsheetBench — the strongest transfer result in the paper and the clearest evidence that the artifact encodes transferable procedural knowledge. - **The missing:** No statistical significance tests or confidence intervals are reported anywhere; several "wins" in Table 1 are margins of ≤1 percentage point, and the 52/52 best-or-tied claim counts effective ties at that precision — the paper does not distinguish genuinely decisive wins from marginal ones. - **Read it or not:** Essential reading for practitioners building task-specific agents on closed frontier models who want a reproducible, no-weight-update adaptation procedure; less useful for those seeking theoretical guarantees or working outside the procedural-task regime where the method's gains are largest.
Full summary
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces SkillOpt, a text-space optimizer that trains a frozen agent's natural-language skill document using rollout batches, bounded add/delete/replace edits, a held-out validation gate, a rejected-edit buffer, and an epoch-wise slow/meta update — applying deep-learning training discipline to a portable text artifact. - Why it's important: Weight fine-tuning is unavailable for closed frontier models and expensive for open ones, yet hand-crafted or one-shot-generated skills are brittle; agents operating on procedural tasks (spreadsheet manipulation, embodied navigation, enterprise QA) need reliable iterative domain adaptation without touching model weights. - How it does it: A separate optimizer model reads scored trajectory batches and proposes structured edits; a textual learning-rate budget caps how many edits are applied per step; a held-out selection gate accepts only candidates that strictly improve validation score; rejected edits feed back as negative evidence in the same epoch. - The good: SkillOpt is best or tied-best across all 52 evaluated (model, benchmark, harness) cells, achieving average +23.5 absolute points over no-skill on GPT-5.5 direct chat and demonstrating Codex→Claude Code cross-harness transfer of +59.7 on SpreadsheetBench — the strongest transfer result in the paper and the clearest evidence that the artifact encodes transferable procedural knowledge. - The missing: No statistical significance tests or confidence intervals are reported anywhere; several "wins" in Table 1 are margins of ≤1 percentage point, and the 52/52 best-or-tied claim counts effective ties at that precision — the paper does not distinguish genuinely decisive wins from marginal ones. - Read it or not: Essential reading for practitioners building task-specific agents on closed frontier models who want a reproducible, no-weight-update adaptation procedure; less useful for those seeking theoretical guarantees or working outside the procedural-task regime where the method's gains are largest. Problem & Motivation: Frontier LLMs are increasingly deployed as agents inside tool-use harnesses (Codex, Claude Code) and multi-turn execution loops where success requires domain-specific procedures: correct spreadsheet manipulation idioms, structured answer formatting, search-frontier discipline in embodied environments, and precise evidence binding in document QA. None of these procedures are reliably captured by model weights alone, and fine-tuning closed models is infeasible. Hand-crafted and one-shot LLM-generated skills are brittle under harness changes and cannot correct themselves after observing failures. Prior skill-evolution systems (Trace2Skill, EvoSkill) mine trajectories or evolve skill folders but lack validation gates, bounded update controls, and negative-feedback memory, leaving the central question open: if skills are the domain-adaptation layer, how should they be optimized? Key Idea: The core insight is that a natural-language skill document can be treated as the external trainable state of a frozen agent, with the optimizer analogized to weight-space gradient descent: rollout batch size controls evidence noise, a bounded edit budget controls step size, a held-out selection gate plays the role of validation, rejected edits carry negative feedback analogous to gradient history, and an epoch-wise slow/meta update captures long-horizon regularities. The key move is that bounded, validation-gated updates keep adjacent skill versions close enough that the optimizer's history of accepted and rejected edits remains informative — unlike unbounded rewriting, which erases continuity. Methodology: The pipeline operates on three fixed splits (train, selection, test) and two model roles (frozen target, separate optimizer). Each optimization step proceeds as: (1) the target model executes a rollout batch of size B=40 with the current skill; (2) the optimizer model partitions trajectories into failures and successes, analyzes each in minibatches of size Bm=8, and proposes add/delete/replace patch edits; (3) edits from multiple minibatches are merged hierarchically (failure-first priority) and ranked by expected utility; (4) at most Lt edits (the textual learning rate, default 4, cosine-decayed) are applied to form a candidate skill; (5) the candidate is evaluated on the selection split — accepted only if strictly greater than the current selection score, otherwise rejected and logged to an epoch-local buffer; (6) at epoch boundaries, the slow/meta update compares the same tasks under adjacent epoch skills, writes longitudinal guidance into a markup-protected section of the skill document (also validated through the selection gate), and updates an optimizer-side meta skill that guides future reflection calls but is never shipped with the deployed artifact. The deployed output is a single best_skill.md of 300–2,000 tokens. Critical design decisions: (i) bounded edit budget vs. unconstrained rewriting — well-justified, ablations confirm +2.5–22.5 point degradation without it; (ii) strict greater-than acceptance criterion (ties rejected) — well-justified to prevent silent drift; (iii) separate slow-update protected region preventing fast edits from overwriting epoch-level lessons — well-justified, ablation shows −22.5 on SpreadsheetBench when removed. Key Results: On GPT-5.5 direct chat across six benchmarks, SkillOpt lifts SearchQA 77.7→87.3, SpreadsheetBench 41.8→80.7, OfficeQA 33.1→72.1, DocVQA 78.8→91.2, LiveMathematicianBench 37.6→66.9, ALFWorld 83.6→95.5, for a +23.5 average gain over no skill and +5.4 over an oracle that picks the strongest of six competing methods per cell. On GPT-5.5 inside the Codex harness the average gain over no skill is +24.8, and inside Claude Code +19.1. Across all seven direct-chat target models the average per-model improvement is approximately +17.6 points. The cross-harness transfer result is the most striking: a SpreadsheetBench skill trained inside Codex transfers to Claude Code with +59.7 absolute gain over the Claude Code no-skill baseline (22.1→81.8), slightly exceeding the in-domain Claude Code SkillOpt reference of 80.4. All cross-model and cross-benchmark transfer rows are positive. Training cost ranges from 20.8M tokens total (OfficeQA, 1 accepted edit, +39.0 points) to 213.8M tokens (SearchQA, 4 accepted edits, +9.6 points), with 1–4 median accepted edits across benchmarks — evidence that the validation gate filters aggressively. The strongest baselines (GEPA, EvoSkill) are strong competitors: GEPA reaches 73.6 on SpreadsheetBench for GPT-5.5 vs. SkillOpt's 80.7; EvoSkill reaches 75.0 on Codex SpreadsheetBench vs. SkillOpt's 85.0. All evaluated benchmarks are held-out test splits disjoint from the validation split used to gate edits. No error bars or significance tests are reported anywhere in the paper. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The genuine novelty is the training-style formulation of skill optimization: the bounded edit budget, strict held-out gate, rejected-edit buffer as negative feedback, and protected slow/meta update are a coherent system that has no prior counterpart for text-artifact training. The empirical finding that 1–4 accepted edits suffice for large gains, and that artifacts transfer across harnesses and model scales, is also new. What is not novel: trajectory-driven reflection (Reflexion, Self-Refine), prompt optimization (TextGrad, GEPA), and skill construction from experience (Trace2Skill, EvoSkill) are all prior work that SkillOpt explicitly positions against. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are present and informative: learning-rate form, rejected buffer, slow/meta update, training set size, batch size, and schedule are each swept. The ablations are run on three benchmarks (SearchQA, SpreadsheetBench, LiveMath) using GPT-5.5 as both target and optimizer, which is adequate breadth but not comprehensive. No error bars, confidence intervals, or significance tests are reported on any result. Several margins in Table 1 are ≤1 percentage point (e.g., SkillOpt 47.1 vs. LLM skill 46.5 on Qwen3.6-35B-A3B OfficeQA) making the "52/52 best or tied" claim partly a consequence of measurement precision rather than robust superiority. Code is publicly released at https://aka.ms/SkillOpt. The paper is a preprint (arXiv:2605.23904v2, May 2026), not peer reviewed. Several baselines (OfficeQA Pro, LiveMathematicianBench) are themselves 2026 preprints, making the benchmark landscape less settled. The use of GPT-5.5 as both target and optimizer in the primary results introduces a potential same-family confound, though Table 5 partially addresses this with target-matched optimizer experiments. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: Runs entirely via standard LLM API calls on commercially available models (GPT, Qwen); no specialized hardware required for either training or deployment. - Efficient: Training costs 20.8M–213.8M tokens per benchmark per model (a one-time expense); the deployed artifact is a static text file adding zero inference-time overhead, which is a material deployment advantage over methods that require optimizer calls at serve time. - Effective: Held-out test scores confirm that gains generalize beyond the validation split (Figure 3 shows validation checkpoint selection correlates with test performance); the largest absolute gains (+39 points on OfficeQA) appear on procedural benchmarks with strict format requirements where the method's learned rules are most directly applicable. - Scalable: The method is tested on benchmark-sized training pools (35–200 examples per epoch); behavior at 10×–100× scale is not evaluated, and the paper's own limitation note acknowledges that a single skill may be insufficient for highly heterogeneous domains requiring many disjoint procedures. The work delivers a software artifact (released code at https://aka.ms/SkillOpt) plus benchmark-specific best_skill.md artifacts described qualitatively in the paper. Strengths: - The deep-learning analogy is operationally precise rather than decorative: each component (edit budget, selection gate, rejected buffer, slow update) maps to a training-loop primitive with a clear justification and an ablation confirming its contribution. - Transfer experiments across three axes (model scale, harness, benchmark) are the paper's most original empirical contribution; the +59.7 Codex→Claude Code SpreadsheetBench transfer is striking and suggests the learned rules encode domain knowledge that is not harness-specific. - The "edit economy" finding — that +39 points on OfficeQA arises from a single accepted edit — is a surprising and credible result that directly supports the validation-gate design and distinguishes SkillOpt from prompt-inflation approaches. - The qualitative skill inspection (Figure 4, Section 4.5) is unusually informative: verbatim learned rules are procedural, generalizable, and interpretable, providing a sanity check that the optimizer is not simply overfitting to benchmark quirks. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly acknowledge in Appendix B: (1) dependence on automatic verifiers — the method is less applicable to open-ended tasks without reliable feedback; (2) non-trivial training cost despite being amortized at deployment; (3) single-skill design may be insufficient for highly heterogeneous domains; (4) optimized skills can encode training-distribution heuristics that require careful evaluation before transferring to substantially different settings. These are confronted directly, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The 52/52 best-or-tied claim does not distinguish decisive wins from marginal ones (≤1 percentage point differences appear in multiple cells) and no statistical tests are provided — a reviewer would reasonably push back on this as a headline claim. The evaluation is conducted entirely with GPT and Qwen model families in 2026; the method's behavior with open-weight models at the scale of Llama-3 70B or Mistral families is untested. The paper benchmarks against EvoSkill only in the harness settings, not in direct chat (presumably because EvoSkill is specifically a harness-side system), which means the strongest evolution baseline is missing from the largest portion of the main table. Training token costs for SearchQA (213.8M tokens total) and DocVQA (188.2M tokens total) are non-trivial at current API pricing and are not discussed in the context of cost-effectiveness relative to simply fine-tuning a smaller open model. The GPT-5.5 optimizer optimizing GPT-5.5 target creates a potential self-referential advantage that the Table 5 target-matched experiment only partially addresses — a cross-family optimizer experiment (e.g., Claude as optimizer, GPT as target) would be more diagnostic. Bottom Line: A practitioner deploying frontier agents on procedural tasks should read this paper: the method is immediately actionable, the code is public, and the empirical scope is unusually broad, with the cross-harness transfer result being the most practically significant finding — though the absence of error bars and the reliance on 2026-vintage closed models means independent replication will be needed before treating the 52/52 claim as settled.

Quest: Training Frontier Deep Research Agents with Fully Synthetic Tasks

arxiv.org · 📄 Open PDF
**TL;DR** - **What it does:** This paper introduces Quest, a family of open-weight deep research agents (2B–35B parameters) trained using a fully synthetic data pipeline based on hierarchical rubric trees, a structured context management mechanism, and a staged MT→SFT→RL training recipe. - **Why it's important:** Frontier deep research agents (OpenAI DeepResearch, Gemini) remain proprietary and opaque; existing open alternatives cover only narrow capability slices (fact-seeking or report synthesis, not both), leaving the field without a reproducible general-purpose baseline. - **How it does it:** The core mechanism is a rubric tree — a hierarchically decomposed, auto-verifiable specification of correctness — that enables generating 8K synthetic training tasks with fine-grained, automated rewards covering fact-seeking, citation grounding, and open-ended report synthesis jointly. - **The good:** Quest-35B achieves 80.8% on GAIA-Text (vs. GPT-5 at 76.4%), 48.2% on DeepResearch Bench (vs. OpenAI-DR at 47.0%), and 30.7% on Mind2Web 2 (vs. OpenAI-DR at 28.0%), narrowing or closing the open-weight/proprietary gap across three distinct capability dimensions simultaneously. - **The missing:** The training and inference pipelines depend on proprietary models (GPT-5-mini as context condenser and visit summarizer, Claude Sonnet 4.5 for data generation, Tongyi-DR for trajectory distillation), which means "fully open" overstates reproducibility; no contamination analysis is provided despite training data being crawled from live web content that may overlap with evaluated benchmarks. - **Read it or not:** Essential reading for researchers building or studying autonomous web agents — the rubric-tree synthesis and structured context-state mechanism are genuinely novel components and the full release (models, data, training scripts) provides a credible public reference for future work.
Full summary
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Quest, a family of open-weight deep research agents (2B–35B parameters) trained using a fully synthetic data pipeline based on hierarchical rubric trees, a structured context management mechanism, and a staged MT→SFT→RL training recipe. - Why it's important: Frontier deep research agents (OpenAI DeepResearch, Gemini) remain proprietary and opaque; existing open alternatives cover only narrow capability slices (fact-seeking or report synthesis, not both), leaving the field without a reproducible general-purpose baseline. - How it does it: The core mechanism is a rubric tree — a hierarchically decomposed, auto-verifiable specification of correctness — that enables generating 8K synthetic training tasks with fine-grained, automated rewards covering fact-seeking, citation grounding, and open-ended report synthesis jointly. - The good: Quest-35B achieves 80.8% on GAIA-Text (vs. GPT-5 at 76.4%), 48.2% on DeepResearch Bench (vs. OpenAI-DR at 47.0%), and 30.7% on Mind2Web 2 (vs. OpenAI-DR at 28.0%), narrowing or closing the open-weight/proprietary gap across three distinct capability dimensions simultaneously. - The missing: The training and inference pipelines depend on proprietary models (GPT-5-mini as context condenser and visit summarizer, Claude Sonnet 4.5 for data generation, Tongyi-DR for trajectory distillation), which means "fully open" overstates reproducibility; no contamination analysis is provided despite training data being crawled from live web content that may overlap with evaluated benchmarks. - Read it or not: Essential reading for researchers building or studying autonomous web agents — the rubric-tree synthesis and structured context-state mechanism are genuinely novel components and the full release (models, data, training scripts) provides a credible public reference for future work. Problem & Motivation: Web-scale information-seeking tasks — locating obscure facts, synthesizing multi-source reports, and grounding claims in verifiable citations — require autonomous agents that can plan, search, verify, and revise over long horizons. Existing proprietary systems (OpenAI DeepResearch, Gemini Deep Research) handle these tasks well but share neither models nor training recipes. Open-weight alternatives (Tongyi-DR, OpenResearcher, DR Tulu) each address a narrow slice: Tongyi-DR focuses on fact-seeking via single-answer supervision; OpenResearcher concentrates on citation grounding; DR Tulu on report synthesis. None provides a unified recipe covering all three capabilities, and none releases a fully reproducible training pipeline. The deeper problem is that synthesizing training data with verifiable rewards for open-ended research tasks (where there is no single correct answer) has resisted clean solutions — prior single-answer approaches provide binary rewards that are insufficient to guide multi-aspect report quality. Key Idea: The central insight is that any deep research task — whether it has a unique factual answer or requires a multi-page synthesized report — can be formalized as a rubric tree: a hierarchical decomposition of correctness criteria where leaf nodes are directly auto-verifiable (factual match, URL presence, source attribution) and internal nodes aggregate their children's scores. Once this representation exists, a single synthetic pipeline can generate diverse tasks, evaluate agent responses with fine-grained partial credit, and provide rubric-tree-shaped rewards for reinforcement learning — all without human annotation. Methodology: The pipeline has four components. (1) Data synthesis: trending Google keywords seed autonomous web exploration by Claude Sonnet 4.5 (Gsyn), which generates queries, derives verifiable constraints, and organizes them into rubric trees. Objective tasks receive Python evaluation scripts (generated by GPT-5) that programmatically verify each leaf node; open-ended tasks fix four top-level criteria (instruction following, comprehensiveness, readability, insight) and use pairwise scoring against a reference report generated by Gsyn, with the final score defined as J(rcand)/(J(rcand)+J(rref)) to avoid reference-bias inflation. Starting from ~20K raw tasks, strict filtering yields 8,228 high-quality SFT/RL instances. (2) Context management: a Context Condenser (GPT-5-mini) triggers once the active context exceeds a threshold, compressing history into a structured JSON "Context State" with three typed buckets — Trusted (verified facts with source URLs), Untrusted (contradicted claims with reason), and Uncertain (pending hypotheses with follow-up actions). Sessions restart from this state. (3) Training: mid-training (MT) on 300K context summarization examples and 1M+ HTML extraction examples adapts the base model to structured context formats. SFT trains on trajectories from Tongyi-DR distillation (session-level decomposition enables training on 200K-token trajectories on 16 H100 GPUs). RL uses GRPO with a composite reward R = 0.75·s_rubric + 0.25·min(s_fact, s_rubric), where s_fact measures the fraction of inline citations verified as supported by GPT-5-mini. (4) Infrastructure: dual-cache (exact + FAISS semantic fallback) for search/visit calls and a fully asynchronous RL pipeline built on VERL decouple rollout, evaluation, and training, accommodating rubric-tree evaluations that average 4 minutes and spike to 30 minutes. Critical design decisions: (a) session-level training instead of full-trajectory training is well-justified by GPU memory constraints (200K-token trajectories become tractable); (b) pairwise normalization for open-ended scoring over pointwise or ternary win/tie/lose is well-justified by empirical failure modes documented in Section 7.4; (c) excluding KL penalty from GRPO is stated but not ablated. Key Results: Quest-35B (Qwen3.5-35B-A3B base) on the full test sets: BrowseComp 64.6% (with discard-all, 45.5% otherwise) vs. OpenAI-DR 51.5%, Claude Opus 4.5 67.8%; BrowseComp-Plus 69.5% vs. OpenAI-DR not reported, Tongyi-DR 44.5%; Mind2Web 2 30.7% vs. OpenAI-DR 28.0%, Tongyi-DR 16.7%; WideSearch 60.6% vs. GPT-5 54.0%, Tongyi-DR 37.3%; HLE 37.2% vs. Claude Opus 4.5 43.2%, GPT-5 35.2%; GAIA 80.8% vs. GPT-5 76.4%; DeepResearch Bench 48.2% vs. OpenAI-DR 47.0%, Tongyi-DR 40.5%; LiveResearchBench 68.2% vs. GPT-5 73.1%, Tongyi-DR 56.3%. At equivalent 30B scale, Quest-30B beats Tongyi-DR and OpenResearcher on 4/8 benchmarks. Quest-2B-SFT scores 30.3% on HLE and 72.8% on GAIA, competitive with or exceeding OpenAI o3 (24.9% HLE, 70.5% GAIA). Ablations show SFT alone degrades open-ended tasks; MT recovers them; RL provides the largest boost for report synthesis but mildly hurts HLE and GAIA (alignment tax). Baselines are current and strong for open-weight comparisons; however, frontier proprietary agents (GPT-5, Claude Opus 4.5) have missing values across several benchmarks, making head-to-head comparisons incomplete. Ablations use sampled subsets (e.g., BrowseComp: 130 of 1,266), which may inflate variance. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD + NEW SYSTEM + NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK. The rubric-tree synthesis framework is genuinely novel as a unified mechanism for generating verifiably-graded training data across both objective and open-ended research tasks without human annotation. The structured three-bucket Context State as an explicit epistemic encoding of an agent's research state extends prior context management work (discard-all, keep-last-N) in a principled direction. The full open release (Quest-8K dataset, models 2B–35B, training scripts) is a practical contribution given that no prior open deep research agent released all three components together. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations cover the effect of each training stage (Vanilla → SFT → MT+SFT → MT+SFT+RL) and model size (2B, 4B, 9B, 35B). No error bars or statistical significance tests are reported for any benchmark result; most ablations use subsets rather than full test sets, and subset sizes (130 examples for BrowseComp) are small enough for noise to influence comparisons. Code, data (Quest-8K), and model weights (Quest-35B-RL at HuggingFace) are publicly released. A notable methodological concern: the training data is generated by web crawling and autonomous exploration, and the evaluated benchmarks (BrowseComp, HLE, GAIA) also draw from public web sources; no contamination analysis is presented. A second concern: BrowseComp results are reported under two different protocols (discard-all giving 64.6% vs. standard giving 45.5%); the headline Figure 1 number uses discard-all while the main table reports both, which requires careful reading to avoid conflation. The manual examination of evaluation scripts (50 samples, 4 annotators) found 6/50 rubric-related errors and 2/50 non-executable scripts — a non-trivial error rate that could introduce noisy rewards. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: SYSTEMS (a built open-weight model family evaluated by deployment on benchmarks). - Practical: Quest-2B and Quest-4B run on commodity GPUs; Quest-35B uses a MoE architecture (3B active parameters out of 35B total) so inference fits on hardware that can serve a standard 7B-dense model, making deployment practical. - Efficient: Training Quest-35B requires 32 H100 GPUs for ~5 days (MT) + ~1 day (SFT) + RL; inference relies on GPT-5-mini (via API) for context condensation and page visit summarization, introducing ongoing API cost and a proprietary external dependency that is not fully replaceable without retraining. - Effective: Evidence across 8 diverse benchmarks shows the method solves all three claimed capability dimensions; the clearest margin over open-weight baselines is on citation grounding (Mind2Web 2: 30.7% vs. 16.7% for Tongyi-DR) and report synthesis (DeepResearch Bench: 48.2% vs. 35.4% for OpenResearcher). - Scalable: The context management mechanism is designed to extrapolate to arbitrarily long horizons (demonstrated at >200 turns during inference despite training on ≤100-turn trajectories), but no benchmark in the evaluation explicitly stress-tests very-long-horizon tasks (hours-long research sessions); scaling to larger model sizes is explored only through SFT, not the full MT+SFT+RL recipe. The work delivers trained model weights (HuggingFace), training scripts (GitHub), and a synthetic dataset (Quest-8K) — a complete software artifact. Strengths: - The rubric-tree formalism is a clean unification of objective and open-ended task supervision; the hierarchical partial-credit score provides a richer RL signal than binary exact-match while remaining fully automated. - The structured Context State (trusted/untrusted/uncertain) goes beyond prior discard-all and keep-last-N strategies by preserving epistemic distinctions — a genuinely useful inductive bias for long-horizon research agents. - Section 7 documents four categories of unsuccessful attempts (search result prediction in MT, rubric-based error identification, DPO for report comparison, evaluation design failures) with hypotheses, which is unusually candid and provides actionable guidance for the field. - Full open release of models, data, and training code substantially lowers the barrier for follow-on research in a space dominated by proprietary systems. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors note that RL slightly sacrifices HLE and GAIA performance (alignment tax from specialization toward report synthesis behaviors). They acknowledge that small models (2B) underperform on open-ended tasks. The section on unsuccessful attempts acknowledges instability in DPO training for long-form comparisons and the failure of pointwise scoring. These are confronted directly, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - The training pipeline depends on GPT-5-mini (context condensation, visit summarization, fact-checking reward computation, citation insertion) and Claude Sonnet 4.5 (data generation) at multiple stages. Despite being called "fully open," these proprietary API dependencies mean training cannot be reproduced without incurring API costs to external vendors; the fallback models (DeepSeek V3.2) are noted only for RL-stage API rate-limit handling, not as full replacements. - No contamination analysis: the web-crawled training data is temporally overlapping with several benchmarks, and the paper does not analyze whether benchmark-relevant content appeared in training queries or visited URLs. - The BrowseComp headline score (64.6%) is produced under the discard-all context strategy (not Quest's Context Condenser), chosen to match the evaluation protocol used by proprietary competitors — but this means the primary advertised result for the most prominent benchmark does not use Quest's own claimed contribution of structured context management. - Ablation subsets are small (130 examples) and no variance estimates are reported, making it difficult to assess whether differences of 1–3 percentage points are meaningful. - The base model (Qwen3.5-35B-A3B) is a mixture-of-experts architecture; comparisons with dense 30B models conflate different computational classes without discussion. Bottom Line: Quest is the strongest open-weight deep research agent published to date and provides the field's most complete reproducible training recipe; a researcher building or benchmarking web agents should read this paper, with the caveat that "fully open" overstates the case given the proprietary API dependencies embedded throughout the training pipeline.

BLINKG: A Benchmark for LLM-Integrated Knowledge Graph Generation

arxiv.org · 📄 Open PDF
**TL;DR** - **What it does:** This paper introduces BLINKG, a benchmark that evaluates LLMs' ability to generate declarative semantic mappings from heterogeneous data sources (CSV, JSON, XML) to ontology terms during knowledge graph construction, decomposed into eight independently scorable atomic subtasks. - **Why it's important:** KG construction mapping phases routinely exceed six person-months in large real-world projects, yet no standardized evaluation framework exists for comparing LLM-based mapping approaches — each prior proposal uses different tasks, datasets, and metrics, making cross-study comparison impossible. - **How it does it:** BLINKG defines three scenarios of increasing schema-to-ontology lexical distance (Basic / Schema-Aligned using LinkedGTFS / Schema-Distant using eProcurement Ontology + CODICE XML), evaluates six LLMs using task-level precision/recall/F1 scored via a combined Levenshtein + embedding similarity metric calibrated against expert annotations, and releases all gold standards and evaluation code openly. - **The good:** The benchmark exposes a diagnostic capability cliff: LLMs achieve near-perfect entity class identification (similarity > 0.95) in simple scenarios but score F1 = 0.00 on join conditions and transformation functions in schema-distant Scenario 3 — across all six models and all prompting strategies — providing actionable granularity no end-to-end benchmark delivers. - **The missing:** The benchmark uses real-world ontologies (GTFS, ePO) that may overlap with LLM training corpora, and the similarity threshold is calibrated on the same expert annotations used for evaluation — creating a circularity that no held-out validation corrects. - **Read it or not:** Worth reading for researchers and practitioners working on LLM-assisted knowledge engineering or KG construction pipelines; less relevant outside the Semantic Web community, and the small gold standards (72 and 26 instances in the two realistic scenarios) limit statistical confidence in task-level findings.
Full summary
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces BLINKG, a benchmark that evaluates LLMs' ability to generate declarative semantic mappings from heterogeneous data sources (CSV, JSON, XML) to ontology terms during knowledge graph construction, decomposed into eight independently scorable atomic subtasks. - Why it's important: KG construction mapping phases routinely exceed six person-months in large real-world projects, yet no standardized evaluation framework exists for comparing LLM-based mapping approaches — each prior proposal uses different tasks, datasets, and metrics, making cross-study comparison impossible. - How it does it: BLINKG defines three scenarios of increasing schema-to-ontology lexical distance (Basic / Schema-Aligned using LinkedGTFS / Schema-Distant using eProcurement Ontology + CODICE XML), evaluates six LLMs using task-level precision/recall/F1 scored via a combined Levenshtein + embedding similarity metric calibrated against expert annotations, and releases all gold standards and evaluation code openly. - The good: The benchmark exposes a diagnostic capability cliff: LLMs achieve near-perfect entity class identification (similarity > 0.95) in simple scenarios but score F1 = 0.00 on join conditions and transformation functions in schema-distant Scenario 3 — across all six models and all prompting strategies — providing actionable granularity no end-to-end benchmark delivers. - The missing: The benchmark uses real-world ontologies (GTFS, ePO) that may overlap with LLM training corpora, and the similarity threshold is calibrated on the same expert annotations used for evaluation — creating a circularity that no held-out validation corrects. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers and practitioners working on LLM-assisted knowledge engineering or KG construction pipelines; less relevant outside the Semantic Web community, and the small gold standards (72 and 26 instances in the two realistic scenarios) limit statistical confidence in task-level findings. Problem & Motivation Knowledge engineers must map heterogeneous data source schemas — CSV columns, JSON paths, XML XPath expressions — to ontology terms in order to build knowledge graphs. In large-scale real-world projects this mapping phase alone regularly exceeds six person-months. Declarative mapping languages (RML, R2RML, SPARQL-Anything) reduce repetition but do not eliminate the semantic alignment work. LLMs have been proposed as accelerators, but existing proposals (Hofer et al. for RML from JSON, Schmidt et al. for YARRRML in manufacturing, Höltgen et al. for infrastructure RDF, Freund et al.'s ReMap) each define their own tasks, datasets, and success criteria, making cross-study comparison impossible.
