Prior benchmarks are inadequate: RODI targets RDB-only settings and evaluates mappings indirectly through SPARQL query accuracy; SemTab evaluates annotation of tabular data against pre-existing KGs, not ontology alignment for new graph construction. The result is a fragmented landscape with no agreed-upon tasks, gold standards, or metrics for LLM mapping evaluation. Key Idea The core insight is to decompose the monolithic KG mapping problem into eight independently scorable atomic subtasks — Ontology Class Identification, Subject URI Generation, Ontology Property Identification, Data Reference Identification, Related Entity Class and Joins, Language Annotation, Datatype Annotation, and Transformation Functions — and to evaluate LLMs on these subtasks across scenarios that vary in schema-to-ontology lexical distance, rather than measuring end-to-end pipeline quality or syntactic mapping language correctness. This decomposition exposes precisely where LLMs succeed or fail rather than collapsing all failure modes into a single end-to-end metric. Methodology BLINKG defines three scenarios. Scenario 1 (Basic) comprises eight atomic test cases over a minimal three-class toy ontology (Person, Sport, City) with artificial CSV/JSON/XML data derived from RML test cases; it tests each of the eight subtasks in isolation with 1–7 gold-standard instances per case. Scenario 2 (Schema-Aligned) uses the LinkedGTFS ontology (17 classes, 49 datatype properties, 10 object properties, 10 SKOS controlled vocabularies) with real Madrid Metro GTFS CSV feeds (13 files, 72 total gold instances); source and ontology have high lexical alignment since the ontology was derived bottom-up from the GTFS specification. Scenario 3 (Schema-Distant) uses a subset of the eProcurement Ontology (11 classes from the pan-European epo-core module) with CODICE XML procurement entries from Spain (26 gold instances); CODICE and ePO were developed independently, introducing significant structural and lexical divergence requiring deep domain expertise to bridge. Each LLM is prompted with a standardized natural-language template requesting a structured table output listing data references, ontology properties, entity classes, subject templates, joins, datatypes, language tags, and transformation functions. Temperature is fixed at 0.3 based on preliminary runs. Each prompt runs three times; results are macro-averaged. To fit context windows, Scenario 2 is split into 6 sub-scenarios and Scenario 3 into 2. Three evaluation modes are reported: expert (manual annotation), raw (automatic on raw output), and post-processed (automatic after heuristic cleaning — whitespace removal, discarding incoherent fragments, dropping misaligned columns). Correctness is binary: an annotation is correct if max(Levenshtein similarity, raw SBERT cosine, verbalized-ontology SBERT cosine) ≥ τ = 0.8, where τ was chosen by minimizing MAE against expert annotations. Zero-shot, one-shot, and few-shot prompting strategies are compared on Scenario 3 for DeepSeek-R1. Six LLMs are evaluated: DeepSeek-R1, Gemini 2.5 Pro, GPT-4o, OpenAI o3, LLaMA 3.3 70B Instruct, and Mixtral 8x22B Instruct. Three critical design decisions: (1) Tabular output rather than raw RML/R2RML — well justified, as LLMs are known to produce syntactically invalid mapping language files. (2) Threshold τ = 0.8 calibrated against expert annotations — problematic because it is selected on the same data it evaluates (no held-out calibration set). (3) Sub-scenario splitting for context-window limitations — pragmatic but introduces potential coherence loss not ablated. Key Results Scenario 1: Entity class identification similarity ≥ 0.95 for most models; several reach 1.00. Ontology property identification reliably high. Join condition is the hardest task, with similarity ranging 0.37 (LLaMA) to 0.69 (Gemini 2.5 Pro). Language annotation shows high variance: DeepSeek/Gemini > 0.83; Mixtral/LLaMA < 0.53. MAE between expert and post-processed F1 ranges 0.106 (DeepSeek) to 0.236 (Mixtral), indicating the automatic metric is a reasonable but imperfect proxy in simple settings. Scenario 2 (72 gold instances): Entity class and data reference post-processed similarity > 0.90 for top models (GPT-4o data reference: 0.91; DeepSeek entity class: 0.95). Ontology property F1 approaches 1.00 (post-processed) for DeepSeek and Gemini. Join condition: no model exceeds 0.85 F1 (post-processed) and most fall below 0.65. Function Name: all models below 0.20 similarity raw; post-processed jumps to 0.20 (a flat heuristic artifact). Function Output: below 0.41 post-processed for most. MAE between expert and post-processed F1 ranges 0.13 (GPT-4o) to 0.22 (Gemini, Mixtral). Scenario 3 (26 gold instances): No task exceeds mean similarity 0.53 post-processed for any model. Entity class best: DeepSeek 0.53, GPT-4o 0.42. Join condition F1 = 0.00 for all models in all evaluation modes. Function Output F1 = 0.00 for all models except o3 (0.25 post-processed). Subject Generation F1 = 0.00 for all models. Prompting strategy experiment (DeepSeek): few-shot raises data reference F1 from 0.38 to 0.86 and subject generation from 0.00 to 0.71, but join and function tasks remain at 0.00 regardless of strategy. MAE between expert and post-processed ranges 0.32 (o3) to 0.42 (DeepSeek), indicating the automatic metric substantially underestimates LLM performance in complex schema-distant settings. No non-LLM baselines are included. Baselines are limited to cross-model comparison within the six LLMs. Novelty Classification NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK, NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The primary contribution is a structured, openly released benchmark with gold standards across three scenarios. The finding that join conditions and transformation functions are a universal failure mode across six diverse LLMs — persistent even under few-shot prompting in schema-distant settings — is a genuine empirical finding. The evaluation methodology (task decomposition + multi-similarity scoring) is an incremental methodological contribution. The paper does not introduce a new method, algorithm, or system for KG construction itself. Rigor & Reproducibility All resources (ontologies, datasets, gold standards, evaluation library) are released at https://github.com/citiususc/blinkg under Apache 2.0. Only 3 prompt runs per scenario — standard deviations across runs are not reported, and significance tests are absent. No inter-annotator agreement is reported for the expert evaluation used as the gold calibration reference. The similarity threshold τ = 0.8 is selected by minimizing MAE against the same expert annotations it evaluates — this is a Goodhart's Law concern: the metric is tuned to its own benchmark. Context-window splitting into sub-scenarios is not evaluated as a confound. The benchmark uses real-world ontologies (GTFS, ePO) publicly available on the Web and potentially in LLM training corpora; the authors explicitly acknowledge data leakage as a limitation and recommend synthetic ontologies for future versions but do not control for it here. The paper is peer-reviewed, accepted to Transactions on Graph Data and Knowledge (received July 2025, accepted March 2026). Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL (evaluation of LLM systems against gold standards; the paper reports no deployment of a new system, only evaluation of existing LLMs). - Practical: The benchmark runs against API-accessible commercial and open-source LLMs with no specialized hardware beyond API access; any practitioner can reproduce the evaluation. - Efficient: Scenario 2 requires splitting into 6 sub-scenarios to fit LLM context windows, and running 6 models × 3 prompt repetitions over 72+26 instances is modest in compute but non-trivial in API cost; the evaluation library lowers the barrier for reuse. - Effective: The benchmark successfully differentiates model capabilities across difficulty levels and identifies specific failure subtasks (join conditions, transformation functions) that end-to-end evaluation would obscure; expert-vs-automatic MAE confirms the metric is a reasonable proxy in simpler scenarios. - Scalable: Gold standards are small (72 and 26 instances in the two realistic scenarios), limiting statistical confidence; the framework architecture is extensible through community contributions, but current coverage is insufficient to claim generalization to the full diversity of real-world KG mapping challenges. The work delivers a software artifact (benchmark suite with ontologies, datasets, gold standards, evaluation library) at the stated GitHub URL. Strengths - Task decomposition is the primary contribution: separating KG mapping into 8 independently scorable subtasks enables precise failure-mode diagnosis that RODI's SPARQL-based or SemTab's annotation-level evaluation cannot provide. - The three-scenario difficulty ladder with real-world ontologies (LinkedGTFS, ePO) and data sources (Madrid Metro GTFS, Spanish procurement CODICE XML) provides external validity beyond toy examples and covers both CSV and deeply nested XML. - The three-mode evaluation design (expert, raw, post-processed) reveals that raw automatic scoring systematically underestimates LLM performance — a finding with practical implications for how future LLM evaluations in this domain should be designed. - The prompting strategy ablation on Scenario 3 demonstrates concretely that few-shot prompting helps local selection tasks but fails entirely for compositional tasks (joins, transformations), providing actionable guidance for future research. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly state: (1) data leakage risk from publicly available GTFS and ePO ontologies that may overlap with LLM training corpora, with a commitment to synthetic ontologies in future versions; (2) reliance on manual inspection or simplified metrics for semantic equivalence; (3) exclusion of advanced mapping constructs (RML-CC list generation, RML-Star, RML-IO) deferred to future versions; (4) fragility of proprietary LLM APIs — OpenAI o1 and Gemini 2.0 Pro were removed or restricted mid-evaluation, requiring repetition. The authors confront these limitations directly, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - No non-LLM baselines: existing automated mapping tools (MIRROR, AutoMap4OBDA, BootOX, D2RQ) are not compared, leaving unanswered whether LLMs even outperform classical heuristic-based tools on Scenario 1. - Threshold circularity: τ = 0.8 is calibrated by minimizing MAE against the same expert annotations used for evaluation — this overfits the metric to the benchmark and should be validated on held-out data or via cross-validation. - Scenario 3 gold standard instability: with only 26 instances spread across 9 task types, individual task scores (e.g., Join Condition: 16 instances) are computed over counts too small for stable estimates; confidence intervals are absent. - Context-window splitting as an uncontrolled confound: decomposing Scenario 2 into 6 sub-scenarios removes global ontology context an LLM would otherwise use; the effect of this decomposition on results is never ablated. - Single temperature: only temperature 0.3 is reported in final experiments despite the claim that preliminary runs were conducted; no sensitivity analysis is included. - No inter-annotator agreement: the expert evaluation serves as the calibration anchor for τ, but no agreement metric (Cohen's κ, Fleiss's κ) is reported, so the reliability of the gold calibration reference is unknown. Bottom Line BLINKG is a useful, openly released benchmark that fills a genuine gap by providing the first task-decomposed, format-agnostic, multi-scenario evaluation framework for LLM-assisted KG mapping; knowledge graph construction researchers should read it for its diagnostic task decomposition and the finding that compositional mapping tasks (joins, transformation functions) are a universal current LLM failure mode, while keeping in mind that absent non-LLM baselines, small gold standards, and an uncorrected threshold circularity make the quantitative results preliminary rather than definitive.

Skill Weaving: Efficient LLM Improvement via Modular Skillpacks

arxiv.org · 📄 Open PDF
**TL;DR** - **What it does:** This paper introduces SkillWeave, a modular framework that decomposes a general-purpose LLM into domain-specific delta modules ("skillpacks") trained via full-parameter DPO and compressed via SkillZip — a joint weight-and-activation quantization scheme — enabling multi-domain LLM serving under fixed memory and latency budgets. - **Why it's important:** Practitioners serving LLMs across heterogeneous domains (math, coding, dialogue, agentic tasks) must choose between maintaining multiple full-model copies (prohibitive memory/swap latency) or scaling to large monolithic models (high cost); existing PEFT and delta-compression approaches either underperform or leave inference latency unsolved. - **How it does it:** Each domain receives a full-parameter DPO fine-tune; shared cross-task knowledge is then extracted via model merging and subtracted from each delta; the residual domain deltas are factorized via SVD with double smoothing (channel-wise outlier redistribution + rank-wise orthogonal rotation) and quantized to INT8/INT4 for hardware-native GEMM execution without runtime dequantization. - **The good:** A 10B SkillWeave model (8B backbone + 5× ~0.5B skillpacks) outperforms Gemma2-27B on MATH (62.5 vs 54.4) and all same-size baselines across most of 8 benchmarks, while delivering 4.2× inference speedup over a 32B monolith in agent deployment. - **The missing:** The headline comparison table (Table 1) uses oracle domain labels — a condition unavailable in real deployment; the learned router ablation is presented separately and shows negligible degradation, but under mixed-domain or adversarial inputs this gap is not stress-tested. - **Read it or not:** Worth reading for ML engineers targeting multi-domain LLM deployment under GPU memory constraints; the SkillZip quantization design and the full-tune-then-compress principle are the most practically transferable contributions.
Full summary
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces SkillWeave, a modular framework that decomposes a general-purpose LLM into domain-specific delta modules ("skillpacks") trained via full-parameter DPO and compressed via SkillZip — a joint weight-and-activation quantization scheme — enabling multi-domain LLM serving under fixed memory and latency budgets. - Why it's important: Practitioners serving LLMs across heterogeneous domains (math, coding, dialogue, agentic tasks) must choose between maintaining multiple full-model copies (prohibitive memory/swap latency) or scaling to large monolithic models (high cost); existing PEFT and delta-compression approaches either underperform or leave inference latency unsolved. - How it does it: Each domain receives a full-parameter DPO fine-tune; shared cross-task knowledge is then extracted via model merging and subtracted from each delta; the residual domain deltas are factorized via SVD with double smoothing (channel-wise outlier redistribution + rank-wise orthogonal rotation) and quantized to INT8/INT4 for hardware-native GEMM execution without runtime dequantization. - The good: A 10B SkillWeave model (8B backbone + 5× ~0.5B skillpacks) outperforms Gemma2-27B on MATH (62.5 vs 54.4) and all same-size baselines across most of 8 benchmarks, while delivering 4.2× inference speedup over a 32B monolith in agent deployment. - The missing: The headline comparison table (Table 1) uses oracle domain labels — a condition unavailable in real deployment; the learned router ablation is presented separately and shows negligible degradation, but under mixed-domain or adversarial inputs this gap is not stress-tested. - Read it or not: Worth reading for ML engineers targeting multi-domain LLM deployment under GPU memory constraints; the SkillZip quantization design and the full-tune-then-compress principle are the most practically transferable contributions. Problem & Motivation Deploying LLMs across diverse domains — math reasoning, code generation, dialogue, agentic tool use — forces one of two costly strategies: maintain multiple full-parameter specialized models (multiplicative memory and model-swap latency costs) or rely on a single large monolithic model (high inference cost per query). PEFT methods like LoRA reduce training cost but systematically underperform full fine-tuning on domain-specific tasks. Existing delta-compression methods (BitDelta, DeltaCome, ASVD) compress the stored weight delta but require runtime dequantization back to FP16, leaving inference latency unchanged. Task interference and catastrophic forgetting further limit joint fine-tuning approaches. The paper targets the specific gap of achieving full-fine-tune quality with near-backbone inference cost across multiple concurrent domains. Key Idea The core insight is to decouple the quality phase from the efficiency phase: train each domain at full parameter capacity (capturing fine-grained domain improvements that LoRA cannot), then compress the resulting delta — not the original weights — using joint weight-and-activation quantization that enables direct INT8/INT4 GEMM execution. The enabling trick for the compression step is a two-stage outlier smoothing: first redistribute channel-wise activation outliers into the weight matrix (SmoothQuant-style), then apply an orthogonal rotation to the SVD factors to equalize rank-dimension energy, making both the activation and the low-rank weights amenable to uniform quantization without dequantization overhead. Methodology SkillWeave runs a structured three-stage pipeline. (1) Skillpack Building: for each of K domains, the base model Mθ₀ is prompted to self-generate responses on a partitioned seed dataset; a domain-specific rule-based filter (regex answer matching for math, sandbox execution for code, structural heuristics and reward-model scoring for dialogue) splits outputs into helpful/harmful preference pairs; online LN-DPO (length-normalized, no explicit reference log-ratio) fine-tunes the full model to produce Mₖ = M₀ + Δₖ. (2) Skillpack Compression (SkillZip): a shared component Δ_shared = Merge([Δ₁,...,Δₖ]) is absorbed into the backbone and subtracted from each delta, making residuals sparser and more domain-specific. Each residual delta is factorized via truncated SVD (W ≈ AB), then double-smoothed: channel-wise scaling of X redistributes activation outliers; orthogonal rotation Q disperses rank-concentrated singular energy. The objective minimizes ||XW - Dequant(X̂ÂB̂)|| with per-token/per-tensor quantization on X̂, per-tensor on Â, per-channel on B̂ — chosen specifically for hardware GEMM compatibility. Final GPTQ step reduces weights to INT4. (3) Skillpack Integration: at inference, the FP16 backbone runs constantly; a domain router (Qwen2.5-0.5B with a linear classification head, for general tasks) selects one skillpack; output = XᵢW + X̂ᵢÂᵢB̂ᵢ using S-LoRA-style batching. Critical design decisions: (a) full fine-tuning before compression vs. PEFT training — justified by ablation showing ~30% average advantage over PEFT across domains; (b) joint activation+weight quantization vs. weight-only — eliminates runtime dequantization, enabling 2.1× kernel decode speedup; (c) merge-before-compress — empirically reduces required compression ratio per delta. Key Results General capability (Table 1, LLaMA-3.1-8B base, 10B total): SkillWeave scores GSM8K 91.0 (+0.2 over best same-size baseline EMR-Merging at 16.7B), MATH 62.5 (+2.1 over Twin-Merging r1024 at 21B), HumanEval 75.0, MBPP 77.8, AlpacaEval2 52.8, IFEval 79.1, BBH 76.2, ARC-C 88.6 — ranking first in 4 of 8 benchmarks. Notably surpasses Gemma2-27B on MATH (62.5 vs 54.4) and GSM8K (91.0 vs 90.4). However, SkillWeave scores 52.8 on AlpacaEval2 versus FuseChat3.0's 64.2 — an 11.4-point deficit on dialogue that is attributed to FuseChat's stronger teacher models but not directly ablated. Agent deployment (Figure 5, Qwen2.5-7B backbone): SkillWeave achieves 4.2× throughput speedup over 32B monolith and 5.5× over 5×7B ensemble, while remaining within 3% and 5% of their respective task performance. SkillZip compression (Table 2): X8A8B8 configuration achieves average score 59.8 vs DeltaCome 59.7 and BitDelta 57.7, with 1.38× end-to-end latency improvement over DeltaCome. Kernel-level decode latency (batch=1): X8A8A8 achieves 0.0558ms vs FP16 0.1167ms (2.1× speedup). Routing: learned Qwen2.5-0.5B router achieves >0.999 F1 across all four domains (Table 3); oracle vs. learned routing performance gap is negligible (Table 4). Results at 1B scale (Table 5) show SkillWeave outperforms Twin-Merging and Self-Rewarding but underperforms FuseChat3.0 on most dimensions. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The full-tune-then-compress design principle as applied to per-domain LLM skillpacks is a genuinely novel framing that resolves the PEFT-vs-full-FT quality tradeoff. SkillZip's joint weight+activation quantization applied specifically to SVD-factorized task deltas, with the rank-wise rotation smoothing, extends SmoothQuant in a non-obvious direction. The surrounding pipeline (DPO self-improvement, model merging, SVD compression) assembles known techniques, but their integration and the hardware-aware inference design constitute a substantive contribution beyond a standard combination applied to a new setting. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present for both the SkillZip components (Table 2, testing merge/smooth/rotate combinations) and for the self-improvement algorithm choice (Table 1 ablation rows). No error bars, confidence intervals, or multiple random seeds are reported anywhere in the paper. The headline Table 1 uses oracle domain labels — the paper discloses this in Section 3.3 and Appendix B.3, and provides a separate oracle-vs-learned router ablation (Table 4) showing negligible gap (MATH: 62.5 vs 62.3; all other benchmarks identical or within 0.2), but the main table numbers are technically achieved under a condition unavailable in unstructured deployment. Code and training scripts are released at an anonymous repository (https://anonymous.4open.science/r/anonymous-repo-BFE7) — available but not yet under a named project or permanent DOI. The paper is a preprint (arXiv:2605.22205v1); results are not peer-reviewed. Training data for SkillWeave overlaps significantly with FUSECHAT-MIXTURE (disclosed in Appendix E.2), which complicates the comparison with FuseChat baselines since FuseChat additionally uses stronger external teacher models. AlpacaEval2 raw win rate (not length-controlled) is reported in the main table — a metric known to favor verbosity; LC scores are relegated to the appendix. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL + SYSTEMS — the paper builds and evaluates a deployed inference system. - Practical: All experiments run on single NVIDIA A100-80G GPUs; the 9.5B total parameter footprint (7B backbone + 5×0.5B skillpacks resident in memory) fits in a single commercially available high-memory GPU. - Efficient: SkillZip X8A8B8 achieves 2.1× kernel decode speedup over FP16 and 1.38× end-to-end speedup over DeltaCome; the routing model (Qwen2.5-0.5B) adds marginal overhead (one forward pass per request, not per token), with the dominant slowdown being GPU memory contention rather than computation. - Effective: Within 3% of a 5×7B task-specialized ensemble on AgentBench and within 5% of a 32B monolith; outperforms all same-size baselines on math and coding in general evaluation; underperforms FuseChat3.0 by 11.4 points on AlpacaEval2 dialogue, with the gap attributed to teacher strength rather than method limitation — this attribution is asserted but not controlled for. - Scalable: Tested at two backbone scales (1B and 8B) with up to 5 concurrent skillpacks; the paper claims the approach is "scalable" to additional domains but provides no experiment beyond K=5 skills, and does not analyze how compression quality degrades as K grows or as domain boundaries become less distinct. The system delivers a software artifact (code + training scripts at anonymous repo); trained model weights are not explicitly released. Strengths - The full-tune-then-compress paradigm is both conceptually clear and empirically well-supported: ablations in Table 2 and the single-skill rows in Table 1 confirm that full-parameter training before compression consistently outperforms LoRA training (+30% over ASVD, consistent advantage over PEFT across 8 benchmarks). - SkillZip's joint activation quantization is the technically strongest contribution: by eliminating runtime dequantization through hardware-compatible INT8/INT4 GEMM, it achieves speedups that prior weight-only delta-compression methods (BitDelta, DeltaCome) structurally cannot, while matching or exceeding their accuracy. - The rank-wise orthogonal rotation to handle SVD-induced outliers is a non-obvious and technically grounded solution; it addresses a real failure mode of applying per-tensor/per-channel quantization to SVD factors where energy concentrates in leading rank dimensions. - The agent deployment evaluation is more realistic than standard benchmark comparison: testing against both multi-model ensemble and large monolith under actual throughput and latency measurement on a real serving stack (S-LoRA + Gamma arrival process) gives deployment practitioners actionable numbers. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly identify two limitations. First, evaluation covers benchmarks with clear domain boundaries; effectiveness in open-ended or weakly defined task settings (emergent user behaviors, mixed-domain agentic workflows) requires further investigation. Second, rule-based verification works well for math and code (exact answer or execution correctness) but fails for creative writing or abstract conversation where no correctness criterion exists, limiting self-improvement to structured domains. The authors frame both as future work directions rather than minimizing them — a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The oracle routing assumption inflates headline numbers: while Table 4 shows negligible oracle-vs-learned router degradation on single-domain benchmarks, the claim does not hold for mixed-domain or adversarial prompts (the case study in Figure 4 actually shows that misrouting can sometimes help, but this is cherry-picked rather than systematically evaluated). - No ablation on K (number of domains): it is unknown whether adding a 6th or 10th skillpack degrades the shared backbone, the merge quality, or the compression fidelity of existing skillpacks. - The 11.4-point AlpacaEval2 gap versus FuseChat3.0 (52.8 vs 64.2) is not adequately explained or ablated; the authors attribute it to teacher strength but offer no controlled experiment (e.g., SkillWeave with the same FuseChat teacher data) to substantiate this. - The 1B backbone results (Table 5) show SkillWeave underperforms FuseChat3.0 on most dimensions; the comparison across model scales uses different baseline classes, making it difficult to isolate the method contribution from scale effects. - Latency experiments are single-GPU A100-80G only; multi-GPU tensor-parallel or pipeline-parallel deployments common in production are not addressed, and the eviction-and-reloading policy used as the 5×7B baseline is designed to make that baseline look worse. - The paper does not analyze what happens to previously trained skillpacks when a new domain is added — whether the backbone merge step must be redone, and whether that invalidates existing compressed skillpacks. Bottom Line A practitioner targeting memory-constrained multi-domain LLM deployment will find the full-tune-then-compress principle and SkillZip's hardware-aware INT8/INT4 delta execution directly actionable, but should treat the headline benchmark numbers with caution given the oracle-routing assumption and the unresolved dialogue performance gap.

LoMo: Local Modality Substitution for Deeper Vision-Language Fusion

arxiv.org · 📄 Open PDF
**TL;DR** - **What it does:** This paper introduces LoMo, a data curation method that converts text-only SFT samples into text–image–text interleaved sequences by rendering a middle span of the text prompt as an image, injecting implicit cross-modal alignment supervision into standard VLM fine-tuning without any architectural change. - **Why it's important:** Current VLMs drop 8.70–11.68 accuracy points when identical semantic content is delivered as a rendered image rather than text tokens, a fragility that undermines real-world robustness in document understanding, mixed-modality interfaces, and any deployment where linguistic content arrives through visual channels. - **How it does it:** By forcing the model to predict the same answer from a sequence where the semantically critical middle segment is a rendered image bracketed by text tokens, the standard NLL loss implicitly minimizes a KL divergence between the model's predictive distributions under textual and visual carriers — alignment becomes a task-level requirement with no extra objective. - **The good:** Under a rendered evaluation protocol (entire text question delivered as a pixel image), LoMo raises LLaVA-OV1.5-8B average accuracy across 13 benchmarks by +18.86 points (15.24→34.10), with DocVQA gaining +43.51, and a controlled ablation confirms that the gains are not attributable to simply having more multimodal training samples. - **The missing:** Evaluation is restricted to 8B–9B models trained for one epoch; whether gains persist or grow at 70B+ scale or after RL-based post-training, and whether the rendered test distribution matching the training renderer inflates rendered evaluation numbers, are not examined. - **Read it or not:** VLM practitioners who fine-tune on SFT corpora should read this — the method is plug-in compatible, introduces zero inference cost, and is supported by thorough ablations; the core idea is simple enough to implement in a weekend.
Full summary
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces LoMo, a data curation method that converts text-only SFT samples into text–image–text interleaved sequences by rendering a middle span of the text prompt as an image, injecting implicit cross-modal alignment supervision into standard VLM fine-tuning without any architectural change. - Why it's important: Current VLMs drop 8.70–11.68 accuracy points when identical semantic content is delivered as a rendered image rather than text tokens, a fragility that undermines real-world robustness in document understanding, mixed-modality interfaces, and any deployment where linguistic content arrives through visual channels. - How it does it: By forcing the model to predict the same answer from a sequence where the semantically critical middle segment is a rendered image bracketed by text tokens, the standard NLL loss implicitly minimizes a KL divergence between the model's predictive distributions under textual and visual carriers — alignment becomes a task-level requirement with no extra objective. - The good: Under a rendered evaluation protocol (entire text question delivered as a pixel image), LoMo raises LLaVA-OV1.5-8B average accuracy across 13 benchmarks by +18.86 points (15.24→34.10), with DocVQA gaining +43.51, and a controlled ablation confirms that the gains are not attributable to simply having more multimodal training samples. - The missing: Evaluation is restricted to 8B–9B models trained for one epoch; whether gains persist or grow at 70B+ scale or after RL-based post-training, and whether the rendered test distribution matching the training renderer inflates rendered evaluation numbers, are not examined. - Read it or not: VLM practitioners who fine-tune on SFT corpora should read this — the method is plug-in compatible, introduces zero inference cost, and is supported by thorough ablations; the core idea is simple enough to implement in a weekend. Problem & Motivation Vision-language models (VLMs) treat text and image as modality-specific inputs — text as the linguistic query, images as the visual scene — because this is how virtually all standard training corpora (image captioning, VQA, OCR, web-interleaved data) are structured. This asymmetry means the model never needs to equate semantically identical content delivered as text tokens versus as pixel images. The practical symptom is severe: rendering the text question as an image while keeping all other inputs identical causes LLaVA-OV1.5-8B to drop 11.68 points, InternVL3.5-8B to drop 8.70 points, GLM-4.1V-9B to drop 10.09 points, and Qwen3.5-9B to drop 9.25 points across standard multimodal benchmarks. The authors measure this more precisely by extracting hidden states of text tokens and their rendered-image counterparts and computing cosine distances; accuracy drop correlates strictly monotonically with this cross-carrier distance, growing from 7.75% in the closest distance bin to 21.23% in the farthest, establishing a mechanistic link between representational distance and performance degradation. Key Idea The core insight is that the standard NLL fine-tuning objective, applied to an instance where a text span has been replaced by its rendered-image counterpart, implicitly decomposes into the standard SFT loss plus a KL divergence term between the model's predictive distribution conditioned on the original text and its distribution conditioned on the image-substituted version. No new loss function, no auxiliary head, and no contrastive pairs are needed; by merely reformatting the input while keeping the supervision target unchanged, cross-carrier representational alignment becomes a first-class task-level requirement. Methodology LoMo transforms a text-only instance (x, a) through three successive stages. (1) Structure-Aware Span Localization: For short inputs (≤3 sentences), the entire text is the target span. For longer inputs, a formula-aware chunker treats LaTeX expressions and common math commands as atomic units and extracts the middle one-third by character length, ensuring equation boundaries are never split. This produces (x_pre, x_mid, x_suf). (2) Visual Rendering: x_mid is routed to a LaTeX renderer if it contains mathematical expressions, otherwise to a standard text renderer (font size 20, Latin Modern Math at size 26); LaTeX failures automatically fall back to the text renderer. Margin trimming removes large whitespace. (3) Perceptual Distortion: One operation is sampled uniformly from {clean, rotate, blur, shadow/stain, wave} and applied to the rendered image, simulating real-world document capture noise. The final instance (x_pre, I', x_suf) replaces x while preserving the original answer a. Three critical design decisions are well-justified: the middle-span placement (ablation in Table 4 confirms +2.68 vs. +1.56 for prefix placement), the 50% rewrite ratio (Table 3 shows gains peak at 50% and fall to +1.80 at 100%), and formula-aware chunking (full-text rendering without span localization yields only +1.19 average gain, Table 2). Training uses 4M examples total (2M multimodal + 2M text-only, 50% of text-only rendered), LLaMA-Factory, FlashAttention 2, bf16, DeepSpeed ZeRO Stage 1, 8×H200 GPUs, AdamW with cosine schedule (4e-5 to 1e-6), 1 epoch, vision tower frozen. Key Results Under Standard Evaluation (text question delivered as text): LoMo improves LLaVA-OV1.5-8B by +2.68 average (40.88→43.56) and Qwen3.5-9B by +2.82 average (54.43→57.25) across 13 benchmarks. Largest single-task gains on LLaVA: CountBench +8.15 (42.97→51.12), V* +3.99 (47.71→51.70), MM-IFEval +3.21 (58.40→61.61). Largest on Qwen: MMMU-Pro +6.35 (39.83→46.18), MM-IFEval +5.49 (51.74→57.23), DocVQA +6.10 (85.89→91.99). Improvements appear in 23 of 26 comparisons; three marginal regressions (MMMU −0.56 on LLaVA; MathVista −0.60, CC-OCR −0.30 on Qwen). Under Rendered Evaluation (text question delivered as pixel image): gains expand to +18.86 on LLaVA (15.24→34.10) and +11.92 on Qwen (43.26→55.18), roughly 7× and 4× the standard gains. DocVQA: +43.51/+44.49; CC-OCR: +25.40/+16.54; CountBench: +34.82/+12.32. On Qwen, the Standard→Rendered accuracy gap compresses from 11.17 points (Standard SFT) to 2.07 points (LoMo). Alignment metrics at 4M scale: LoMo reduces MIR by 0.122 below Standard SFT and reduces pairwise cross-modal distance from 0.52 to 0.49, while Standard SFT increases it from 0.52 to 0.57. Pure-text benchmarks (MMLU-Pro, GSM8K, HumanEval, LiveCodeBench, IFEval): LoMo matches or slightly improves Standard SFT (+0.28 and +0.58 average), confirming no pure-text regression. The baseline — Standard SFT on the identical 4M-instance corpus — is well-matched; no comparison against other cross-modal alignment methods (VCD, HA-DPO) is provided. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The use of in-context rendered-text insertion as an implicit cross-carrier alignment mechanism during standard SFT is genuinely novel; prior text-as-pixels work (Pix2Struct, Glyph, DeepSeek-OCR) treats pixel rendering as an efficiency substitute for tokens, not as a complementary alignment signal within a single training instance. The KL-divergence interpretation of the implicit loss (Eq. 5–6) is a clean and non-obvious theoretical contribution. The monotonic correlation between pairwise cross-carrier representational distance and accuracy drop is a new empirical finding, though the general modality gap concept originates in CLIP-era work. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present and well-targeted: component ablation ruling out full-text rendering and isolating the contribution of perceptual distortion (Table 2); rewrite ratio sweep over 0%, 25%, 50%, 75%, 100% (Table 3); rendering position comparison of prefix/middle/suffix/multi-span (Table 4); controlled image-to-text ratio comparison at matched 1:1 ratio (Table 5, +2.45 gain confirms the gains are not merely from more multimodal samples); data scale ablation from 1M to 4M (Figure 4). No error bars, confidence intervals, or multiple seeds are reported anywhere — all results are single-run, which is standard in VLM fine-tuning but leaves borderline gains (1–2 points) unverified statistically. Two architecturally different backbones mitigate cherry-picking. No code, data, or model weights are stated to be released in the paper (a project page is listed but no explicit open-source statement). Evaluation uses deterministic decoding (T=0) via EvalScope, aiding reproducibility. No Goodhart's Law concern — benchmarks are standard held-out sets not used in training. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: Data construction requires only CPU servers (two 128-core machines, ~20 hours for 4M examples); training runs on 8×H200 for one epoch — within reach of well-resourced labs and cloud budgets. Zero inference overhead. - Efficient: The rendering preprocessing is a one-time CPU cost; LoMo adds no new model parameters, no additional forward passes at inference, and minimal training compute overhead (interleaved tokens are a modest increase over text-only samples). - Effective: The controlled ratio ablation (Table 5) and component ablation (Table 2) together show that the gains are attributable to the interleaved cross-carrier formulation specifically, not to increased multimodal sample count or simple pixel exposure; 23/26 positive comparisons across two architectures is credible evidence. - Scalable: Tested only at 8B–9B scale; the authors explicitly disclaim verification at larger scales. Data scaling from 1M to 4M shows monotonically increasing benefit, but extrapolation beyond the tested range is unsupported by evidence. The work delivers a data curation procedure (reproducible from the paper's algorithm and hyperparameter appendix) but no software artifact, trained model weights, or released dataset. Strengths - The theoretical decomposition (Eq. 5–6) showing that NLL on LoMo-transformed data implicitly minimizes a KL divergence between text-carrier and visual-carrier predictive distributions is a clean, non-obvious insight that elevates this beyond a pure engineering contribution. - The controlled image-to-text ratio ablation (Table 5) explicitly anticipates and rules out the most obvious confound, demonstrating intellectual rigor that many data augmentation papers omit. - Architecture agnosticism and zero inference overhead make LoMo immediately applicable to any SFT pipeline; the data construction cost (~20 CPU-hours per 1M samples) is low enough for routine use. - Measuring both set-level (MIR) and pair-level (pairwise cosine distance) alignment metrics provides complementary evidence that the mechanism is doing what the theory claims — the finding that Standard SFT actually pushes paired text/image representations apart during training (0.52→0.57) is a striking and counter-intuitive result. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors provide a dedicated Limitations section and directly confront three scope boundaries: (1) LoMo is applied only during SFT, with pre-training and RL post-training integration left to future work; (2) the middle-span heuristic is not difficulty-aware or curriculum-style, leaving potential gains on the table; (3) validation is restricted to 8B–9B models due to compute constraints. This direct engagement with limitations is credibility-positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - No direct comparison against other cross-modal alignment methods (VCD, HA-DPO) on downstream task accuracy — the paper claims to address the same modality gap from the data side, making accuracy comparisons with these approaches a natural and missing baseline. - The rendered evaluation protocol uses the same font, size (20pt), and rendering pipeline for both LoMo training (rendered spans) and test-time question rendering, which may inflate rendered evaluation improvements through distribution matching; varying the test-time renderer (different fonts, resolutions) would disentangle genuine alignment from renderer-specific overfitting. - No breakdown of which SFT data categories (math, natural language, OCR) benefit most from rendering — this would guide practitioners on where to concentrate the rewrite budget and explain why visual perception and instruction following gain more than math reasoning. - Single-run results with no error bars; gains of 1–2 points on individual benchmarks (e.g., CC-OCR +2.21, DocVQA +1.72 on LLaVA standard evaluation) cannot be distinguished from run-to-run variance without multiple seeds. - No code, dataset, or model release stated, reducing immediate independent reproducibility. Bottom Line A practitioner fine-tuning VLMs on standard SFT corpora gains a concrete, zero-inference-cost technique with solid mechanistic grounding and thorough ablations; the method is immediately applicable and the evidence — particularly the dramatic rendered evaluation gains and the controlled ratio experiment — is credible enough to justify adoption pending replication at larger model scales.

Prism: Private Relational Data Synthesis with Language Models

dl.acm.org · 📄 Open PDF
**TL;DR** - **What it does:** This paper introduces Prism, an end-to-end framework that adapts the PATE (Private Aggregation of Teacher Ensembles) framework to autoregressive LLM generation for differentially private synthesis of relational tabular data via a novel tuple distillation mechanism. - **Why it's important:** Existing DP synthesis methods either apply DP-SGD to LLMs — which corrupts pretrained representations and degrades utility — or rely on lower-capacity models (graphical models, GANs) that fail to capture complex attribute correlations under a tight privacy budget; Prism sidesteps both problems. - **How it does it:** An ensemble of teacher LLMs fine-tuned on disjoint data partitions vote token-by-token during autoregressive generation; trie-guided pruning constrains votes to schema-valid tokens and short-circuits deterministic completions, data-dependent adaptive noise scales Gaussian perturbation to histogram range, and probabilistic sampling with threshold filtering replaces argmax to preserve diversity. - **The good:** Prism outperforms eight DP synthesis baselines across five benchmark datasets at all tested DP budgets (ε ∈ {1.0, 3.2, 6.8, 10.0}), with a formal end-to-end RDP-based privacy proof that correctly handles the adaptive (data-dependent) noise mechanism. - **The missing:** Evaluation is limited to small-to-medium tables (up to 236K rows, 22 columns) with entirely enumerable attribute domains; the method's behavior on truly high-cardinality continuous attributes — which require pre-discretization that the paper never discusses — is unaddressed. - **Read it or not:** Database and privacy researchers working on DP data synthesis should read this — it delivers a clean, formally sound PATE-to-LLM adaptation with strong empirical results; practitioners needing fast synthesis should be aware the 8.8h runtime on a dataset of 32K rows using 8× A100s is a real deployment barrier.
Full summary
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Prism, an end-to-end framework that adapts the PATE (Private Aggregation of Teacher Ensembles) framework to autoregressive LLM generation for differentially private synthesis of relational tabular data via a novel tuple distillation mechanism. - Why it's important: Existing DP synthesis methods either apply DP-SGD to LLMs — which corrupts pretrained representations and degrades utility — or rely on lower-capacity models (graphical models, GANs) that fail to capture complex attribute correlations under a tight privacy budget; Prism sidesteps both problems. - How it does it: An ensemble of teacher LLMs fine-tuned on disjoint data partitions vote token-by-token during autoregressive generation; trie-guided pruning constrains votes to schema-valid tokens and short-circuits deterministic completions, data-dependent adaptive noise scales Gaussian perturbation to histogram range, and probabilistic sampling with threshold filtering replaces argmax to preserve diversity. - The good: Prism outperforms eight DP synthesis baselines across five benchmark datasets at all tested DP budgets (ε ∈ {1.0, 3.2, 6.8, 10.0}), with a formal end-to-end RDP-based privacy proof that correctly handles the adaptive (data-dependent) noise mechanism. - The missing: Evaluation is limited to small-to-medium tables (up to 236K rows, 22 columns) with entirely enumerable attribute domains; the method's behavior on truly high-cardinality continuous attributes — which require pre-discretization that the paper never discusses — is unaddressed. - Read it or not: Database and privacy researchers working on DP data synthesis should read this — it delivers a clean, formally sound PATE-to-LLM adaptation with strong empirical results; practitioners needing fast synthesis should be aware the 8.8h runtime on a dataset of 32K rows using 8× A100s is a real deployment barrier. Problem & Motivation Organizations store sensitive personal data in relational tables and need to share it for analytics and ML without violating privacy regulations (GDPR, HIPAA). The standard approach — privately training a generative model from scratch — forces a harsh privacy-utility trade-off: injecting DP noise into LLM weights (DP-SGD) distorts the pretrained representations that make LLMs useful for capturing complex attribute correlations, yielding poor utility. Non-LLM alternatives (Bayesian networks, GANs, autoencoders, diffusion models) lack the representational capacity to model high-dimensional joint distributions under a tight privacy budget. The specific gap: how to leverage LLMs' pretrained knowledge for relational synthesis while enforcing formal, provable DP without corrupting pretrained representations. Key Idea Perturb LLM outputs rather than weights: treat the problem as PATE-style knowledge distillation where k teacher LLMs vote at the token level during autoregressive generation, and add calibrated Gaussian noise to the token vote histogram before selection. The critical enabling trick is a token trie built per attribute domain that simultaneously enforces schema validity (pruning out-of-domain tokens), reduces the number of privacy-budget-consuming teacher queries (short-circuiting deterministic completions when a trie node has a single child), and compacts the vote histogram to improve the signal-to-noise ratio of the DP mechanism. Methodology Prism is a three-step pipeline. Step 1 (teacher training): partition the private table D into k disjoint equal subsets; serialize each tuple as "A1 is v1, A2 is v2, ..." and fine-tune k copies of a pretrained LLM using causal language modeling (CLM) cross-entropy loss. Step 2 (tuple distillation): for each token position in an autoregressive generation, query all k teachers for their top-token predictions; aggregate into a count histogram; apply three innovations — (a) Trie-guided pruning: for each attribute, build a token trie from the attribute domain; prune out-of-domain tokens from the histogram; short-circuit single-child trie nodes without a teacher query; (b) Data-dependent DP noise: compute histogram range r = max(c[τ]) − min(c[τ]), perturb r with Gaussian noise N(0,(σr)²) (sensitivity Δr = 2, DP-accounted), then linearly interpolate a noise scale σh ∈ [σh_min, σh_max] where high-range (strong consensus) histograms get larger σh (saves budget) and low-range gets smaller σh (preserves signal); add N(0,(σh)²) to each count (sensitivity Δh = √2); (c) Probabilistic sampling with threshold filtering: zero out noisy counts below 2σh, normalize the remaining histogram, and sample rather than taking the argmax. Repeat until the RDP accountant signals budget exhaustion, yielding differentially private distilled dataset D̂. Step 3 (student training): fine-tune a student LLM on D̂ (no additional privacy cost by post-processing property), then sample the final synthetic dataset D̃ by autoregressive generation from the student. Privacy analysis uses Rényi DP (RDP) composition. Sensitivity of the range query Δr = 2 (Lemma 2: disjoint partitions mean one tuple change affects at most one teacher, shifting max and min by at most 1). Histogram L2 sensitivity Δh = √2 (Lemma 3: worst case is a valid→valid token vote change). Per-round RDP: εr(α) = 2α/(σr)² for range release; εh_t(α|H_t) = α/(σh_t)² for histogram release conditioned on privatized history. The adaptive noise σh_t is a deterministic function of privatized history H_t, so RDP composes additively across rounds. Standard RDP-to-(ε,δ)-DP conversion completes the proof (Theorems 1–2). Three critical design decisions: (1) Token-level voting, not value-level — well-justified: LLM knowledge is encoded at the token level; treating multi-token categorical values (e.g., "Bachelors" → ["ba","che","lors"]) as atomic would require new vocabulary entries without pretrained representations. (2) Trie short-circuiting — well-justified: reduces queries from one-per-token to approximately one-per-value for categorical attributes, delivering substantial budget savings. (3) Probabilistic rather than deterministic argmax selection — well-justified and empirically validated: argmax induces mode collapse toward high-frequency values, artificially inflating recall while collapsing ranking metrics (AUROC, AUPRC). Key Results Experiment 1 (main comparison): Prism outperforms 8 baselines across 5 datasets (Abalone 4,177; Tax 30,000; Adult 32,561; Cardio 70,000; Diabetes 236,378 rows) at ε = 6.8, δ = 1e-5. Representative results — Adult F1: Prism 0.8222 vs. best baseline DP-LLM Full 0.8036; Adult AUROC: Prism 0.6941 vs. best baseline AIM 0.6916; Tax F1: Prism 0.5776 vs. next-best DP-LLM LoRA 0.5555; Diabetes F1: Prism 0.7939 vs. next-best AIM 0.7910. Prism consistently achieves highest AUROC and AUPRC across almost all datasets. Baseline set is current and strong: includes both DP-SGD LLM methods, GAN-based, diffusion-based, and graphical-model-based methods. Experiment 2 (ablation on Adult): All three components contribute. Removing probabilistic sampling + threshold filtering causes the largest degradation in ranking metrics (AUROC drops from 0.6941 to 0.6726), with spuriously high recall (0.9192) confirming mode collapse. Removing data-dependent DP and token pruning each cause smaller but consistent drops. Experiment 5 (varying ε on Adult): Prism is best or near-best at all four tested budgets ε ∈ {1.0, 3.2, 6.8, 10.0}; even at ε = 1.0 (strongest privacy), Prism outperforms all baselines. Experiment 7 (impact of noise): Prism w/o DP achieves AUROC 0.7098 vs. Prism 0.6941 — the noise slightly reduces ranking quality as expected, but F1 difference is small (0.8169 vs. 0.8222), suggesting the noise acts as mild regularization at the chosen scale. Experiment 11 (runtime): 8.8h total for Prism with 1800 teachers on Adult (8× A100s, 96 vCPUs, 1TB RAM), dominated by teacher fine-tuning (linear in k). 600 teachers achieves 4.1h with near-identical utility. Lightweight baselines run in < 1h; P3GM takes 8.4h. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD, NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The genuine novelty is the first adaptation of PATE to autoregressive LLM-based tabular data synthesis, with the trie-guided pruning + data-dependent adaptive noise + probabilistic sampling mechanism enabling tractable budget use. Each individual component (tries, data-dependent DP, probabilistic sampling) has prior art in the database literature; the contribution is the non-obvious combination needed to make token-level PATE work for this setting. The student-teacher LLM framing and LLMs-for-tabular-data are both established; what is new is the mechanism connecting them under formal DP. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present (Exp 2) and cover all three components, but only on the Adult dataset — the paper asserts "consistent trends across datasets" without showing ablation tables for other datasets, which is a reproducibility gap given that Cardio and Abalone show narrower advantages over baselines. Error bars (mean ± std of 3 runs) are reported throughout. The RDP-based privacy proof is complete and technically sound, correctly handling the data-dependent adaptive noise via the conditioning argument that σh_t is a deterministic function of privatized history H_t. Code is publicly available at https://github.com/golden-eggs-lab/prism. One methodological concern: the paper uses only downstream predictive utility (Train-on-Synthetic, Test-on-Real with 9 classifiers) as the evaluation metric; statistical fidelity metrics (marginal distributions, pairwise correlations, Jensen-Shannon divergences) are entirely absent, making it impossible to assess whether Prism preserves data structure beyond what classifiers capture. No empirical privacy auditing (membership inference, attribute inference attacks) is performed to validate that the formal DP guarantee translates to empirical resilience. Published as a peer-reviewed SIGMOD paper (Proc. ACM Manag. Data, received Oct 2025, accepted Feb 2026). Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL + SYSTEMS (builds and evaluates a complete end-to-end synthesis system). - Practical: Requires 8× A100 GPUs and 1TB RAM as tested; a reduced configuration (600 teachers) achieves nearly identical utility in 4.1h, lowering the hardware bar, but the memory footprint of 1800 fine-tuned LLM copies is still substantial and not characterized separately. - Efficient: Teacher fine-tuning dominates runtime and scales linearly with k; at 1800 teachers, Prism takes 8.8h vs. < 1h for lightweight alternatives — a 10–40× overhead that is a real deployment barrier for iterative workflows. - Effective: The TSTR evaluation consistently shows Prism synthetic data preserves downstream classification utility better than all 8 baselines at all tested DP budgets, directly solving the stated problem; the student model generalizes substantially better than direct release of the distilled set (29,035 unique tuples vs. 501 in teacher-sampling on Adult). - Scalable: Tested up to 236K rows and 22 attributes — moderate tabular scale, not stress-scale. The trie-based approach requires fully enumerable attribute domains, which is structurally incompatible with high-cardinality continuous attributes without pre-discretization; this discretization step and its privacy/utility implications are not addressed. Single-table scope only; multi-table and foreign key scenarios are acknowledged as out of scope. The paper delivers an open-source prototype at https://github.com/golden-eggs-lab/prism. Strengths - The RDP-based end-to-end privacy proof is technically non-trivial and correctly handled: the key step — showing that σh_t is a deterministic function of the privatized history H_t, enabling additive RDP composition — is not obvious and is cleanly formalized. - Trie-guided pruning simultaneously solves three problems: schema validity enforcement, vocabulary restriction to schema-plausible tokens, and dramatic reduction in teacher query count (often one query per multi-token categorical value), making the approach budget-efficient in a way that would not be apparent from the PATE starting point alone. - The mode-collapse hypothesis for argmax selection is empirically confirmed in Exp 2 with the characteristic signature: highest recall (0.9192) but worst AUROC/AUPRC — a crisp, interpretable result that validates a key design choice. - The evaluation at ε = 1.0 (very high privacy) is particularly informative: Prism still leads all baselines, suggesting the pretrained LLM's prior knowledge provides a floor of utility that other methods cannot match under strong noise. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly limit scope to single-table synthesis, noting multi-table and foreign key handling as "orthogonal." They acknowledge the runtime disadvantage vs. lightweight baselines and discuss it as a utility-cost trade-off. They note the consistent LLM architecture choice (same model for teachers and students) as a simplification. They address the pretraining exposure concern (§7.1) with an argument that DistilGPT2 was trained on unstructured web text unlikely to contain the benchmark tables in CSV form, and cite the Diabetes dataset (postdates OpenWebText) as additional evidence — though this argument is intuitive rather than rigorous. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The trie-based approach requires each attribute domain to be explicitly enumerated. For continuous numerical attributes (floating-point income, continuous age), this requires pre-discretization — but the paper never discusses how discretization is performed, what discretization granularity is used, or how granularity affects the privacy-utility trade-off. The benchmark datasets happen to have largely categorical attributes and low-cardinality integers; this structural dependency is a serious unacknowledged gap for real-world tables with continuous-valued columns. - Ablation study (Exp 2) is presented only for Adult, not for the other four datasets. The claim of "consistent trends across datasets" is unverified and particularly important for datasets where Prism's margin over baselines is smaller (e.g., Cardio, where PATE-CTGAN achieves F1 0.7332 vs. Prism 0.7533). - No empirical privacy audit is conducted (no membership inference or attribute inference attacks). The formal DP guarantee is sound, but the gap between provable and empirical privacy can be large depending on calibration; its absence is a standard omission in the DP synthesis literature but notable given the paper's emphasis on formal guarantees over empirical heuristics. - Statistical fidelity metrics (marginal distributions, pairwise correlations) are absent. Using only downstream classification utility as the sole metric is a proxy that can miss structural data quality issues — a synthetic dataset that inflates certain attribute combinations might score well on TSTR while being useless for other downstream tasks. - Experiment 7 shows Prism w/o DP achieves slightly lower F1 (0.8169) than Prism (0.8222), meaning adding DP noise marginally improves the primary metric. This counterintuitive result is not explained and could indicate that hyperparameters (noise scales) were tuned to implicitly benefit the Adult benchmark. Bottom Line Prism delivers a technically sound and empirically well-validated contribution — the first clean PATE-LLM integration for DP tabular synthesis with a rigorous privacy proof and consistent gains over eight baselines — but the steep compute cost, unenumerated continuous-attribute limitation, and absence of statistical fidelity metrics and empirical privacy auditing mean a practitioner should treat the results as promising rather than deployment-ready for arbitrary real-world tables.

From Raw Experience to Skill Consumption: A Systematic Study of Model-Generated Agent Skills

arxiv.org · 📄 Open PDF
**TL;DR** - **What it does:** This paper introduces a utility-grounded evaluation framework that systematically studies the full three-stage lifecycle of model-generated agent skills (experience generation → skill extraction → skill consumption) across five agentic task domains, six target models, and five extractor models, and derives a meta-skill prior that consistently improves skill quality at extraction time. - **Why it's important:** Model-generated skills are increasingly deployed in commercial agent platforms as a scalable alternative to hand-crafted skills, but practitioners have had no principled basis for choosing extractors or predicting when skills will help versus hurt — the study finds that 25% of extractor-target pairings exhibit negative transfer. - **How it does it:** By holding the target model fixed while varying only the extractor across a controlled cross-matrix experiment, then using automated contrastive analysis of high-gap skill pairs to identify three validated rubric dimensions (Failure Mechanism Encoding, Actionable Specificity, High-Risk Action Blacklist) that predict downstream utility, and operationalizing them as a system-prompt insertion at extraction time. - **The good:** The validated 3-dimension meta-skill improves downstream performance in all nine evaluated cells (+1.55 pp average) and raises LLM judge pairwise accuracy on skill pairs from 46.4% to 73.8%, while the intuitive plausibility rubric hurts performance in 6 of 9 cells (−0.59 pp average). - **The missing:** The validated rubric was derived from and tested on the same experimental cells (3 domains × 3 targets), with no held-out domain to confirm generalizability; the study also does not test multi-skill libraries, skill retrieval, or cross-domain transfer, which are the primary deployment scenarios. - **Read it or not:** Essential reading for practitioners building agent skill libraries or researchers studying LLM agent self-improvement, as it provides the first comprehensive lifecycle analysis and a concrete, drop-in extraction improvement backed by systematic experiments.
Full summary
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces a utility-grounded evaluation framework that systematically studies the full three-stage lifecycle of model-generated agent skills (experience generation → skill extraction → skill consumption) across five agentic task domains, six target models, and five extractor models, and derives a meta-skill prior that consistently improves skill quality at extraction time. - Why it's important: Model-generated skills are increasingly deployed in commercial agent platforms as a scalable alternative to hand-crafted skills, but practitioners have had no principled basis for choosing extractors or predicting when skills will help versus hurt — the study finds that 25% of extractor-target pairings exhibit negative transfer. - How it does it: By holding the target model fixed while varying only the extractor across a controlled cross-matrix experiment, then using automated contrastive analysis of high-gap skill pairs to identify three validated rubric dimensions (Failure Mechanism Encoding, Actionable Specificity, High-Risk Action Blacklist) that predict downstream utility, and operationalizing them as a system-prompt insertion at extraction time. - The good: The validated 3-dimension meta-skill improves downstream performance in all nine evaluated cells (+1.55 pp average) and raises LLM judge pairwise accuracy on skill pairs from 46.4% to 73.8%, while the intuitive plausibility rubric hurts performance in 6 of 9 cells (−0.59 pp average). - The missing: The validated rubric was derived from and tested on the same experimental cells (3 domains × 3 targets), with no held-out domain to confirm generalizability; the study also does not test multi-skill libraries, skill retrieval, or cross-domain transfer, which are the primary deployment scenarios. - Read it or not: Essential reading for practitioners building agent skill libraries or researchers studying LLM agent self-improvement, as it provides the first comprehensive lifecycle analysis and a concrete, drop-in extraction improvement backed by systematic experiments. Problem & Motivation Language agents increasingly rely on reusable procedural artifacts called "skills" — distilled from past execution trajectories — to improve performance without retraining. Domain-level, model-generated skills are particularly attractive because they scale beyond labor-intensive hand-crafting and are now standard in several commercial agent platforms. However, prior evaluation work has studied only isolated stages of this lifecycle: SkillsBench, SWE-Skills-Bench, and Skills-in-the-Wild measure skill consumption with pre-authored or repository-sourced skills; SkillCraft studies extraction but restricts skills to executable function compositions not generalizable to broader domains. No prior work traverses the full experience-generation → extraction → consumption pipeline and asks whether model-generated skills reliably work, when they fail, and what structural properties determine utility. The result is that practitioners deploying skill libraries have no principled basis for choosing an extractor model, managing experience pool composition, or screening skills before deployment. Key Idea The core insight is that skill extraction capability is distinct from and uncorrelated with a model's task-solving capability — the same model can be a strong executor and a weak extractor, or vice versa — and that downstream skill utility is not predictable from surface textual properties such as fluency, format, or completeness. Instead, utility is governed by three specific content properties: encoding why agents fail with named mechanisms (not just that they fail), providing step-level procedures tied to domain objects and tools (not abstract process advice), and explicitly blacklisting high-risk action patterns. These properties can be operationalized as a compact meta-skill injected into the extractor's system prompt without modifying the extraction pipeline. Methodology The pipeline has three formal stages. Stage 1: a target model M executes tasks from a training split to produce an experience pool T_{M,D} containing both successful and failed trajectories. Stage 2: an extractor model E distills the pool into a single domain-level skill S_{E,M,D} via a two-stage framework — per-trajectory pattern extraction (up to K=3 success or failure patterns per trajectory, fully parallelizable) followed by tree-structured hierarchical consolidation (merging in groups of G=10 until a single consolidated pattern set remains, then converting to a schema-conformant skill via structured tool calls). Stage 3: the skill is inlined into M's system prompt and evaluated on held-out test tasks, with the metric being performance delta Δ(E,M,D) = Perf(M|skill,test) − Perf(M|test). Two aggregate metrics disentangle the two roles: Extraction Efficacy (EE) = average Δ across all targets for a fixed extractor, and Target Evolvability (TE) = average Δ across all extractors for a fixed target. The extraction framework intentionally uses minimal structure with no domain heuristics, leaving all abstraction decisions to the extractor model itself so that performance differences are attributable to extractor capability rather than pipeline engineering. Critical design decisions: (1) consolidating all experience into a single domain-level skill — well-justified for causal attribution but explicitly limits ecological validity; (2) minimal extraction scaffold — appropriate for isolating extractor differences but means the pipeline may underperform more engineered alternatives; (3) downstream Δ as the sole quality metric — well-justified by the finding that text-based quality signals are unreliable. The rubric discovery pipeline analyzes 17 high-gap skill pairs (same target, same domain, |Δ_A − Δ_B| ≥ 0.5pp), extracts per-pair differences via GPT-5.4, consolidates them into 7 candidate dimensions, and validates each by better-rate (proportion of pairs where the higher-Δ skill scores better on that dimension); three dimensions with better-rates 64–66% form the validated rubric. Key Results Across 5 domains, 6 targets, and 5 extractors, model-generated skills improve performance in 75% of extractor-target pairings and degrade it in 25%, with domain-level negative transfer rates ranging from 13% (SpreadsheetBench, SWE-bench-Verified) to 47% (ALFWorld). Extraction Efficacy is uncorrelated with model capability: on SpreadsheetBench, Gemini-3.1-Flash-Lite achieves the highest EE (+5.86 pp) while GPT-5.4 — the strongest task-performer — ranks last (+1.67 pp). Target Evolvability varies sharply within domains: on ALFWorld, GPT-5.4 reaches TE = +4.93 while Qwen3.5-9B reaches TE = −1.69 from the same set of extractors. Experience composition studies (3 domains, 5 success ratios) show pure-failure pools are consistently worst; the optimal mix is domain-specific (ALFWorld peaks at failure-heavy pools due to informative dead-end states; SpreadsheetBench peaks near all-success). Format normalization (Friedman test) yields p > 0.34 for all 4 formats on all 6 targets (no detectable effect), while switching extractors yields p < 0.005 for 5/6 targets. LLM pairwise judgment without rubric achieves 46.4% accuracy overall, declining to 15.8% on high-gap pairs (δ ≥ 5pp) — the fluent skill is the worse performer. The validated meta-skill improves all 9 evaluation cells (+1.55 pp average, largest +3.7 pp on SpreadsheetBench); the 7-dimension plausibility rubric hurts 6/9 cells (−0.59 pp average). Validated rubric raises pairwise judge accuracy from 46.4% to 73.8%. Alternative harness validation (Claude Code, Codex) on SpreadsheetBench yields consistent directional results (+0.4 pp average), confirming results are not artifacts of the Python-script harness. Novelty Classification NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING + NEW METHOD. The three-stage lifecycle analysis spanning extractors, targets, and domains is genuinely novel in scope — no prior benchmark crosses all three stages with domain-level, model-generated skills. The finding that LLM judgment of skill quality inversely predicts actual utility at high performance gaps (15.8% accuracy at δ ≥ 5pp) is a striking empirical result with direct practical consequences. The automated rubric-discovery and validation pipeline that closes the loop from diagnostic analysis to a concrete extraction improvement is a methodologically novel contribution. The meta-skill itself is a straightforward prompt-engineering intervention, not a novel algorithm. Rigor & Reproducibility All main table entries are averaged over 3 independent evaluation runs. The format experiment uses the Friedman test with σ-ratio effect size, providing a principled statistical basis. The experience composition study holds the extractor fixed and manipulates only the success-failure ratio, cleanly isolating one variable. Pairwise skill evaluation uses 9-vote majority voting with randomized presentation order to mitigate position bias, on 151 pairs exceeding a 0.5 pp gap threshold. Code is released at https://aka.ms/SkillLens. However, no confidence intervals or error bars appear in Table 1, making it difficult to determine whether individual small Δ values (e.g., ±0.5 pp) exceed run-to-run noise. The validated rubric was derived using pairs from the same experimental matrix it is subsequently tested on (the better-rate evaluation and the meta-skill evaluation draw from the same 9 (domain, target) cells), raising the risk of overfitting the rubric to the specific experimental distribution. No held-out domain is used to confirm generalizability of the rubric. This is an arXiv preprint (May 2026) without peer review; results should be treated with the corresponding confidence level. Prompt templates and hyperparameters are fully documented in appendices (B–H). Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: The extraction pipeline runs on API-accessible frontier models or locally served open-source models (Qwen3.5-35B, Qwen3.5-9B on 8 NVIDIA B200 GPUs via vLLM); the meta-skill is a prompt insertion requiring no additional infrastructure. - Efficient: Per-trajectory extraction is embarrassingly parallel; hierarchical consolidation scales as O(n log n) in trajectory count with fixed group size; the meta-skill adds zero inference-time compute overhead. - Effective: The meta-skill improves downstream performance in all 9 evaluated cells, but absolute effect sizes are modest (+1.55 pp average); the core finding that 25% of pairings exhibit negative transfer is robustly replicated across two alternative harnesses (Claude Code, Codex). - Scalable: The study consolidates all experience into a single domain-level skill and evaluates only in-distribution held-out tasks; performance with multi-skill libraries, larger experience pools, or cross-domain transfer is not evaluated and is explicitly deferred to future work — scalability claims are not substantiated experimentally. Software artifact: code available at https://aka.ms/SkillLens. Strengths - The controlled cross-matrix design (6 targets × 5 extractors × 5 domains) is the most comprehensive evaluation of the skill lifecycle to date and surfaces non-obvious empirical patterns — particularly that extraction capability is a distinct, non-monotone function of model capability across all domains tested. - The quantified finding that LLM judgment of skill quality inversely predicts actual utility at high performance gaps (15.8% accuracy at δ ≥ 5pp) is concrete, surprising, and practically consequential: it invalidates the common assumption that a capable LLM can screen a skill library reliably. - The automated rubric-discovery pipeline (contrastive pair analysis → candidate dimensions → better-rate validation → end-to-end meta-skill test) is methodologically coherent and avoids purely post-hoc rationalization by testing the rubric in downstream extraction rather than only in pairwise judgment. - The experience composition study (Section 5.1) cleanly isolates the effect of success-failure pool composition, yielding directly actionable guidance: all-failure pools are universally worst, and the optimal mix is domain-specific for identifiable reasons (ALFWorld failures reveal dead-end states; SpreadsheetBench successes encode stable procedural strategies). Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly and directly acknowledge that consolidating all experience into a single domain-level skill injected via system prompt is a deliberate interpretability choice that sacrifices coverage — it excludes retrieval, multi-skill libraries, planning scaffolds, and skill interference. They frame scaling to richer harnesses and larger skill libraries as future work rather than a gap, and acknowledge that skills extracted from imperfect experience pools may carry biases or unsafe shortcuts that become harder to audit at scale. This transparent scoping is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The validated rubric was derived from pairs drawn from the same 3-domain, 3-target cells used for the meta-skill evaluation; no held-out domain tests whether the three dimensions generalize to BFCL or SEAL-0, where experience pool dynamics (tool-calling, multi-hop retrieval) differ substantially from the derivation domains. - No statistical significance tests appear for the main Δ matrix (Table 1); with only 3 evaluation runs per cell, many small positive and negative Δ values could fall within run-to-run noise, and the reported 75%/25% positive/negative split may shift under more runs. - The study does not report experience pool sizes per (target, domain) pair or investigate sensitivity to pool size — a critical practical variable, since experience collection is the most expensive step and pool sizes presumably vary widely across domains. - The experience generator and skill consumer are the same model M, confounding pool quality with target capability; a stronger design would decouple them (e.g., generate experience with a fixed strong model and vary only the extractor and consumer). - The behavioral impact analysis (Appendix D) characterizing why skill consumption helps GPT-5.4 and hurts Qwen3.5-9B is qualitative trajectory inspection on one domain (SpreadsheetBench); it is descriptive rather than causal and is not replicated across other domains or targets. Bottom Line Practitioners building model-generated skill libraries should read this paper — it provides the first rigorous evidence that extraction capability is model-specific and uncorrelated with task strength, that textual plausibility is a misleading quality signal, and that inserting a validated 3-dimension rubric into any extractor's system prompt measurably reduces negative transfer without modifying the underlying pipeline.

Auto-Dreamer: Learning Offline Memory Consolidation for Language Agents

arxiv.org · 📄 Open PDF
**TL;DR** - **What it does:** This paper introduces Auto-Dreamer, a Qwen3-14B consolidator trained with GRPO to perform offline cross-session region rewriting of language-agent memory banks, replacing accumulated session-specific entries with compact, provenance-grounded abstractions. - **Why it's important:** Language agents operating over task streams accumulate redundant, contradictory, and overspecific memories that bloat retrieval context and degrade performance; existing methods couple acquisition and consolidation into a single online step that lacks the global view needed to fix this. - **How it does it:** The consolidator treats a selected working region as read-only evidence, performs bounded tool-use rollouts (search_memory, get_source_trace, synthesize) over it and provenance-linked source trajectories, and replaces the region with a synthesized replacement set — making forgetting, deduplication, and abstraction structurally default rather than explicit decisions. - **The good:** Achieves 41.1% success on ScienceWorld (+7.0pp over UMEM, the strongest RL-trained baseline) using 12× less active memory, and transfers to held-out ALFWorld (60.2%) and WebArena (52.3%) without retraining, including across a Qwen-to-Gemini writer-backbone shift. - **The missing:** The ALFWorld margin over UMEM is only 1.8pp with overlapping bootstrap confidence intervals, the two strongest RL-trained baselines are excluded from the WebArena column due to context-window constraints, and no seed or task-order variance is reported for main results. - **Read it or not:** Researchers building multi-session language-agent memory systems should read this — the region-rewriting formulation and counterfactual utility reward are intellectually novel, the cross-domain transfer is a strong result, and the 10-baseline comparison is unusually thorough for the subfield.
