Full summary
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Auto-Dreamer, a Qwen3-14B consolidator trained with GRPO to perform offline cross-session region rewriting of language-agent memory banks, replacing accumulated session-specific entries with compact, provenance-grounded abstractions. - Why it's important: Language agents operating over task streams accumulate redundant, contradictory, and overspecific memories that bloat retrieval context and degrade performance; existing methods couple acquisition and consolidation into a single online step that lacks the global view needed to fix this. - How it does it: The consolidator treats a selected working region as read-only evidence, performs bounded tool-use rollouts (search_memory, get_source_trace, synthesize) over it and provenance-linked source trajectories, and replaces the region with a synthesized replacement set — making forgetting, deduplication, and abstraction structurally default rather than explicit decisions. - The good: Achieves 41.1% success on ScienceWorld (+7.0pp over UMEM, the strongest RL-trained baseline) using 12× less active memory, and transfers to held-out ALFWorld (60.2%) and WebArena (52.3%) without retraining, including across a Qwen-to-Gemini writer-backbone shift. - The missing: The ALFWorld margin over UMEM is only 1.8pp with overlapping bootstrap confidence intervals, the two strongest RL-trained baselines are excluded from the WebArena column due to context-window constraints, and no seed or task-order variance is reported for main results. - Read it or not: Researchers building multi-session language-agent memory systems should read this — the region-rewriting formulation and counterfactual utility reward are intellectually novel, the cross-domain transfer is a strong result, and the 10-baseline comparison is unusually thorough for the subfield. Problem & Motivation Language agents deployed across streams of related tasks accumulate session-level memories through local writers, but these banks grow monotonically, accumulate near-verbatim duplicates, and encode contradictory or overspecific entries whose shared structure is only visible when looking across sessions simultaneously. The core gap is a consolidation problem: existing methods — retrieval-augmented stores, procedural skill libraries (Voyager, Memp), reflection methods (Reflexion, ExpeL), and RL-trained writers (UMEM, Mem-α) — all perform acquisition and consolidation in a single online pass with access only to the current session's evidence. This precludes discovering recurring patterns, abstracting reusable procedures, resolving contradictions across sessions, or making principled forgetting decisions. A secondary problem is that RL-trained writers optimize online construction or retrieval but do not learn which memories are load-bearing versus redundant, so they cannot trade off success against bank compactness. Key Idea The single core insight is that replacing CRUD-style per-entry memory updates with region rewriting — treating a selected working region as read-only evidence and synthesizing a fresh replacement set that supersedes it — makes compactness, abstraction, deduplication, and forgetting structurally default rather than behaviors that must be explicitly learned. In CRUD-style systems, old entries persist unless the controller explicitly edits or deletes them, so consolidation decomposes into many local retention decisions and redundant entries survive by default. In region rewriting, information survives only if it is re-synthesized into the replacement set, shifting the inductive bias toward compact abstractions without requiring the policy to learn to delete. This is grounded in the operational structure of complementary learning systems (CLS) theory — fast hippocampal acquisition, slow neocortical consolidation — adopted as a design principle rather than a biological claim. Methodology Auto-Dreamer is a two-timescale system. The fast path is a fixed prompted writer (Qwen3-14B on ALFWorld/ScienceWorld, Gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview on WebArena) that runs after each session, emitting zero or more typed INSERT_SEMANTIC or INSERT_PROCEDURAL entries with provenance pointers into an append-only bank; it does not read existing entries or resolve conflicts. The slow path is a learned consolidator Cθ (Qwen3-14B initialized, fine-tuned with GRPO) that triggers every k sessions (k=8 ALFWorld, k=10 ScienceWorld, k=5 WebArena). At each consolidation event, the working region R is defined as the union of newly written entries plus entries retrieved by the task agent during the interval. The consolidator performs a bounded tool-use rollout over (R, TR) using four tools — search_memory, check_memory, get_source_trace, synthesize — and terminates either explicitly or at a turn budget. The bank is updated as B = (B∖R) ∪ S. The read operator uses a frozen Qwen2.5-3B sentence encoder (2048-d) with FAISS IndexFlatIP retrieval at top-K=3 within a 1,500-token budget, refreshed every 8 environment steps during each episode. Training uses GRPO over 200 steps (batch 16, group N=8) on ScienceWorld trajectories only, with 8 H100 GPUs. The reward is r_g = U_V(S_g) + α·r_cf(S_g; V), where U_V is mean task return on a held-out evaluation set V using a local bank containing only the synthesized entries (B_g = S_g), and r_cf is a counterfactual term estimated by Monte Carlo random masking of entries (fraction ρ), penalizing redundant entries (masking them leaves performance unchanged) and rewarding load-bearing ones (masking them hurts). Three critical design decisions: (1) local-bank credit assignment for training, which aligns the reward unit with the policy's output unit but creates a distribution shift relative to full-bank deployment — partially addressed empirically; (2) provenance grounding requiring all synthesized entries to cite source_ids, which prevents hallucination but means information missed by the writer cannot be recovered; (3) heuristic region selection (new + recently retrieved), which is not ablated. Key Results Continual-memory deployment (bank starts empty, grows during evaluation, prequential protocol): - ScienceWorld (660 tasks): Auto-Dreamer 41.1% SR vs UMEM 34.1% (+7.0pp), ReasoningBank 30.9% (+10.2pp), no-memory 28.7% (+12.4pp); AUC 0.411 vs 0.353 (UMEM); bank 6,947 tokens vs 80,918 (UMEM), 155,117 (ReasoningBank). - ALFWorld (274 tasks): 60.2% [54.65, 65.59 bootstrap CI] vs UMEM 58.4% [53.15, 63.65] (+1.8pp, overlapping CIs), Mem-α 57.4% [52.04, 62.59]; bank 10,954 tokens vs 62,947 (UMEM, 6× less). - WebArena (117 tasks): 52.3% [43.5, 60.9]; leads all evaluated baselines; UMEM and Mem-α excluded due to context-window constraints, so comparison is against AWM/LightMem at 52.0% and Reflexion/ExpeL at ≤50.8%. Fixed-bank consolidation (controlled, single-pass): Auto-Dreamer 72.7% ALFWorld vs ExpeL 70.1% (+2.6pp); 44.3% ScienceWorld vs Mem0 42.0% (+2.3pp). Narrower margins than continual deployment, consistent with removal of cumulative retrieval-competition effects. Ablations: Writer-only (no consolidation) gives 48.0%/29.6%/44.2%; untrained pipeline (region rewriting without GRPO) gives 59.2%/31.4%/46.6% — showing that structure (region rewriting) accounts for most bank-size reduction and GRPO training adds 9.7pp on ScienceWorld and 5.7pp on WebArena. Without counterfactual reward, the bank grows rapidly during training without corresponding performance gains; with it, the bank first grows then contracts while performance is maintained. Cadence sensitivity: ScienceWorld drops ~10–15pp at k=1 or k=20 relative to k=10; ALFWorld is more robust (range ~5pp). Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW FORMULATION. The region-rewriting formulation of offline consolidation and the GRPO-trained multi-step tool-using consolidator with counterfactual credit assignment are genuinely novel. The two-timescale architecture is conceptually related to LightMem but differs fundamentally in that LightMem uses fixed prompted pipelines with per-entry CRUD decisions, while Auto-Dreamer learns a rewrite policy over regions. The CLS inspiration is well-established in cognitive science but has not been operationalized as a region-rewriting RL objective for agent memory. The application of GRPO to tool-using memory consolidation (as opposed to task execution) is novel in this setting. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present and informative: the writer-only vs. untrained vs. trained progression isolates the contributions of region rewriting and GRPO; the counterfactual reward ablation shows its specific effect on bank size vs. task performance; the cadence sweep characterizes a key hyperparameter. Bootstrap CIs (N=10,000) are reported in Appendix H — these reveal that the ALFWorld advantage over UMEM is not clearly statistically significant (overlapping 95% CIs). No seed or task-order variance is reported for main runs (acknowledged as a limitation). Prompts and hyperparameters are reported verbatim in the appendix. UMEM and Mem-α are excluded from WebArena and not retrained with larger context, which weakens the WebArena claim. The training distribution includes ScienceWorld evaluation (the consolidator is trained and evaluated on ScienceWorld tasks), creating an in-distribution advantage that is mitigated but not eliminated by the held-out task disjointness. Training uses environment simplifications (teleport, openDoors, self-watering pots) whose effect on learned generalization is not analyzed. No code/checkpoint release URL is provided; the paper references OpenTinker and verl frameworks implicitly. This is a preprint (arXiv:2605.20616v1, May 2026); results are not peer-reviewed. Deployability (4-property check) EMPIRICAL profile. - Practical: Runs on 8 H100 GPUs for training and 4 GH200 GPUs for evaluation serving multiple model endpoints; the consolidator (Qwen3-14B) and task agent (Qwen3.5-9B or Gemini) require substantial GPU memory but are commercially available models. The consolidation step is offline (does not block task execution) and triggers periodically. - Efficient: Active memory banks are 6–24× smaller than comparable-performance baselines at retrieval time; the consolidator incurs additional LLM calls every k sessions, but per-episode total token cost across the stream (writer + consolidator + agent) is not reported, making full cost accounting impossible. - Effective: Evidence is strong on ScienceWorld (+7pp, large margin), moderate on ALFWorld (+1.8pp, overlapping CIs with the strongest baseline), and incomplete on WebArena (two strongest RL-trained baselines excluded). - Scalable: Tested on up to 660-task streams; no stress-scale test at 10×–100× episodes is reported. The formal relationship between the local-bank training objective and full persistent-bank performance under long-horizon composition is uncharacterized. Consolidation cadence must be tuned per domain (sensitivity varies substantially). No standalone software artifact is released; contribution is at paper and framework-integration level. Strengths - Region rewriting as a structural compactness primitive is a sharp idea: by making forgetting the default update semantics rather than something to be learned, the policy search space is substantially reduced and the training signal is cleaner. - The counterfactual utility reward correctly distinguishes load-bearing from redundant memories and ablations show it controls bank size without hurting task performance — a clean design choice with clear empirical support. - Transfer across task domains (ScienceWorld → ALFWorld → WebArena) and across writer backbones (Qwen → Gemini) without retraining is the strongest empirical result, providing genuine evidence that the consolidator learns a domain-agnostic procedure over textual memory entries. - Baseline coverage is unusually broad (10 systems, 7 families), implementation details are fully documented with commit hashes, and bootstrap CIs are reported — a higher standard of transparency than typical in this subfield. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors directly acknowledge: (1) evaluation restricted to three text-based environments, no claims about structured state representations or multimodal settings; (2) robustness to alternative writers, schemas, or noisier provenance links is untested — information missed by the writer cannot be recovered; (3) retrieval budget (top-K=3) favors compact banks and may disadvantage methods that benefit from looser budgets; (4) local-bank training objective is a surrogate for deployment-time bank composition with no formal characterization of the gap; (5) no seed or task-order variance reported, and several ALFWorld margins are small. The authors confront these directly rather than minimizing them, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The ALFWorld advantage over UMEM (1.8pp) has overlapping bootstrap CIs and is not statistically significant by the paper's own reported intervals; the abstract's claim of leading on ALFWorld is technically accurate in point estimate but could mislead without this context. - UMEM and Mem-α are excluded from WebArena due to context-window constraints but not retrained — this omission removes the two strongest RL-trained baselines from the hardest benchmark and inflates the apparent WebArena advantage without acknowledgment of the confound. - The region selection policy (new entries + recently retrieved entries) is a heuristic that is never ablated; an alternative selection strategy (e.g., clustering-based or staleness-based) might substantially change results. - Total LLM token consumption per episode across the full pipeline (writer + consolidator + task agent) is not reported, making it impossible to assess whether the compact bank trades reduced retrieval cost for increased consolidation cost. - Training uses three environment simplifications (teleport, openDoors, self-watering pots) for ScienceWorld; the degree to which these simplify the task distribution and inflate the learned policy's apparent generalization is not discussed. - The cadence hyperparameter k is environment-specific and shows substantial sensitivity (ScienceWorld drops ~10–15pp at k=1 or k=20), but no adaptive or learned cadence policy is considered, which would be required for deployment to new domains without tuning. Bottom Line The region-rewriting formulation is a genuinely sharp structural contribution and the ScienceWorld results and cross-domain transfer are strong enough to make this paper worth reading for anyone designing memory systems for multi-session language agents — but the ALFWorld advantage is within noise, the WebArena comparison is incomplete, and practitioners should not treat the results as fully established until code is released and peer review is completed.

Conceptual Schema Inference for Tabular Datasets using Large Language Models

arxiv.org · 📄 Open PDF
**TL;DR** - **What it does:** This paper introduces two LLM-based methods — GeSI (generative, decoder-based) and EmSI (embedding, encoder-based) — for automatically deriving conceptual schemas (entity types, type hierarchies, attributes, and inter-type relationships) from raw tabular datasets using only column headers and cell values, without external ontologies or task-specific training. - **Why it's important:** Data lakes and open data portals contain thousands of heterogeneous tables that cannot be navigated or queried without a global conceptual map; existing dataset discovery tools operate at the individual table or attribute level and provide no repository-wide semantic understanding. - **How it does it:** GeSI chains per-table LLM type-path inference → weighted merging → LLM-verified pruning → column-type hierarchy construction → bottom-up attribute inheritance → attribute-value-guided relationship and cardinality inference; EmSI clusters subjectness-weighted table embeddings into top-level types, then clusters columns into attributes, builds sub-hierarchies from Jaccard-similarity dendrograms with silhouette-based pruning, and identifies relationships via column-embedding similarity thresholds. - **The good:** GeSI achieves RI > 0.978 and Purity > 0.89 for conceptual attribute inference across all three evaluated benchmarks, and F1 scores of 0.675–0.967 for relationship inference, outperforming the embedding-based EmSI (best F1 ≤ 0.359) on every relationship metric. - **The missing:** No downstream task evaluation demonstrates that an inferred schema actually improves dataset search, data integration, or annotation quality; the only baselines for type inference and attributes are prior works from the same author group. - **Read it or not:** Database researchers working on data lake management, ontology learning, or LLM-assisted data integration will find the formal problem definition and the generative vs. embedding trade-off analysis valuable; practitioners should note that GeSI's attribute inference dominates runtime (>90% of total) and reaches ~2000 minutes at 10K tables with a local 14B model.
Full summary
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces two LLM-based methods — GeSI (generative, decoder-based) and EmSI (embedding, encoder-based) — for automatically deriving conceptual schemas (entity types, type hierarchies, attributes, and inter-type relationships) from raw tabular datasets using only column headers and cell values, without external ontologies or task-specific training. - Why it's important: Data lakes and open data portals contain thousands of heterogeneous tables that cannot be navigated or queried without a global conceptual map; existing dataset discovery tools operate at the individual table or attribute level and provide no repository-wide semantic understanding. - How it does it: GeSI chains per-table LLM type-path inference → weighted merging → LLM-verified pruning → column-type hierarchy construction → bottom-up attribute inheritance → attribute-value-guided relationship and cardinality inference; EmSI clusters subjectness-weighted table embeddings into top-level types, then clusters columns into attributes, builds sub-hierarchies from Jaccard-similarity dendrograms with silhouette-based pruning, and identifies relationships via column-embedding similarity thresholds. - The good: GeSI achieves RI > 0.978 and Purity > 0.89 for conceptual attribute inference across all three evaluated benchmarks, and F1 scores of 0.675–0.967 for relationship inference, outperforming the embedding-based EmSI (best F1 ≤ 0.359) on every relationship metric. - The missing: No downstream task evaluation demonstrates that an inferred schema actually improves dataset search, data integration, or annotation quality; the only baselines for type inference and attributes are prior works from the same author group. - Read it or not: Database researchers working on data lake management, ontology learning, or LLM-assisted data integration will find the formal problem definition and the generative vs. embedding trade-off analysis valuable; practitioners should note that GeSI's attribute inference dominates runtime (>90% of total) and reaches ~2000 minutes at 10K tables with a local 14B model. Problem & Motivation Data scientists working with data lakes, web tables, and open data portals face repositories of hundreds to thousands of tables from heterogeneous producers. These repositories exhibit both schema-level heterogeneity (the same attribute represented under different column names) and granularity variation (tables mixing entity records at different hierarchical levels). Existing methods for dataset discovery — keyword search, join discovery, table union search — operate at the level of individual tables or attribute pairs and cannot provide a global conceptual understanding: which entity types are present, what attributes characterize them, and what semantic relationships recur across types. Prior schema inference work targets semi-structured formats (XML, JSON, RDF) using syntactic similarity and is unsuitable for raw tabular data. Data profiling and join discovery enrich the logical schema but do not reduce complexity or resolve semantic inconsistencies. The gap is a method that automatically constructs an Entity-Relationship-style conceptual schema from arbitrary tabular repositories, without curated taxonomies, external ontologies, or task-specific labeled training data. Key Idea The central insight is that LLMs — both generative and embedding variants — contain sufficient world knowledge to map raw table columns to semantic entity types and to reason about hierarchical and relational structure, provided that inference is decomposed into tractable per-table or per-column subtasks and the outputs are merged, verified, and propagated bottom-up. For GeSI, the key trick is introducing an intermediate column type hierarchy as a shared semantic backbone: each column is anchored to its lowest-common-ancestor in this hierarchy, so columns that share a semantic domain (e.g., both Composer and Performer are typed as Musician) are clustered together before being resolved into distinct attributes via LLM role-naming. This prevents premature semantic collapse and is the direct source of GeSI's superior Purity in attribute inference. For EmSI, the key trick is replacing column identity by embedding similarity, using subjectness-weighted table embeddings to cluster tables that describe the same real-world concept regardless of their surface column names. Methodology GeSI is a three-step sequential pipeline. Step 1 infers a global type hierarchy: each table receives one or more type paths (e.g., Thing→Event→MusicEvent) via few-shot LLM prompts with optional constraints (ABS blacklists abstract top-level types; SLC forces second-level convergence; FET pre-extracts leaf types then generates full paths; FULL combines SLC and FET). Per-table paths are merged by edge frequency weighting and pruned via self-loop removal, inverse-edge resolution, and LLM-based is-a verification (each candidate edge A→B is validated by asking the LLM "Is A the parent type of B?"). Step 2 constructs a parallel column type hierarchy, maps each column to its lowest-common-ancestor semantic type, clusters co-typed columns per entity type, names attributes via LLM using column name + sampled values + table context, deduplicates synonymous attribute names (Fig. 7 prompt), and promotes attributes appearing in ≥θ=0.9 of child types up the hierarchy. Step 3 infers relationships by identifying attributes whose sampled values are named entities of other inferred types (two-pass top-K then refined selection), generating a predicate label, and inferring cardinality from three column statistics (null ratio ρ_null, multi-value ratio ρ_multi, uniqueness ratio ρ_uniq) plus sampled rows. LLM backbones evaluated: Llama-3.1-8B, Qwen2.5-14B, GPT-3.5, GPT-4. EmSI proceeds in four steps. Step 1 computes subjectness scores (via TableMiner+) for each column, constructs weighted table embeddings as weighted aggregations of column embeddings, and clusters tables via agglomerative clustering. Step 2 clusters all columns within each top-level type by column-embedding similarity; each cluster becomes a conceptual attribute; subject attributes are identified by named-entity membership and subjectness score ≥ 50%. Step 3 runs hierarchical clustering on tables within each top-level type using Jaccard similarity of their conceptual attribute sets, selects dendrogram slices with silhouette score within Δ=0.15 of the maximum, and promotes retained clusters to subtypes. Step 4 establishes a relationship between subject attribute cs_ai of type t_i and non-subject attribute ca_j of type t_j when more than δ_2 of ca_j's columns are within distance δ_1 of cs_ai's columns. Embedding backbones: Starmie, DeepJoin, SwAV (fine-tuned), SBERT, Unicorn (pretrained). Critical design decisions: (a) the hierarchy-first ordering in GeSI is well-justified as type context disambiguates attribute roles; (b) the frequency-weighted edge merging is reasonable but can produce overlapping type clusters (a Book table assigned to both Product and CreativeWork paths) that reduce RI; (c) the silhouette-based dendrogram pruning in EmSI is principled but Δ=0.15 was tuned on WDC and applied across all datasets, which risks overfitting to WDC structure. Key Results All experiments run on a single NVIDIA A100 (80 GB), 5 repetitions averaged. Four benchmarks: WDC (602 tables / 43 types), GDS (660 tables / 53 types), OD Small (158 tables / 24 types), OD Large (10,361 tables / 53 types — type/hierarchy only). Top-level types: GeSI achieves highest Purity on all benchmarks (0.963–0.993 best-variant), outperforming GeTT baseline by 2.8–8.1% and EmSI by 1.6–9.8%. EmSI achieves highest RI on 3/4 benchmarks (SwAV: 0.887 WDC, 0.894 GDS, 0.952 OD Small, 0.892 OD Large). GeSI RI lags primarily because its multi-path assignment creates overlapping clusters. Type hierarchy (PTCS): EmSI dominates (0.800–1.000 best-variant) vs. GeSI (0.633–0.868); EmSI outperforms GeSI's best by 9.3–15.3%. GeSI generates 24–213 more distinct types than EmSI but hierarchies are 0.5–1.0 levels shallower on three datasets. LLM-based verification improves GeSI's PTCS by +0.053 (GDS) to +0.153 (WDC). Prompt constraints (FET/FULL) improve RI by ~7% on WDC; ABS/SLC improve PTCS by 8–12% on WDC. Conceptual attributes: GeSI substantially outperforms EmSI and SI-LLM baseline: GeSI RI 0.978–0.995 / Purity 0.867–0.933 vs. EmSI best RI 0.933/Purity 0.803, SI-LLM best RI 0.966/Purity 0.742 (on WDC). GeSI Purity advantage over EmSI is up to +0.209. Relationships: GeSI best F1 0.729 (WDC), 0.807 (GDS), 0.967 (OD Small). EmSI best F1 0.359 (WDC), 0.177 (GDS), 0.222 (OD Small) — primarily recall-constrained (recall ≤ 0.243). GeSI worst variant outperforms EmSI best by +0.316 (WDC), +0.523 (GDS), +0.563 (OD Small). Cardinality: GeSI GPT-4 achieves 0.804–0.857 accuracy; Qwen2.5-14B peaks at 0.870 on WDC but drops to 0.712 on GDS. GPT-4 is most consistent across datasets. Scalability: Near-O(n) scaling for both methods on OD Large (1K–10K tables). GeSI (GPT-3.5) is ~1.4× slower than EmSI (SwAV) at 10K tables; ~20× slower at 1K tables. GeSI attribute inference accounts for >90% of GeSI runtime. EmSI attribute clustering is O(m²) in columns, becoming the primary bottleneck at large scale. Cost: GeSI with GPT-3.5 costs $1.23–$3.77 per benchmark dataset. Novelty Classification NEW PROBLEM: The paper formally defines repository-level conceptual schema inference from raw tabular data — distinguishing it from prior work on schema matching, logical schema profiling, and dataset discovery — and is the first to jointly target entity types, type hierarchies, conceptual attributes, and inter-type relationships under minimal metadata assumptions. NEW METHOD: GeSI's use of an intermediate column type hierarchy for attribute disambiguation is genuinely novel; EmSI's subjectness-weighted embedding clustering with silhouette-based hierarchical type inference is a new adaptation of known clustering methods to this problem. NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING: The paper establishes a quantified complementarity between generative and embedding approaches — generative dominates on attribute and relationship inference while embedding dominates on type hierarchy structural consistency — which was not previously characterized. The individual components (LLM prompting, embedding clustering, agglomerative clustering, silhouette pruning) are standard; the contribution lies in their composition and evaluation for this specific problem. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablation studies are present and informative: prompt constraint variants are evaluated individually on WDC (Figs. 14–15), and LLM-based verification is ablated on all four datasets (Fig. 16), confirming both design choices contribute meaningfully. Results are averaged over 5 runs, but standard deviations are not reported, making it impossible to assess statistical significance of small differences. Code, prompts, and datasets are publicly released at https://github.com/PierreWoL/SILM/. Ground truth for WDC and GDS is based on Schema.org type annotations with manual attribute and relationship labeling; OD Small/Large ground truth derives Schema.org type paths by upward traversal, not by independent domain-expert labeling, which systematically biases evaluation toward Schema.org vocabulary and may advantage GeSI (which generates Schema.org-like type names from LLM priors). The PTCS metric is novel and defined in this paper without external validation that it correlates with human-judged schema utility. The only baselines for type/hierarchy and attribute inference are GeTT and SI-LLM, both prior works from the same author group; no comparison to non-LLM schema discovery systems (e.g., ALITE, Abstra, classical ontology learning) is included. EmSI's Δ and (δ₁, δ₂) thresholds are selected on WDC and applied to all other datasets, including WDC in the evaluation, which is a minor but real source of optimism in WDC results for EmSI. Deployability (4-property check) EMPIRICAL profile. - Practical: GeSI can run using OpenAI API or local open-weight models (Llama-3.1-8B, Qwen2.5-14B) on a single A100 (80 GB); EmSI requires the same hardware for embedding models. Both paths use commercially available hardware, though 80 GB VRAM is high-end. - Efficient: GeSI attribute inference is the dominant cost (>90% of total) with ~2000 minutes at 10K tables using Qwen2.5-14B; EmSI's O(m²) column clustering becomes the bottleneck beyond ~350K columns (currently ~2000 minutes at 10K tables). Neither is near real-time for large repositories. - Effective: GeSI demonstrably solves attribute inference (RI > 0.978, Purity > 0.89) and relationship inference (F1 up to 0.967) on benchmark data; EmSI solves type hierarchy structural consistency (PTCS up to 1.0). No downstream task evaluation confirms utility in actual data lake management. - Scalable: Near-linear scaling demonstrated up to 10,361 tables / 354,666 columns; EmSI's quadratic column complexity would become prohibitive at 10× this column count; GeSI's linear LLM-call count scales better but API cost and latency become significant. The work delivers a software artifact (code + prompts + datasets at GitHub), but no pre-trained models or CLI tool; integration requires engineering effort. Strengths - The formal problem definition (Definition 1–7) cleanly separates conceptual schema inference from related tasks and is the most precise treatment of this problem in the literature, enabling future comparisons. - GeSI's intermediate column type hierarchy construction for attribute disambiguation is a technically sound and empirically validated design choice: it prevents premature semantic collapse (correctly separating Composer and Performer as distinct roles vs. a broad Person attribute) in a way that neither SI-LLM nor EmSI achieves. - The PTCS metric addresses a genuine evaluation problem — multiple valid hierarchies exist for the same data — by measuring ancestor-descendant consistency rather than exact edge match, and is better suited to this task than standard clustering metrics alone. - The efficiency analysis at scale (1K–10K tables, Figs. 17–19) is unusually thorough for a methods paper of this type and provides actionable guidance on when to use each approach. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly acknowledge: (1) GeSI fails on numeric and acronym-heavy columns lacking informative names (e.g., FIFA World Cup position codes "rcm", "st" incorrectly merged into a generic Number cluster); (2) GeSI sometimes produces near-synonymous sibling types (e.g., Musictrack and Song); (3) EmSI's relationship inference captures instance-level rather than conceptual-level relationships, limiting recall; (4) both methods may confuse observed cardinality in sampled data with true conceptual cardinality; (5) EmSI (Unicorn) failed to scale to GDS due to GPU memory limits from quadratic pairwise computation. The authors confront these limitations directly, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The most significant gap is the absence of any downstream task evaluation. The paper does not show that an inferred schema improves dataset search precision, reduces integration effort, or enables better annotation — which is the stated motivation. Schema quality measured against Schema.org ground truth is a proxy, not the target. - All baselines for type/hierarchy inference (GeTT) and attribute inference (SI-LLM) are prior works by the same group. No classical schema inference methods (ALITE, Abstra, schema summarization methods) or non-LLM ontology learning tools are included as comparators, making it impossible to assess how much improvement LLMs actually provide over simpler approaches. - The Schema.org-derived ground truth for OD Small/Large is systematically biased: paths are constructed by upward traversal of Schema.org, not by independent annotation. GeSI's LLM outputs are naturally aligned with Schema.org vocabulary (as it is in LLM pretraining data), which may inflate GeSI's scores relative to methods that do not share this bias. - The 5-run averaging without standard deviations prevents any statistical significance assessment, particularly relevant for closely competing configurations. - EmSI's O(m²) column clustering complexity is mentioned but not addressed; no approximation (e.g., ANN-based similarity) is proposed, which caps EmSI's practical applicability to repositories well below the scale of major data lakes. - The paper claims near-O(n) scaling for GeSI but does not evaluate attribute inference or relationship inference on OD Large (only type/hierarchy), so the claimed scalability is incomplete. Bottom Line Database researchers designing LLM-assisted data lake management systems will find this paper a rigorous and well-executed reference for both the formal problem of conceptual schema inference and the complementary strengths of generative versus embedding approaches; however, the absence of non-LLM baselines and downstream task validation means the practical value of the inferred schemas relative to simpler alternatives remains unestablished.

A Mechanistic Study of Tabular Foundation Models

arxiv.org · 📄 Open PDF
**TL;DR** - **What it does:** This paper delivers a mechanistic audit of three tabular foundation models (TabPFNv2, TabICLv2, Mitra), identifying each model's internal readout algorithm, localizing the architectural components responsible for permutation invariances, and designing mechanism-grounded adversarial attacks that reproduce predicted model-specific failures. - **Why it's important:** Tabular foundation models reach comparable benchmark accuracy despite radically different architectures, yet that convergence conceals qualitatively distinct internal algorithms with distinct attack surfaces — leaderboard scores give no guidance on which perturbations will break which model or how to harden them. - **How it does it:** Layer-wise linear probing, per-block causal knockout, cross-backbone readout transplantation, and a four-criteria faithful-surrogate validation framework (Pearson r, accuracy gap, Cohen's κ, and causal intervention-survival) jointly pin down each model's prediction circuit. - **The good:** Readout transplantation — applying one backbone's surrogate rule to another's frozen representations — collapses accuracy by 33–40 pp with bootstrap CIs that exclude zero, providing causal evidence that readout and backbone are co-designed rather than incidentally correlated. - **The missing:** The paper is inference-only on the 12-block TabPFNv2 checkpoint and excludes the newer 24-block v2.5/v2.6 (licensing barrier), all datasets are ≤2000 rows (standard OpenML scale), and the pretraining data generator is held fixed throughout — so which mechanisms are architectural versus data-induced remains open. - **Read it or not:** Essential for anyone building, deploying, or auditing tabular foundation models; also relevant to mechanistic interpretability researchers seeking a case study outside language models with ground-truth causal validation.
Full summary
TL;DR - What it does: This paper delivers a mechanistic audit of three tabular foundation models (TabPFNv2, TabICLv2, Mitra), identifying each model's internal readout algorithm, localizing the architectural components responsible for permutation invariances, and designing mechanism-grounded adversarial attacks that reproduce predicted model-specific failures. - Why it's important: Tabular foundation models reach comparable benchmark accuracy despite radically different architectures, yet that convergence conceals qualitatively distinct internal algorithms with distinct attack surfaces — leaderboard scores give no guidance on which perturbations will break which model or how to harden them. - How it does it: Layer-wise linear probing, per-block causal knockout, cross-backbone readout transplantation, and a four-criteria faithful-surrogate validation framework (Pearson r, accuracy gap, Cohen's κ, and causal intervention-survival) jointly pin down each model's prediction circuit. - The good: Readout transplantation — applying one backbone's surrogate rule to another's frozen representations — collapses accuracy by 33–40 pp with bootstrap CIs that exclude zero, providing causal evidence that readout and backbone are co-designed rather than incidentally correlated. - The missing: The paper is inference-only on the 12-block TabPFNv2 checkpoint and excludes the newer 24-block v2.5/v2.6 (licensing barrier), all datasets are ≤2000 rows (standard OpenML scale), and the pretraining data generator is held fixed throughout — so which mechanisms are architectural versus data-induced remains open. - Read it or not: Essential for anyone building, deploying, or auditing tabular foundation models; also relevant to mechanistic interpretability researchers seeking a case study outside language models with ground-truth causal validation. Problem & Motivation TabPFNv2, TabICLv2, and Mitra each achieve 0.854–0.864 mean accuracy on 49 classification datasets without task-specific training, making predictions entirely via in-context learning. Their architectures differ in token granularity (per-cell vs. per-row vs. label-slot), positional encoding (learned per-feature embedding vs. RoPE vs. none), and label injection strategy. Prior empirical work catalogued their permutation invariances without locating responsible parameters, and traced task-relevant structure into middle layers without identifying the readout mechanism. The central gap is that equal benchmark performance leaves open whether the models implement the same algorithm, which components break which symmetry, and which perturbations will degrade each model specifically — questions with direct implications for deployment robustness and architecture design. Key Idea The unifying insight is that similar accuracy emerges from qualitatively distinct learned algorithms: TabPFNv2 and Mitra concentrate their critical computation into one early frame-setting block and one late block that organizes representations into an attention-weighted vote, while TabICLv2 builds class-clustered geometry before its deep ICL trunk and reads off nearest class prototypes. This co-design between readout and backbone — confirmed causally by transplantation — implies that mechanism-grounded perturbations will attack each model at a different locus, enabling targeted adversarial tests that distinguish mechanism-specific from generic classifier fragility. Methodology All experiments are inference-only on released checkpoints (except one from-scratch TabICLv1 retraining). The analysis proceeds in four phases. (1) Representation localization: per-layer logistic-regression probes (ℓ2, C=1, 80/20 context split), silhouette scores, effective rank/participation ratio/twoNN intrinsic dimension, and per-block MLP knockout with both frozen and retrained probes, on 49 classification and 10 regression datasets over 5–20 seeds. (2) Readout identification: for each model a simple surrogate rule is proposed and validated against four criteria jointly — Pearson r≥0.85, accuracy gap ≤3 pp, Cohen's κ≥0.8, and intervention-survival (e.g., forcing uniform L9 attention must collapse accuracy to the majority baseline); the faithfulness thresholds are sensitivity-tested by jointly varying r and Δacc within ±0.05. (3) Permutation invariance: synthetic invariance verifications, zero-cost surgical edits (W=0 for TabPFNv2, RoPE removal for TabICLv2), and a 100k-step pretraining ablation for TabICLv1 with four RoPE × class-head combinations. (4) Adversarial probing: eight mechanism-grounded attacks (hub poison, rank warp, SVD burial, noise pad, boundary poison, centroid injection, cube warp, soft-exp warp) on a common 24-dataset × 5-seed grid, compared against Ridge, XGBoost, and a tuned 2-hidden-layer MLP baseline refit on the same poisoned context; significance tested with Holm-corrected Wilcoxon signed-rank. An expressiveness barrier is proven formally: any row representation invariant to all column permutations cannot exceed 0.75 accuracy on Tasks A and B at m=3 (Lemma 1, orbit-majority bound), validated on toy M0–M3 single-attention-layer models. Key Results TabPFNv2 probe accuracy stays near chance (0.467 at L8) then jumps to 0.780 at L9 across all 49 datasets; the attention-weighted vote at L9 achieves mean Pearson r=0.89 (binary mean 0.93, degrades to 0.80 at 10 classes); forcing uniform L9 attention collapses accuracy from 0.874 to 0.488 (majority baseline 0.498). TabICLv2 kNN accuracy is already 0.824 at the column-embedding output (end-to-end: 0.864), meaning the 26M-parameter ICL transformer (95% of parameters) contributes only 4 pp; the L11 prototype achieves 0.854 vs. 0.864 native. Mitra (75.67M parameters) peaks at L9 as a late-jump model, with an attention-weighted vote at 0.815 vs. 0.860 native (4.5 pp gap, marginally exceeding the faithful-surrogate threshold). Transplantation drops: prototype on TabPFNv2 −33 pp; vote on TabICLv2 −40 pp. Zero-cost invariance patches: TabPFNv2 W=0 at 0 pp cost; TabICLv2 no-RoPE at −0.2 pp; invariant TabICLv1 pretraining costs −2.6 pp when both levers are combined. Attack results on the common 24-dataset grid (clean baselines ~0.882–0.888): rank warp −8.0 pp [TabPFNv2], −10.1 pp [TabICLv2], −0.4 pp [Mitra, immune via quantile front-end]; hub poison −3.7 pp, −3.3 pp, −2.8 pp (all Holm-significant for TabPFNv2/TabICLv2); SVD burial −8.3 pp, −5.0 pp, −8.9 pp. The MLP refit baseline loses 4.3 pp under rank warp vs. 8–10 pp for the foundation models, confirming mechanism-specificity. Novelty Classification NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING (distinct readout mechanisms underlying similar benchmark performance), NEW METHOD (four-criteria faithful-surrogate validation framework; mechanism-grounded adversarial attacks derived from causal readout analysis), and ENGINEERING OPTIMIZATION (zero-cost surgical invariance patches, One-vs-All class-invariance wrapper). Applying linear probes and causal intervention to transformer internals is established in NLP; the genuine novelty is the systematic causal audit of tabular ICL models, the formal multiset expressiveness bound applied to column-invariant architectures, and the demonstration that mechanisms predict attack vulnerability cross-model. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are rigorous: frozen vs. retrained probe comparisons isolate causal contributions; the four-criterion faithful-surrogate definition requires both correlation and causal intervention-survival, not just prediction matching; readout transplantation provides independent structural validation. Bootstrap CIs are reported throughout; Holm correction applied within attack families; global BH-FDR at q=0.05 confirms all 30 headline rejections survive. Five to twenty seeds depending on experiment. One methodological concern: Mitra's vote rule fails the ≤3 pp threshold (4.5 pp gap) and is still claimed as the nearest surrogate — the paper flags this but the claim is weaker than for the other two models. The TabPFNv2 vote also fails the r≥0.85 threshold for 10-class problems (r=0.71–0.76). No code or trained model weights are publicly released (no URL stated), reducing reproducibility to re-implementation from the detailed appendix specification (~90 tables). Experiments run on 4× H100 NVL GPUs; total compute ~30 GPU-hours for headline runs. Deployability (EMPIRICAL) - Practical: All experiments run on commercial H100 hardware; inference costs 1.0–1.5s per query; the zero-cost W=0 and no-RoPE patches require no retraining and add zero wall-clock or memory overhead. - Efficient: The One-vs-All class-invariance wrapper multiplies inference time by C (≈10s, ≈30 GiB for C=10 on a single H100), which is a real deployment cost; batching 32 queries per context raises throughput to ~18 queries/s for TabPFNv2 before plateauing. - Effective: Causal interventions confirm the mechanisms are genuinely load-bearing: uniform L9 attention collapses TabPFNv2 accuracy by 38 pp; the ColEmb-2 knockout collapses TabICLv2 by 28 pp. The adversarial attack ordering correctly predicts Mitra's immunity to rank warp (due to its quantile front-end) and shared hub-poison vulnerability (due to its retrieval-style readout). - Scalable: Context sizes tested only at n≤1500; the paper explicitly excludes "the very large dataset regime" and does not test the mechanisms at scale. Attention-weighted voting and nearest-prototype classification are inherently context-size-dependent — whether the L9 vote remains the dominant mechanism at n=10,000 is not addressed. The paper delivers inference-time patches and attack recipes in paper form; no software artifact is released. Strengths - The causal validation protocol (four-criteria faithful surrogate + intervention-survival) is substantially more rigorous than behavioral probing alone; the specific combination of uniform-attention forcing (collapses accuracy to majority baseline) and cross-transplantation (33–40 pp drops) provides converging causal evidence, not mere correlation. - The formal multiset expressiveness bound (Lemma 1) is a genuine theoretical contribution that grounds the representation collapse analysis and precisely characterizes when column-invariant architectures cannot exceed 0.75 accuracy — rare in an otherwise empirical paper. - The finding that TabICLv2's 26M-parameter ICL trunk (95% of parameters) adds only 4 pp over the column embedder is a concrete, potentially actionable efficiency finding with implications for architecture redesign. - Mitra serves as a natural controlled experiment: by achieving competitive accuracy without any within-row symmetry-breaker, it empirically proves that representation collapse is an architectural choice, not an inherent property of column-invariant designs. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The pretraining mixture is held fixed, so the paper cannot distinguish which mechanisms are properties of the architecture versus the synthetic data generator. The very large dataset regime (where in-context inference becomes infeasible) is explicitly out of scope. The Mitra vote surrogate marginally fails the ≤3 pp accuracy threshold (4.5 pp gap), and the faithful-surrogate bar is softer for 10-class problems on TabPFNv2. The "redundant positional components" claim is inference-only, meaning the W and RoPE parameters are unused by these weights on this benchmark — not that they would be unnecessary in general training. Analyst-Identified Gaps The paper audits the 12-block TabPFNv2 but excludes the 24-block v2.5/v2.6 due to licensing — practitioners using current TabPFNv2 releases are left without a mechanistic account. The claim that TabICLv2's ICL trunk is largely redundant is not validated by training a stripped-down replacement; this finding is suggestive but unconfirmed. Code is not released, limiting reproducibility for the multi-hundred-table experiment suite. All tested datasets are clean, small-scale (≤2000 rows) OpenML benchmarks — the adversarial vulnerabilities (rank warp causing 8–10 pp drops) may be larger or smaller on noisy, high-dimensional real-world tabular data. The connection between Mitra's balance-scale robustness and its row-attention ablation is described as an inference rather than a direct causal test — these use different datasets and operating regimes. The paper does not examine what happens to the readout mechanisms as context set size grows toward inference limits. Bottom Line This is a methodologically careful and practically actionable mechanistic audit that a practitioner deploying tabular foundation models should read — it delivers zero-cost hardening patches, a precise model-specific attack taxonomy, and concrete architecture design principles, all validated causally rather than merely correlationally; the primary caveat is that the 12-block TabPFNv2 audited here is not the current production checkpoint.

optimize_anything: A Universal API for Optimizing any Text Parameter

arxiv.org · 📄 Open PDF
**TL;DR** - **What it does:** This paper introduces optimize_anything, an open-source declarative API that unifies LLM-based evolutionary search across arbitrary text artifacts (prompts, code, agent architectures, scheduling policies, images) under three modes — single-task, multi-task, and generalization — using Pareto-based search and diagnostic side information. - **Why it's important:** Every prior LLM-evolution system (AlphaEvolve, GEPA, MIPROv2) is locked to one artifact type and one search mode, forcing practitioners to adopt different frameworks for different domains; a single unified interface that matches or beats domain-specific tools would dramatically lower the barrier to applying LLM-based optimization. - **How it does it:** The core mechanism inherits Pareto-frontier candidate selection from GEPA, extends it to arbitrary text artifacts, and elevates diagnostic feedback (compiler errors, profiler traces, per-example subscores, rendered images) to a first-class typed "Side Information" contract that allows the LLM proposer to diagnose *why* a candidate failed rather than only observing a scalar score change. - **The good:** The ARC-AGI experiment is the strongest evidence: starting from a 10-line single-call seed, the system autonomously discovers a 300-line 4-stage pipeline (rule induction, exec-verified code generation, iterative debugging, structured fallback) that raises test accuracy from 32.5% to 89.5% — a qualitative architectural leap that typically requires manual engineering — using only Gemini 3 Flash as both proposer and agent. - **The missing:** All six primary experiments use different frontier LLMs as proposers (Claude Opus 4.6, Gemini 3 Pro/Flash, GPT-5, GPT-5.1), which confounds domain difficulty with proposer capability; no experiment holds the proposer constant across domains to isolate the framework's contribution from the LLM's. - **Read it or not:** Worth reading for anyone building or deploying LLM-based optimization pipelines, especially those needing to extend beyond prompt tuning to agent architectures or code generation, though readers should treat the headline numbers with awareness that frontier model availability is a prerequisite.
Full summary
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces optimize_anything, an open-source declarative API that unifies LLM-based evolutionary search across arbitrary text artifacts (prompts, code, agent architectures, scheduling policies, images) under three modes — single-task, multi-task, and generalization — using Pareto-based search and diagnostic side information. - Why it's important: Every prior LLM-evolution system (AlphaEvolve, GEPA, MIPROv2) is locked to one artifact type and one search mode, forcing practitioners to adopt different frameworks for different domains; a single unified interface that matches or beats domain-specific tools would dramatically lower the barrier to applying LLM-based optimization. - How it does it: The core mechanism inherits Pareto-frontier candidate selection from GEPA, extends it to arbitrary text artifacts, and elevates diagnostic feedback (compiler errors, profiler traces, per-example subscores, rendered images) to a first-class typed "Side Information" contract that allows the LLM proposer to diagnose why a candidate failed rather than only observing a scalar score change. - The good: The ARC-AGI experiment is the strongest evidence: starting from a 10-line single-call seed, the system autonomously discovers a 300-line 4-stage pipeline (rule induction, exec-verified code generation, iterative debugging, structured fallback) that raises test accuracy from 32.5% to 89.5% — a qualitative architectural leap that typically requires manual engineering — using only Gemini 3 Flash as both proposer and agent. - The missing: All six primary experiments use different frontier LLMs as proposers (Claude Opus 4.6, Gemini 3 Pro/Flash, GPT-5, GPT-5.1), which confounds domain difficulty with proposer capability; no experiment holds the proposer constant across domains to isolate the framework's contribution from the LLM's. - Read it or not: Worth reading for anyone building or deploying LLM-based optimization pipelines, especially those needing to extend beyond prompt tuning to agent architectures or code generation, though readers should treat the headline numbers with awareness that frontier model availability is a prerequisite. Problem & Motivation: Existing LLM-based optimization frameworks are siloed by artifact type and search mode. AlphaEvolve and its derivatives (OpenEvolve, ShinkaEvolve) optimize code artifacts in single-task mode. GEPA and MIPROv2 handle prompt generalization but cannot optimize agent code or scheduling policies. Practitioners who need to optimize, say, both a CUDA kernel and an agent architecture must maintain separate frameworks with incompatible abstractions (island topologies, EVOLVE-BLOCK markers, mutation templates). There is also no existing framework that supports multi-task optimization — exploiting cross-problem transfer when solving a batch of related optimization problems simultaneously. The gap is the absence of a domain-neutral interface that treats the artifact, evaluator, and diagnostic feedback as the only required inputs, letting the optimization algorithm remain unchanged across domains. Key Idea: The core insight is that any optimization problem whose solution can be serialized as a string and evaluated by a scoring function can be addressed by the same LLM-based evolutionary search loop. The non-trivial technical move is elevating diagnostic feedback (Side Information, SI) to a first-class typed evaluator contract — effectively treating SI as the text-space analogue of a gradient — so that the LLM proposer can perform targeted algorithmic shifts rather than blind mutations. Combined with Pareto-frontier tracking across per-example or per-metric dimensions (preserving candidates that are best at something even if their average is suboptimal), this produces convergent search across fundamentally different artifact types. Methodology: The system wraps the GEPA optimization backend with five extensions: (1) new frontier types for single-task mode (where no dataset exists and Pareto tracking reduces to per-metric tracking) and multi-task mode (a shared frontier across N tasks that enables cross-transfer while each task retains its own best artifact); (2) a pre-evaluation refiner step that sanitizes LLM outputs (malformed code blocks, import errors) before costly rollouts; (3) content-addressed evaluation caching; (4) SI as a typed primitive (text, structured sub-scores, images via oa.Image for VLM proposers); and (5) a backend adapter layer for future optimization algorithms. The core loop: select a candidate from the Pareto front proportional to how many objectives it dominates; execute it on a minibatch of 2–3 examples; present scores and SI to the proposer LLM in a structured reflection prompt; if the proposed artifact improves on the minibatch, evaluate fully and add to the candidate pool; prune dominated candidates. The three modes differ only in whether `dataset` and `valset` are provided at the API call. A seedless mode bootstraps the first candidate from a natural-language objective. Critical design decisions: (a) minibatch reflection rather than full-dataset reflection — justified by enabling targeted improvements and avoiding proposer context overload; (b) Pareto selection rather than top-k by average — justified by preserving complementary strengths; (c) SI as opt-in but zero-friction (capture_stdio=True requires no evaluator changes) — justified by practical adoption concerns. Key Results: All results are from a peer-reviewed paper (CAIS '26). Agent skills (Bleve repository): Haiku 4.5 pass rate 79.3% → 98.3%, Sonnet 4.5 94.8% → 100%, resolution time cut 47%, using Claude Opus 4.6 as proposer ($50 total). Cloud scheduling (ADRS benchmark): CloudCast achieves 40.2% cost savings over Dijkstra routing; Can't Be Late achieves 7.8% savings; optimize_anything scores 96.6 aggregate on the ADRS leaderboard vs. OpenEvolve 92.9 and ShinkaEvolve 72.0, using Gemini 3 Pro. ARC-AGI: test accuracy 32.5% → 89.5% (+57pp), validation 93.5%, using Gemini 3 Flash as both proposer and agent ($144.70 total). AIME 2025: GPT-4.1-mini 46.67% → 60.0% (+13.3pp) using GPT-5 as proposer, outperforming MIPROv2 (51.33%). CUDA kernels (KernelBench, 31 problems, V100 32GB): 87% match or beat PyTorch baseline, 48% achieve ≥10% speedup, 25% achieve ≥20% speedup ($140 total, ~$4.51/kernel), using GPT-5. Multi-task CUDA outperforms single-task at all speedup thresholds; MT20 outperforms MT10 which outperforms single-task. Circle packing (n=26): sum_radii 2.63598 in 63 evaluations ($3.18), vs. OpenEvolve 2.4583 at 100 evaluations and 2.6307 at 200 evaluations ($6.85), both using GPT-5.1 — a controlled rerun under matched conditions. SI ablation: 4–6× faster convergence and 86.32 vs. 82.5 final test score on Facility Support Analysis; for CUDA multi-task with SI, 40% of kernels exceed 1.1× speedup vs. 0% without SI. Proposer sensitivity: GPT-5-nano reduces AIME gain to 46.67% → 50.0% vs. 60.0% for GPT-5.1, at ~40% lower cost. Novelty Classification: NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The framework itself is a genuine systems contribution: the multi-task search mode is genuinely new to LLM-evolution, SI as a first-class typed API contract is a design novelty, and the declarative interface without mutation templates or island configurations is a meaningful simplification. The empirical finding that a single system matches or beats domain-specific tools across six diverse domains is non-trivial and directly contradicts the prevailing assumption that LLM-evolution requires domain-specific scaffolding. However, the core optimization algorithm (Pareto-based reflective mutation) is inherited directly from GEPA [3]; the novelty is in generalization and unification, not in a fundamentally new optimization primitive. Rigor & Reproducibility: Reproducibility is strong: code is open-sourced at github.com/gepa-ai/gepa, a full reproduction artifact with runnable code and saved GEPAState checkpoints is at github.com/gepa-ai/optimize-anything-artifact, and hardware requirements are documented. The circle packing comparison with OpenEvolve is commendably done under controlled, matched conditions (same proposer LLM, GPT-5.1). Ablations are present for multi-task vs. single-task (§5.8), SI vs. score-only (§5.9), and proposer sensitivity (§5.10). However, no error bars or multiple-seed runs are reported for any experiment — the ARC-AGI run costs $144.70 per run, making replication expensive but not impossible. The use of different proposer LLMs across experiments (Gemini 3 Flash for ARC-AGI, GPT-5 for CUDA/AIME/circle packing, Gemini 3 Pro for cloud scheduling, Claude Opus 4.6 for agent skills) makes cross-domain performance comparisons unclean. The authors disclose that generative AI was used to write sections of the paper including text, tables, and graphs (Appendix A), noting that experiment design was the authors' original work — this disclosure is appropriate but unusual and warrants reader attention. The CUDA evaluation covers only 31 of KernelBench's full problem set. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: SYSTEMS — an open-source tool evaluated by deployment across real benchmarks. - Practical: Most experiments run on a single CPU host with API access to frontier LLMs; CUDA kernel generation requires an NVIDIA V100 32GB GPU (a commercially available but non-commodity machine). - Efficient: Optimization costs range from $1 (numerical blackbox) to $144.70 (ARC-AGI), with total spend dominated by evaluator cost, not reflection; at $4.51/kernel for CUDA this is tractable for production use but not cheap, and the system requires frontier LLMs (GPT-5, Gemini 3 Pro) to achieve the headline results. - Effective: The evidence is strong that the system solves the stated optimization objectives across diverse domains, with the SI and multi-task ablations confirming which components drive improvements rather than relying on confounded aggregate performance. - Scalable: Multi-task benefits scale with task count (MT20 > MT10 > single-task for CUDA kernels); the ARC-AGI experiment used 200 tasks on the Pareto front without bottleneck. No experiments test beyond the evaluated problem sizes or examine whether optimization cost scales gracefully with artifact complexity — the circle packing n=26 result does not test n=100 or n=1000. The system delivers a software artifact (open-source Python library with multiple backends). Strengths: - The multi-task search mode with cross-problem transfer via a shared Pareto frontier is a genuine and principled contribution: the mechanism (frontier preserves patterns that help multiple tasks, enabling knowledge transfer without explicit cross-task prompting) is well-motivated and confirmed by ablation. - The SI-as-gradient analogy is insightful and backed by a rigorous ablation showing 4–6× convergence speedup and a 40% vs. 0% kernel speedup threshold in the multi-task setting — the strongest causal claim in the paper is well-supported. - The ARC-AGI architecture discovery result demonstrates something qualitatively new: the system autonomously discovers a multi-stage verify-then-fallback pipeline with exec()-based verification, not just parameter tuning, starting from 10 lines. - The controlled comparison with OpenEvolve on circle packing (matched proposer, reproducible conditions) addresses a legitimate concern about published-result comparisons and is methodologically honest. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors clearly identify: (1) proposal quality depends on proposer LLM capability — weaker models produce weaker results; (2) evaluator costs can be high ($144 for ARC-AGI); (3) artifacts must be representable as text — continuous or binary parameters need a text proxy; (4) multi-task transfer degrades when tasks lack shared structure (demonstrated on circle packing for different N); (5) designing effective SI still requires domain expertise even though the API makes it zero-friction to surface diagnostics. The authors frame limitation (5) as a tradeoff: the system trades optimization expertise for domain expertise, which is a defensible position. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - The ARC-AGI experiment uses the same frontier model (Gemini 3 Flash) as both the optimization proposer and the agent being optimized, which means the evolved architecture may be overfit to Gemini 3 Flash's specific strengths and failure modes. Whether the discovered architecture transfers to a different agent model (e.g., GPT-4o or Claude Sonnet) is untested — this is the most important unevaluated claim given that the agent skills experiment explicitly demonstrates cross-model transfer as a positive property. - The headline CUDA result (87% match or beat) covers only 31 problems from KernelBench; performance on the full benchmark, including harder and more diverse kernels, is unknown. - No experiment controls for proposer LLM identity across domains, making it impossible to determine how much of the per-domain performance gain comes from the framework vs. the choice of a stronger proposer model for that domain. - The image generation evaluation (§5.7) relies on 5 human evaluators across 4 tasks — a statistically thin evaluation that supports qualitative rather than quantitative claims. - The circle packing n=26 result outperforms AlphaEvolve's reported solution; since AlphaEvolve used Gemini models and optimize_anything used GPT-5.1, the controlled comparison with OpenEvolve (same proposer) is appropriate but still leaves open whether the Pareto-based search or simply GPT-5.1's stronger reasoning is responsible for the improvement. Bottom Line: Any practitioner building LLM-based optimization pipelines — especially for agent architecture search, prompt engineering, or code generation — should read this paper; the multi-task transfer mechanism and SI-as-gradient design are immediately actionable ideas backed by solid ablations, even if the headline numbers are entangled with frontier LLM capability.

LLM-COGNICA: Towards Reliable Agent Decision Making

nexus.telecom-paris.fr · 📄 Open PDF
**TL;DR** - **What it does:** This paper introduces LLM-COGNICA, a neuro-symbolic framework that routes agent decision-making through a preference-based argumentation reasoner (COGNICA) using a 7-template Controlled Natural Language (CNL) as the human-readable intermediate between free-text policies and formal inference. - **Why it's important:** LLM-only agents fail to handle ambiguous or incomplete decision scenarios reliably — they produce definite answers when a dilemma response is appropriate, which is unacceptable for normative agent policies in safety-critical domains such as clinical decision support. - **How it does it:** An LLM (o4-mini) translates free-text policy into COGNICA CNL via a structured 5-step prompt; the Gorgias preference-based argumentation engine performs formal inference; a second LLM pass paraphrases the structured explanation into natural language, with three evaluation channels (syntax verification, semantic similarity, formal analysis) feeding back to the human developer. - **The good:** o3-mini answers "Maybe" to ambiguous query scenarios only 40–41% of the time regardless of reasoning effort, versus COGNICA's 100% rate, providing concrete quantitative motivation for the hybrid architecture. - **The missing:** No empirical comparison against any competing hybrid system (LogicLM, VERUSLM, ArgLLMs) on a shared benchmark, and all 550 evaluation policies are LLM-generated rather than drawn from real-world normative sources, creating a circularity concern. - **Read it or not:** Worth reading for researchers on neuro-symbolic agentic AI or computational argumentation who want a concrete deployed system with a novel CNL-intermediary design; practitioners needing validated downstream decision accuracy or competitive benchmarks will find it premature.
Full summary
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces LLM-COGNICA, a neuro-symbolic framework that routes agent decision-making through a preference-based argumentation reasoner (COGNICA) using a 7-template Controlled Natural Language (CNL) as the human-readable intermediate between free-text policies and formal inference. - Why it's important: LLM-only agents fail to handle ambiguous or incomplete decision scenarios reliably — they produce definite answers when a dilemma response is appropriate, which is unacceptable for normative agent policies in safety-critical domains such as clinical decision support. - How it does it: An LLM (o4-mini) translates free-text policy into COGNICA CNL via a structured 5-step prompt; the Gorgias preference-based argumentation engine performs formal inference; a second LLM pass paraphrases the structured explanation into natural language, with three evaluation channels (syntax verification, semantic similarity, formal analysis) feeding back to the human developer. - The good: o3-mini answers "Maybe" to ambiguous query scenarios only 40–41% of the time regardless of reasoning effort, versus COGNICA's 100% rate, providing concrete quantitative motivation for the hybrid architecture. - The missing: No empirical comparison against any competing hybrid system (LogicLM, VERUSLM, ArgLLMs) on a shared benchmark, and all 550 evaluation policies are LLM-generated rather than drawn from real-world normative sources, creating a circularity concern. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers on neuro-symbolic agentic AI or computational argumentation who want a concrete deployed system with a novel CNL-intermediary design; practitioners needing validated downstream decision accuracy or competitive benchmarks will find it premature. Problem & Motivation LLM-based agentic systems rely on the model's implicit reasoning to enforce decision policies, but this offers no formal guarantees, is opaque, and handles ambiguity and incomplete information poorly — defaulting to definite answers when a dilemma or conditional response is appropriate. The problem is acute in any domain requiring normative policy compliance with explainability requirements: medical guidelines, access control, multi-agent negotiation protocols. Prior neuro-symbolic approaches translate natural language directly into procedural code (Python) or declarative programs (ASP), losing human interpretability in the intermediate artifact and making policy validation inaccessible to domain experts. The gap is a verifiable, human-auditable path from natural-language policy to explainable formal decision. Key Idea The core technical insight is to use a Controlled Natural Language (COGNICA CNL) as a human-readable intermediate layer between free-text policies and a formal argumentation reasoner, so that LLM translation errors and policy ambiguities surface at a level a domain expert can read and correct — without requiring knowledge of Prolog or argumentation theory — while the formal reasoner provides absolute correctness guarantees relative to the validated CNL translation. Methodology The pipeline has three stages. First, an LLM (OpenAI o4-mini at medium reasoning effort) translates a free-text decision policy into COGNICA CNL using a 5-step prompt algorithm: identify supported conclusions, identify supporting conditions, select among 7 canonical templates (NORMALLY, IF–THEN, IF–THEN ALWAYS, IF–THEN MAYBE, IF–THEN–UNLESS–IN SUCH CASES, ONLY IF–THEN MAYBE, ONLY IF–THEN–UNLESS–MAYBE), handle conjunctions by sentence splitting, and normalize negations. A syntax-verification pass uses a second LLM call to fix any template violations. Second, the COGNICA argumentation reasoner (Gorgias, implemented in SWI-Prolog) executes preference-based argumentation: conditionals become argument schemes ASuff/ANecc/ANPoss with a canonical preference ordering (ANecc ≻ ASuff ≻ APoss), and conclusions are drawn when an acceptable argument set — one that defends against all counterarguments — can be constructed; unresolvable conflicts yield a "Maybe" (dilemma) response with structured explanation. Third, an LLM paraphrases the structured argumentation-derived explanation into natural language. Three evaluation channels feed back to the developer: syntax verification (template compliance), semantic similarity (SBERT/USE cosine similarity), and formal analysis (argumentation engine detects dilemmas, incoherence, and redundancy). Critical design decisions: (a) CNL rather than ASP/Python as the translation target is well-justified by interpretability requirements; (b) preference-based argumentation for conflict resolution is appropriate given its connection to human conditional reasoning; (c) human-in-the-loop validation is reasonable for safety-critical domains but introduces an unquantified labor cost. Key Results The evaluation uses 550 policies generated by 10 LLMs (DeepSeek V3, GPT-4o, GPT-4 Turbo, Gemini 2.0 Flash, Grok3, and others), 56% consisting of 2–3 sentences, 86% with 2–3 decision options. For reasoning comparison, 80 ambiguous query scenarios were executed on o3-mini at medium and high reasoning effort and compared to COGNICA. COGNICA answered "Maybe" to all 80 ambiguous scenarios (100%); o3-mini at medium effort answered "Maybe" 40% of the time, and at high effort 41% — a negligible gain from increased compute. For translation quality, 203/550 policies (37%) required manual modification after author review. Modification rate scales with policy length: 26% for 1–2 sentence policies, 43% for 3–4, 79% for 5–6, 100% for 7–10 sentence policies. SBERT and USE semantic similarity scores are weak indicators of translation quality: the ~35–40% modification rate is uniform across all similarity score bands. The hardest reasoning form is "Sufficient & Strong Modality": 91% of such policies needed modification because the LLM fails to recognize implicit strength when linguistic markers ("always", "never") are absent from the original text. A clinical decision support system for Schwannomatosis was built and is described as positively received by medical practitioners; no quantitative clinical outcome is reported. No throughput, latency, or cost numbers are reported anywhere in the paper. Novelty Classification NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW METHOD. The contribution is a deployed agentic framework combining LLM translation with COGNICA CNL as a human-readable intermediate and preference-based argumentation as the inference engine. The individual components — preference-based argumentation (Gorgias), LLM-to-formal-language translation, COGNICA — are prior work by the same group; the novel integration is the "language-to-language" CNL intermediate with its closed-loop developer evaluation mechanism. Rigor & Reproducibility No ablations isolating individual pipeline components (e.g., effect of the syntax-verification pass, or choice of translation LLM). No error bars, multiple runs, or significance tests — all counts are single-run. The evaluation dataset is LLM-generated, not drawn from real-world normative policy corpora, raising circularity: translation and policy-generating LLMs share distributional biases. Manual review of 550 translations is author-conducted with no inter-annotator agreement reported, making the 37% modification figure non-reproducible. The dataset is available "upon request" — not publicly released. The web application is live (cognica.cs.ucy.ac.cy) but the source code and Gorgias rules are not described as open-source. The paper appears in a workshop proceedings (AAMAS NEXUS workshop), not a peer-reviewed main-track venue; results should be treated with correspondingly lower confidence. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: SYSTEMS — a built web application and argumentation engine evaluated by deployment. - Practical: The system runs via a web application backed by SWI-Prolog (Gorgias) and the OpenAI API, requiring no specialized hardware. - Efficient: No latency, throughput, or API-call-count data is provided; each policy translation requires at least two LLM calls (translation + optional syntax repair), but cost is uncharacterized. - Effective: The Schwannomatosis clinical assistant is reported as "positively received," but no quantitative decision accuracy, error rate, or user-study metric is given — this is anecdotal evidence, not validation. - Scalable: Only policies of up to 10 sentences are tested; no evaluation of performance with large policy bases, high query volumes, or complex multi-module normative systems. Scalability is claimed but not tested. The system delivers a working web application. The CNL translation and evaluation pipeline are the new artifacts; Gorgias is pre-existing infrastructure. Strengths - The CNL-as-intermediate-layer design is a genuinely useful engineering insight: it makes the critical translation artifact auditable by domain experts without formal methods expertise, a real advance over ASP- or Python-target approaches. - The quantitative demonstration that o3-mini handles ambiguous queries correctly only ~40% of the time versus COGNICA's 100% is crisp and directly motivates the hybrid architecture. - The three-channel closed-loop evaluation mechanism (syntax, semantic, formal) is a principled and practical approach to iterative policy development in a deployed system. - Real-world deployment in Schwannomatosis clinical decision support demonstrates the system has been exercised beyond synthetic benchmarks. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly acknowledge that the LLM translator fails on implicit strong modality — when "ALWAYS" strength is signaled by discourse context rather than explicit keywords, the 91% modification rate is reported and attributed. They commit to fine-tuning an LLM on hard-case policies as future work. They also acknowledge that human developer review remains necessary and that the process of specifying policies in natural language in a CNL-compatible way requires learning. These are confronted directly and credibly. Analyst-Identified Gaps - Circular evaluation dataset: All 550 policies are generated by the same LLM family used for translation; human-authored policies from actual regulatory, medical, or legal sources would provide far more diagnostic signal. - No competing baselines: The paper cites LogicLM, VERUSLM, and ArgLLMs as closely related hybrid systems but provides zero empirical comparison against any of them; there is no way to assess whether LLM-COGNICA performs better or worse than alternatives. - Downstream decision accuracy unmeasured: The system's actual value is whether it makes correct decisions; no experiment measures decision accuracy on held-out scenarios with ground-truth answers. - Manual review subjectivity: The 37% modification rate is assessed by the authors without inter-annotator agreement, making this the paper's central empirical metric unreproducible. - CNL expressiveness not bounded: The paper does not characterize what fraction of real-world policy complexity falls outside COGNICA's 7-template vocabulary, or how the system degrades when the CNL cannot express a construct. - No latency or cost analysis: For a deployed system making multiple API calls per policy, omitting operational cost data is a significant gap for practitioners. Bottom Line A practitioner building explainable, normative agentic decision systems will find the CNL-as-intermediate-layer design and the reasoning-gap quantification genuinely useful, but the workshop-paper depth of evaluation — LLM-generated policies, no competing baselines, no downstream accuracy measure — means this is a well-motivated early-stage system report that requires substantially more rigorous evaluation before its claims can be accepted.

KernelBenchX: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Evaluating LLM-Generated GPU Kernels

arxiv.org · 📄 Open PDF
