**TL;DR** - **What it does:** This paper introduces KernelBenchX, a 176-task, 15-category benchmark for evaluating LLM-generated Triton GPU kernels on both semantic correctness and hardware efficiency, tested across five representative methods on six NVIDIA GPUs. - **Why it's important:** The capability boundary of LLM-based kernel generation is practically unknown — developers cannot predict which task types are reliably solvable versus systematically broken, making deployment decisions guesswork. - **How it does it:** Category-aware evaluation with a two-stage correctness protocol (compilation check plus outlier-injected execution accuracy) and hardware-efficiency metrics (IOU/MFU normalized utilization), unified across all methods on shared hardware. - **The good:** The finding that task category explains 9.4% of deviance in semantic correctness versus only 3.3% for method identity — with category adding 34.6% deviance reduction on top of method but method adding only 13.0% on top of category — is a crisp, falsifiable result that reframes the entire evaluation paradigm. - **The missing:** No confidence intervals are reported on per-category correctness rates despite several categories having only 2–3 tasks, making the fine-grained category claims statistically fragile. - **Read it or not:** ML systems researchers and practitioners building kernel generation pipelines should read it for the structured diagnosis of failure modes; the correctness-performance dissociation finding alone is worth the read.
Full summary
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces KernelBenchX, a 176-task, 15-category benchmark for evaluating LLM-generated Triton GPU kernels on both semantic correctness and hardware efficiency, tested across five representative methods on six NVIDIA GPUs. - Why it's important: The capability boundary of LLM-based kernel generation is practically unknown — developers cannot predict which task types are reliably solvable versus systematically broken, making deployment decisions guesswork. - How it does it: Category-aware evaluation with a two-stage correctness protocol (compilation check plus outlier-injected execution accuracy) and hardware-efficiency metrics (IOU/MFU normalized utilization), unified across all methods on shared hardware. - The good: The finding that task category explains 9.4% of deviance in semantic correctness versus only 3.3% for method identity — with category adding 34.6% deviance reduction on top of method but method adding only 13.0% on top of category — is a crisp, falsifiable result that reframes the entire evaluation paradigm. - The missing: No confidence intervals are reported on per-category correctness rates despite several categories having only 2–3 tasks, making the fine-grained category claims statistically fragile. - Read it or not: ML systems researchers and practitioners building kernel generation pipelines should read it for the structured diagnosis of failure modes; the correctness-performance dissociation finding alone is worth the read. Problem & Motivation LLM-based Triton GPU kernel generation has attracted significant tooling investment (AutoTriton, GEAK, KernelAgent, KernelEvolve, STARK), but the field lacks a diagnostic framework to identify where these methods reliably succeed and systematically fail. Prior benchmarks (KernelBench, TritonBench, MultiKernelBench, Robust-KBench) aggregate metrics across heterogeneous tasks without category structure, use insufficient correctness protocols that can be passed by chance, and largely ignore hardware efficiency beyond raw runtime. Without category-structured analysis, it is impossible to answer: which task types exceed current capability, does iterative refinement improve correctness or performance, and do correct kernels actually achieve hardware efficiency? GPU kernel efficiency has become a central bottleneck in large-scale ML (motivated by DeepSeek-V3 and FlashAttention-3 scaling results), amplifying the cost of this diagnostic gap. Key Idea The core technical insight is that correctness failure in LLM-generated Triton kernels is structurally governed by task category rather than method sophistication — specifically, tasks requiring non-local semantic coordination across parallel program instances (Fusion, Quantization, SpatialOps) form a distinct correctness barrier that no current method crosses reliably, while tasks with lane-local data dependence (Math, Activation) are solved consistently. This reframes the failure mode from "LLMs need better prompting or more iterations" to "LLMs lack global-contract reasoning," with the key supporting mechanism being that locally correct Triton idioms can compose into globally incorrect kernels (e.g., padding zeros before exponentiation contributing exp(0)=1 to a sum that should exclude masked elements). Methodology KernelBenchX contains 176 tasks across 15 categories organized by the type of knowledge required for correct implementation (not operator type): Direct specification tasks (Activation, Math), Parallel aggregation structures (Reduce, Pooling, Normalization), Structured multi-operand computation (MatrixMultiply, LinearAlgebra), Indexed/spatial computation (Convolution, Index, SpatialOps), Compositional kernels (Fusion, 60 tasks), and Semantic contract categories (Loss, Optimizer, Random, Quantization). Six quantization tasks require explicit manual quantization logic (scale computation, explicit casting, dequantization) with high-level API use statically rejected. The evaluation pipeline runs five methods in parallel — AutoTriton (RL-finetuned), GEAK (agentic iterative system, 3 rounds, DeepSeek-V3.2-Chat), KernelAgent (generate-verify-refine, DeepSeek-V3.2-Chat, 5 refinement rounds), Claude (single-pass), and DeepSeek-Coder (single-pass baseline) — through a shared evaluation harness. The two-stage correctness protocol applies: (1) Call Accuracy — compilation, AST-based kernel-entry check, constraint satisfaction, and static quantization API checker; (2) Execution Accuracy — output comparison under standard N(0,1) inputs AND an outlier mode injecting amplified outliers (0.1% probability, scale factor 50) to expose implementations that pass on typical inputs but fail under distributional shift. Quantization tasks require simultaneous cosine similarity ≥ 0.90–0.95, L1 relative error ≤ 0.05–0.10, and RMSE ≤ 0.10–0.15. Hardware efficiency is measured as max(IOU, MFU) against the dominant bottleneck. Performance is measured with triton.testing.do_bench (25 warmup, 100 runs, median) on six GPUs (RTX 5090, RTX 4090, A100-PCIE-40GB, H20, H800 PCIe, L20). Variance attribution uses task-level logistic models with Pearson correlation of binary outcomes against method and category indicators. Iterative behavior is analyzed from 352 adjacent GEAK diffs classified by edit type. Three design decisions: (1) Two-stage correctness with outlier injection is well-justified — it directly addresses the pass-by-chance weakness. (2) Category taxonomy by knowledge type rather than operator type is reasonable but subjective — assignment criteria are stated but not independently validated. (3) The outlier injection parameters (0.1% probability, scale factor 50) appear chosen without ablation. Key Results Overall correctness rates across 176 tasks: GEAK 30.7%, Claude 22.7%, AutoTriton 17.0%, KernelAgent 10.8%, KernelLLM 0.0%. Compile-to-correct conversion rates reveal a large gap: KernelAgent compiles 64.2% but achieves only 10.8% correctness (16.8% conversion); AutoTriton achieves 46.9% conversion. Category explains 9.4% of deviance in semantic correctness versus 3.3% for method identity. 72% of Fusion tasks fail across all five methods; Math tasks are solved at 40%+ across methods. Quantization achieves 0/30 successes (zero across all 6 tasks × 5 methods) despite non-trivial compilation rates (up to 83.3% for KernelAgent). SpatialOps: 0% correctness for all methods. Static complexity proxies correlate only weakly with correctness failure (cyclomatic complexity r=0.145, intermediate assignment count r=0.211), confirming failures represent semantic rather than syntactic limits. GEAK iterative refinement: compile rate rises 52.3%→68.8% and correctness 18.2%→30.7% over three rounds, but average speedup falls 1.58×→1.44× and score 62.7%→53.3%. Newly rescued kernels in round 0→1 average 1.16× speedup (score 43.7%) versus 1.58× (score 62.9%) for originally correct kernels; the gap persists in round 1→2 (1.32× vs. 1.46×). Edit-type analysis of 352 GEAK diffs: 102 no-substantial-change edits, 101 mask fixes, 65 delegated-op introductions/removals, 36 dtype/casting fixes — performance-oriented rewrites are rare. Among semantically correct kernels, 46.6% are slower than PyTorch eager baseline; pooled median speedup is only 1.0008×. Cross-hardware portability: max/min speedup ratio has median 2.15×, mean 2.73×, worst case 21.4×. Fraction slower than PyTorch ranges from 18% on A100 to 76% on L20. Novelty Classification NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The benchmark extends TritonBench-T with a unified category taxonomy, stricter two-stage correctness protocol, and hardware-efficiency metrics. The empirical findings — category-structured correctness variance, repair-biased iterative refinement, correctness-performance dissociation — are genuinely novel characterizations of LLM kernel generation limits that prior benchmarks lacked the structure to reveal. The mechanistic analysis linking iterative refinement's performance degradation to asymmetric feedback signal (local error vs. non-local performance) is a new conceptual contribution, not merely a quantitative measurement. Rigor & Reproducibility Code is publicly released at https://github.com/BonnieW05/KernelBenchX. The correctness protocol is precisely specified including quantization thresholds. Variance attribution uses Pearson correlation with logistic regression — defensible but not the most powerful test for binary outcomes. No error bars or confidence intervals are reported anywhere in the paper despite small sample sizes for several categories (Convolution: 2 tasks, Pooling: 2 tasks, SpatialOps: 3 tasks — per-method correctness rates in these categories are near-meaningless statistically). The edit-type classification of 352 GEAK diffs is presented without inter-rater reliability. FLOP- and byte-based efficiency metrics are derived from an idealized task-level model, not measured instruction execution — explicitly flagged as proxies, which is the correct disclosure. KernelLLM achieves 0% correctness and 1.7% compilation across all categories; this anomalous result is unexplained and may reflect a misconfigured adapter rather than genuine model capability — if so, it would contaminate the variance attribution analysis. The benchmark was developed in part by the same group that built GEAK, which receives the deepest mechanistic analysis; the evaluation appears methodologically uniform, but this relationship warrants noting. This is a preprint; results carry preprint-level confidence. Deployability Profile: EMPIRICAL — benchmark suite with experimental evaluation across five methods and six hardware platforms. - Practical: Runs on commercially available NVIDIA GPUs (RTX 4090/5090, A100, H20, H800, L20) under a standard Python/PyTorch/Triton stack (Python 3.11, CUDA 11.8, PyTorch 2.10.0+cu128, Triton 3.6.0). - Efficient: Evaluating five methods × 176 tasks × six GPU platforms is non-trivial compute; total evaluation cost is not stated, but the framework is batch-parallelizable and practical for research labs with multi-GPU access. - Effective: The two-stage correctness protocol and outlier injection directly address the proxy-measure weaknesses of prior benchmarks; hardware efficiency via IOU/MFU measures utilization against theoretical peak rather than against a single baseline, making the metric interpretable across hardware. - Scalable: At 176 tasks KernelBenchX is relatively small; several categories have 2–3 tasks, making category-level findings fragile. The paper does not address generalization to larger or more diverse task distributions, and the category boundary hypothesis is not tested beyond the current task set. The work delivers a software benchmark suite plus iteration-level transition pairs (error→patch→pass chains) as training artifacts for future repair and optimization models. Strengths - The category-structured variance attribution (9.4% vs. 3.3% explained deviance for category vs. method) is quantitatively precise and changes the actionable conclusion: improving method design is the wrong lever; the bottleneck is task-structural reasoning capability. - The correctness-performance dissociation (46.6% of correct kernels slower than PyTorch, median speedup 1.0008×) quantifies a practically critical failure mode — correct kernels being slower than the baseline they are meant to replace — that was suspected but uncharacterized before this work. - The mechanistic account of repair-biased iterative refinement (edit distribution dominated by mask fixes and dtype casts, performance rewrites rare; asymmetric feedback structure prevents performance-signal propagation) is structurally sound and grounded in the 352-diff analysis rather than speculation. - The two-stage correctness protocol with outlier injection is a concrete methodological improvement over prior benchmarks and is reusable for future evaluations beyond KernelBenchX. Author-Acknowledged Limitations Hardware efficiency quantities (IOU, MFU) are derived from an idealized task-level model rather than measured executed instructions, making them normalized proxies rather than direct measurements — explicitly disclosed. Performance statistics are reported only over semantically correct kernels, introducing potential survivorship bias. The benchmark focuses on single-operator Triton kernels; multi-operator pipeline evaluation is out of scope. The authors note that KernelLLM results are anomalous but do not investigate the root cause. Analyst-Identified Gaps - No confidence intervals on any correctness rate estimates, despite categories with 2–3 tasks producing point estimates with extremely high variance (e.g., Convolution with 2 tasks: one success or failure changes the rate by 50 percentage points). - KernelLLM's 0% correctness / 1.7% compilation is never explained or investigated; if it reflects a misconfigured adapter rather than the model's actual capability, its presence inflates the category-over-method variance attribution by adding a method that fails uniformly. - The paper's authors have direct involvement in GEAK development (Wang et al. 2025 is cited as GEAK), and GEAK receives qualitatively deeper analysis (iteration trajectories, 352 diff analysis, three case studies) compared to single-pass methods. This is methodologically reasonable given GEAK's iterative nature but creates asymmetric depth. - Prompt sensitivity for single-pass methods (Claude, DeepSeek-Coder) is not evaluated; single-pass results may be highly sensitive to prompt formulation, and the reported rates may not represent these models' best-case performance. - The quantization 0% result is interpreted as "systematic misunderstanding of numerical computation contracts," but the root cause is not isolated — it could equally reflect insufficient training exposure, benchmark specification ambiguity, or the static API-rejection checker over-triggering. No qualitative error analysis for quantization failures is provided. - The benchmark's task taxonomy is described as organized by "type of knowledge required," but the Fusion category at 60/176 tasks (34%) dominates the dataset; findings about Fusion heavily influence overall conclusions. Bottom Line Practitioners building or evaluating LLM-based kernel generation pipelines should read this paper for the category-structured correctness diagnosis and the correctness-performance dissociation finding, both of which are practically significant and provide a more useful diagnostic framework than aggregate benchmark scores, despite the statistical roughness from small per-category sample sizes.

Mid-Training with Self-Generated Data Improves Reinforcement Learning in Language Models

arxiv.org · 📄 Open PDF
**TL;DR** - **What it does:** This paper introduces a heuristic-guided mid-training framework that fine-tunes LLMs on self-generated, diverse correct solution trajectories — conditioned on Pólya's problem-solving heuristics — before RL training, improving subsequent GRPO-based RL across mathematical reasoning benchmarks. - **Why it's important:** RL with verifiable rewards primarily amplifies behaviors already present in the base model rather than inducing new ones, so models lacking diverse solution priors see limited gains; this approach addresses that bottleneck without distillation from a stronger teacher. - **How it does it:** For each training question the base model generates up to 128 heuristic-conditioned candidates, filters for correctness via Math-Verify, selects the highest-reward correct response per heuristic, then fine-tunes on the resulting multi-solution dataset before GRPO — creating N-modal next-token distributions that policy-gradient updates traverse more conservatively and productively. - **The good:** At pass@64, mid-trained models (n=64 heuristics) improve average accuracy from 46.30% (vanilla RL) to 48.17% across six benchmarks, with larger absolute gains on harder tasks (AIME 2025: 12.84%→18.66%, AMC 2023: 83.49%→85.18%). - **The missing:** Experiments are limited to Llama 3.2–3B and Qwen2.5–7B; no evidence at 70B+ scale, and it is unclear whether the benefit persists when the base model already has substantial implicit diversity. - **Read it or not:** Worth reading for researchers building RL post-training pipelines for small-to-medium reasoning models, especially those where pass@k at k>1 matters; those targeting pass@1 or using large models gain less.
Full summary
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces a heuristic-guided mid-training framework that fine-tunes LLMs on self-generated, diverse correct solution trajectories — conditioned on Pólya's problem-solving heuristics — before RL training, improving subsequent GRPO-based RL across mathematical reasoning benchmarks. - Why it's important: RL with verifiable rewards primarily amplifies behaviors already present in the base model rather than inducing new ones, so models lacking diverse solution priors see limited gains; this approach addresses that bottleneck without distillation from a stronger teacher. - How it does it: For each training question the base model generates up to 128 heuristic-conditioned candidates, filters for correctness via Math-Verify, selects the highest-reward correct response per heuristic, then fine-tunes on the resulting multi-solution dataset before GRPO — creating N-modal next-token distributions that policy-gradient updates traverse more conservatively and productively. - The good: At pass@64, mid-trained models (n=64 heuristics) improve average accuracy from 46.30% (vanilla RL) to 48.17% across six benchmarks, with larger absolute gains on harder tasks (AIME 2025: 12.84%→18.66%, AMC 2023: 83.49%→85.18%). - The missing: Experiments are limited to Llama 3.2–3B and Qwen2.5–7B; no evidence at 70B+ scale, and it is unclear whether the benefit persists when the base model already has substantial implicit diversity. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers building RL post-training pipelines for small-to-medium reasoning models, especially those where pass@k at k>1 matters; those targeting pass@1 or using large models gain less. Problem & Motivation RL with verifiable rewards (RLVR) has become a standard post-training step for LLM reasoning, but recent evidence shows it primarily sharpens behaviors already in the base model rather than inducing new capabilities. A single reasoning problem can often be solved via analogy, backward reasoning, decomposition, or auxiliary-quantity introduction; a model that has seen only a narrow set of approaches in training concentrates its distribution on those strategies and RL cannot diversify beyond them. This is especially acute for smaller models (Llama 3.2–3B) that lack the implicit diversity of larger systems. The gap motivates mid-training — an intermediate fine-tuning step — as a principled way to enrich the model's prior before RL, without requiring distillation from a stronger teacher. Key Idea The core insight, formalized in Theorem 4.1, is that a policy-gradient update changes a sampled token's probability by ~ηAε² in the uni-modal regime (model near-certain of one token) but by ~ηA(1/N)(1−1/N) in the N-modal regime (probability mass spread across N dominant modes). In the N-modal regime, each update is conservative but non-negligible, and a negative update on one mode redistributes mass to the remaining N−1 alternatives rather than collapsing onto a single option. By mid-training on n distinct correct solution strategies per question, the model acquires N-modal next-token distributions at reasoning branching points, giving RL a richer probability landscape to optimize over and allowing composition of strategies within a single RL-generated chain. Methodology The pipeline has two phases. In mid-training, the base instruct model (not a stronger teacher) generates 128 candidate responses for each of 7,112 GSM8K training questions, conditioned on a heuristic description + four few-shot exemplars drawn from 64 Pólya-style heuristics (analogy, decomposition, carry-out, bolzano bisection, auxiliary elements, etc.). Candidates are first filtered for correctness via Math-Verify; survivors are scored by Skywork-Reward-V2-Llama-3.2-3B and the top-scoring correct response per question–heuristic pair is retained. This produces D_Pólya containing n solution variants per question (n ∈ {1,2,4,8,16,32,64}). The mid-training loss is average negative log-likelihood over all n trajectories per question, trained for one epoch on 4×H100s with AdamW (lr=1×10⁻⁶, cosine schedule, bfloat16). In RL, GRPO is applied on DAPO-Math-17k with rollout group size g=16, batch size 32, no KL regularization, for one epoch; reward is binary answer correctness. Baselines: zero-shot base instruct, vanilla RL (GRPO on base), and STaR+RL (STaR rationalization then GRPO). Three critical design decisions: (1) bootstrapping from the same base model avoids capability-transfer confounds from distillation; (2) filtering requires both correctness and heuristic adherence, not just stylistic variation; (3) effective batch size is scaled with n to hold total optimization steps constant at 444 across all configurations, enabling fair comparison. Key Results On Llama 3.2–3B–Instruct across Math-500, AIME 2024/2025, AMC 2023, HMMT 2025, and OlympiadBench: at pass@64, D_Pólya (n=64) reaches average 48.17% vs. 46.30% for vanilla RL and 45.69% for STaR+RL. AIME 2025 pass@64: 12.84%→18.66%; OlympiadBench: 42.13%→43.57%. At pass@1, gains are modest (11.50% for n=64 vs. 11.08% zero-shot), and STaR achieves higher average pass@1 (13.02%) than all Pólya configurations — indicating STaR better sharpens single-sample accuracy while Pólya better preserves distributional breadth. The controlled ablation (§6.2) using identical training instance counts (7,408) shows multiple-approaches-per-question outperforms single-solution-per-question by ~7% relative across all k values. Mid-training with heuristic-diverse but incorrect trajectories degrades RL below the vanilla baseline on all benchmarks — correctness is necessary, diversity alone is insufficient. Vendi Score of the mid-training data scales from 1.99 (n=2) to 40.56 (n=64), and the n=16 configuration (Vendi 13.81) outperforms teacher-distilled data (Vendi 10.95) on downstream RL pass@1 and pass@64. On Qwen2.5–7B–Instruct, n=8 is optimal (consistent with the rollout-size hypothesis g=8); n=16 mid-trained model achieves 17.06%/50.50% average pass@1/pass@64 vs. 18.47%/51.39% for zero-shot, suggesting diminishing returns when the base model already has strong priors. OOD: HumanEval 51.14%→52.82% (n=64 vs. vanilla RL); MuSR Murder Mystery 53.15%→57.36%; Team Allocations 23.46% (vanilla RL) → 39.07% (n=64). Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The individual components (self-play data generation, Pólya heuristics as prompts, GRPO RL) are known; the contribution is their systematic combination and the theoretical characterization of the N-modal regime that explains why approach diversity benefits RL. Genuinely novel: operationalizing Pólya's heuristics as a bootstrapped diversity engine, the proof that N-modal distributions slow mode collapse under policy-gradient updates, the empirical finding that the optimal n tracks the RL rollout group size g, and the correctness-necessity ablation. Not novel: the basic mid-training-then-RL recipe and GRPO itself. Rigor & Reproducibility Results are mean ± SE over 3 inference runs. Ablations cover n ∈ {1,2,4,8,16,32,64}, more-problems vs. more-approaches (§6.2), incorrect-trajectories condition (§6.3), distillation comparison (§6.4), and two model families. Theorem 4.1 is formally proved in Appendix A.6 via Taylor expansion of softmax probabilities; the proof is complete and assumptions are explicit. No code repository or trained model weights are mentioned. The LLM judge used to classify reasoning traces (GPT-4o-mini) is a proprietary model; inter-rater reliability with humans is discussed in §A.9 but inter-rater statistics are not reported in the main text. The mid-training seed data (GSM8K) and RL training data (DAPO-Math-17k) are both math-domain, which may inflate estimates of OOD generalization. Deployability (EMPIRICAL) - Practical: Runs on 4×H100 GPUs; data generation (128 samples × 64 heuristics × 7K questions through the base model + reward model scoring) is feasible for academic labs with GPU access. - Efficient: The GPU-hour cost of data generation is not reported, making the total compute budget relative to simply running more RL steps impossible to assess from the paper; mid-training itself is a single-epoch SFT at standard cost. - Effective: Evidence is solid for pass@k improvements at k≥2 on hard math benchmarks and OOD tasks; pass@1 improvement over vanilla RL is marginal and STaR still dominates at pass@1, so the approach is most justified when diverse sampling is the deployment mode. - Scalable: Tested only at 3B and 7B; the rollout-group-size sensitivity (optimal n≈g) and the observation that Qwen2.5–7B saturates at n=8 suggest that scaling behavior requires per-model tuning and is not yet characterized at 70B+. No software artifact, dataset, or trained weights are publicly released. Strengths - Theorem 4.1 and Proposition 4.2 provide a mechanistic, formally proved account of why N-modal mid-training helps RL — uncommon rigor in this space and a genuine intellectual contribution beyond empirical demonstration. - The controlled more-problems vs. more-approaches experiment (§6.2) cleanly isolates approach diversity from dataset size using identical instance counts, delivering a ~7% relative advantage that is robust across all k and all six benchmarks. - The Vendi Score analysis quantifies that self-generated Pólya data (Vendi 13.81 at n=16) is measurably more diverse than teacher-distilled data from QwQ-32B (Vendi 10.95), supporting the bootstrapping design choice over distillation without capability-transfer confounds. - The incorrect-trajectories ablation (§6.3) is a sharp negative control: it establishes that stylistic diversity without correctness actively harms RL, pinpointing what the mechanism actually requires. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The heuristics are inherently math-centric (sourced from Pólya's How to Solve It); whether a domain-general version exists is explicitly left open. Pass@1 improvement is modest, with STaR outperforming at that metric. The study uses a single STaR iteration to mitigate overfitting. The paper explicitly raises but does not resolve whether RL elicits genuinely emergent behaviors or primarily recombines pre-training knowledge. Analyst-Identified Gaps - No experiments at 70B+: the diminishing-return pattern already visible at 7B (where zero-shot outperforms all mid-trained models at pass@1) suggests the benefit may invert for large models with high intrinsic diversity, but this is untested. - GPU-hour cost of the full data-generation pipeline (128 samples × 64 heuristics × 7K questions + reward-model scoring) is not reported; without this, the cost-effectiveness comparison against more RL steps or larger RL datasets is incomplete. - The rollout-group-size hypothesis (optimal n≈g) is empirically supported but not theoretically derived; in practice, n and g are coupled hyperparameters that practitioners must jointly tune, adding non-trivial search cost. - Heuristic classification of generated traces (§6.1) uses GPT-4o-mini as judge; the fidelity of this classification — whether it reliably detects the intended Pólya strategy vs. surface text patterns — is not validated beyond the human-evaluation appendix, which reports agreement but not accuracy on known ground truth. - All RL training data (DAPO-Math-17k) is math-domain, which may overstate OOD benefit by preserving the in-domain structure; a code or science reasoning RL training set would provide a stronger test of whether N-modal distributions transfer across domains. Bottom Line A practitioner building RL post-training pipelines for 3B–7B reasoning models will find concrete, reproducible evidence that Pólya-style mid-training is worth the data generation overhead when pass@k at k>1 is the target metric; those optimizing pass@1 or working with large models with already-diverse priors will see marginal or no benefit.

HodgeCover: Higher-Order Topological Coverage Drives Compression of Sparse Mixture-of-Experts

arxiv.org · 📄 Open PDF
**TL;DR** - **What it does:** This paper introduces HodgeCover, a learning-free expert-selection algorithm for compressing sparse Mixture-of-Experts layers that uses the harmonic kernel of the simplicial Laplacian on a 2-complex built from expert merge barriers as the selection criterion, and a hybrid HodgeCover+Wanda that combines it with weight-level sparsity. - **Why it's important:** Deploying production MoE models (Mixtral, DeepSeek-V3, Qwen) on smaller hardware without retraining requires expert compression, but every existing method scores experts on pairwise signals that are provably blind to irreducible higher-order mergeability obstructions — cyclic incompatibilities among triples of experts that no pair score detects. - **How it does it:** It Hodge-decomposes the edge-supported KL merge-barrier signal on a simplicial 2-complex (vertices = experts, edges = pairwise KL barriers, faces = triplet KL barriers) to isolate the harmonic component — the fraction unreducible by any vertex potential or triangle-boundary correction — then greedily covers the harmonic-critical edges and triplet-critical triangles via a monotone submodular objective. - **The good:** At 66% expert reduction on Qwen 3.5-35B, HodgeCover+Wanda outperforms STUN+Wanda by +12.6 pp downstream-task average (74.6% vs. 62.0%), driven by GSM8K (85.5% vs. 22.1%), and ablation confirms the Hodge decomposition itself — not merely triplet input — accounts for +5.74 pp of that gap. - **The missing:** Hyperparameters (p, qT, λe, λt, α) are set by qualitative inspection on a single Qwen 3.5-35B layer; no empirical sensitivity sweep across values or models is reported, and the triangle cap (|T| ≤ 500) is fixed by wall-clock budget rather than by ablation. - **Read it or not:** Essential reading for practitioners compressing large MoE models and for researchers in topological/spectral methods applied to deep learning; skippable for those focused on fine-tuning or distillation-based compression.
Full summary
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces HodgeCover, a learning-free expert-selection algorithm for compressing sparse Mixture-of-Experts layers that uses the harmonic kernel of the simplicial Laplacian on a 2-complex built from expert merge barriers as the selection criterion, and a hybrid HodgeCover+Wanda that combines it with weight-level sparsity. - Why it's important: Deploying production MoE models (Mixtral, DeepSeek-V3, Qwen) on smaller hardware without retraining requires expert compression, but every existing method scores experts on pairwise signals that are provably blind to irreducible higher-order mergeability obstructions — cyclic incompatibilities among triples of experts that no pair score detects. - How it does it: It Hodge-decomposes the edge-supported KL merge-barrier signal on a simplicial 2-complex (vertices = experts, edges = pairwise KL barriers, faces = triplet KL barriers) to isolate the harmonic component — the fraction unreducible by any vertex potential or triangle-boundary correction — then greedily covers the harmonic-critical edges and triplet-critical triangles via a monotone submodular objective. - The good: At 66% expert reduction on Qwen 3.5-35B, HodgeCover+Wanda outperforms STUN+Wanda by +12.6 pp downstream-task average (74.6% vs. 62.0%), driven by GSM8K (85.5% vs. 22.1%), and ablation confirms the Hodge decomposition itself — not merely triplet input — accounts for +5.74 pp of that gap. - The missing: Hyperparameters (p, qT, λe, λt, α) are set by qualitative inspection on a single Qwen 3.5-35B layer; no empirical sensitivity sweep across values or models is reported, and the triangle cap (|T| ≤ 500) is fixed by wall-clock budget rather than by ablation. - Read it or not: Essential reading for practitioners compressing large MoE models and for researchers in topological/spectral methods applied to deep learning; skippable for those focused on fine-tuning or distillation-based compression. Problem & Motivation Sparse MoE models such as Mixtral, OLMoE, Qwen 3.5, and DeepSeek-V3 host dozens to hundreds of feed-forward experts per layer but activate only a small constant subset per token. Reducing expert count without retraining would let practitioners reuse pretrained checkpoints on smaller fleets. The dominant family of learning-free compressors (REAP, REAM, MC-SMoE, STUN) ranks experts using pairwise signals — output saliency, routing cosine, behavioral similarity. The structural gap is that three experts can each be pairwise compatible yet form an irreducible collective incompatibility: any score that factors through per-expert or per-pair quantities cannot detect this. Prior methods that do ingest triplets (hypergraph spectral cuts, triplet-loss penalties) aggregate them into sum-of-pairs surrogates or binary vetoes, discarding the topologically irreducible residue. The harmonic kernel of the simplicial Laplacian is the precise algebraic object capturing that residue, but topological deep learning has previously used it only as a diagnostic, never as a parameter-selection objective. Key Idea The core insight is that the edge-supported KL merge-barrier signal on a simplicial 2-complex over experts admits an orthogonal Hodge decomposition into gradient, curl, and harmonic components, and the harmonic component is exactly the part that no vertex potential (per-expert score) or triangle-boundary correction (per-face adjustment) can explain. This makes the harmonic component a principled, topology-based certificate of irreducible mergeability obstruction. Turning this decomposition into a selection objective — covering the top-p% harmonic-critical edges and top-qT% triplet-critical triangles with a greedy submodular set function — is the key move that elevates Hodge theory from analysis tool to engineering criterion. Methodology Given a sparse MoE layer with n experts, HodgeCover proceeds in four stages. (1) Barrier construction: compute all pairwise KL merge barriers bij (O(n²) forward passes on 2,048 C4-train tokens) and up to 500 sampled triplet barriers bijk for 3-cliques of the median-barrier subgraph. (2) Complex construction: form the simplicial 2-complex K = (V, E, T) with the complete edge set and the curated triangle set; apply the 1-Hodge Laplacian L1 = ∂₁ᵀ∂₁ + ∂₂∂₂ᵀ using the unweighted form so the harmonic projector is topologically invariant across layers. Solve two sequential least-squares systems via Moore-Penrose pseudoinverse to extract bgrad, bcurl, bharm. (3) Critical simplex extraction: designate top-p% edges by |bharm,e| as harmonic-critical and top-qT% triangles by |bijk| as triplet-critical. (4) Greedy coverage: maximize the monotone submodular objective Φ(S) = Σsal(i) + λe|CE(S)|/|E| + λt|CT(S)|/|T| with the standard (1 − 1/e) greedy guarantee. Non-survivors are redirected to the nearest survivor under a Hodge-weighted barrier that penalizes routing through high-harmonic edges. HodgeCover+Wanda then applies unstructured Wanda weight pruning on survivors at residual sparsity r2 ≈ 0.575 (at 66% total compression with r1 = 0.20 expert drop). Key design decisions: (a) unweighted Laplacian to decouple topology from barrier normalization — well-justified; (b) complete edge set rather than thresholded subgraph to avoid discontinuous spectral behavior — well-justified with analysis; (c) triangle cap at 500 fixed by compute budget rather than sensitivity study — not ablated. Key Results Evaluation covers OLMoE-1B-7B (64 experts, 16 layers), Qwen 3.5-35B-A3B (256 experts, 40 layers), and Qwen 3.5-122B-A10B (256 experts, 48 layers) at 33% and 66% expert reduction rates. Baselines: REAP, REAM, MC-SMoE, STUN+Wanda (published), plus REAP+Wanda and REAM+Wanda computed by the authors. Benchmarks: WikiText-103 perplexity, C4 perplexity, and 9 downstream tasks (ARC-c/e, BoolQ, HellaSwag, MMLU 5-shot, PIQA, TruthfulQA-MC2, WinoGrande, GSM8K 8-shot). At 66% on Qwen 3.5-35B-A3B, HodgeCover+Wanda achieves 74.6% DS-Avg vs. 62.0% for STUN+Wanda (+12.6 pp) and 74.1% for the matched-control REAP+Wanda (+0.5 pp); GSM8K 85.5% vs. 22.1% (STUN+W) and vs. 87.6% (REAP, expert-count axis only). At 66% on Qwen 3.5-122B-A10B, HodgeCover+Wanda achieves 75.9% DS-Avg vs. 70.8% for STUN+Wanda and 75.4% for REAP+Wanda. On the expert-count axis alone (no Wanda), HodgeCover matches REAP within ±0.3 pp DS-Avg on all four Qwen cells. The ablation on Qwen 3.5-35B-A3B at 66% shows the Hodge step accounts for +5.74 pp over Triplet-Penalty soft (same triplet input, no Hodge) and +5.7 pp over Greedy-Barrier; removing triangles entirely costs −11.5 pp; using a binary triangle veto (Triplet-Hypergraph) collapses by −30.5 pp. Harmonic energy fraction ρharm ranges 29–62% of per-layer barrier energy across the three models, confirming the obstruction is non-trivial. Plan-time for the full HodgeCover pipeline on Qwen 3.5-35B is ~480 seconds vs. ~25 seconds for REAP — a 19× overhead, offline and amortized. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The genuinely novel contribution is operationalizing the harmonic kernel of the simplicial Laplacian as a parameter-selection objective for MoE compression — prior topological deep learning work uses Hodge decomposition only as an analysis diagnostic. The inexpressibility result (Remark 1, Proposition 2) formally separates HodgeCover from all per-expert-ranking methods. The empirical finding that harmonic energy constitutes 29–62% of per-layer barrier energy in production MoE models is also new. The application of greedy submodular coverage and the composition with Wanda are standard techniques applied in a new setting. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablation structure is strong: four ablations systematically isolate the Hodge decomposition (vs. soft triplet penalty), the triangle term (vs. harmonic-edge-only), and hard vs. soft triplet constraints. The Hodge-step contribution of +5.74 pp on Qwen 3.5-35B at 66% is the sharpest number in the paper and is experimentally clean. Matched-control hybrids (REAP+Wanda, REAM+Wanda) are computed by the authors to control for Stage-2 Wanda differences — a creditable methodological choice. Error bars, multiple seeds, and significance tests are absent throughout. Hyperparameters (p = qT = 20%, λe = 1.0, λt = 0.5, α = 3.0) are set by qualitative inspection on a single layer of a single model; no empirical sensitivity grid is reported. MC-SMoE is evaluated without its mandatory KD pass (training on 2,048 tokens causes overfitting), which reduces its baseline strength and is disclosed. Calibration corpus is fixed at 2,048 C4-train tokens, matched across all methods. Code and model weights are not mentioned as released; the paper is a preprint (arXiv:2605.13997v1). Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL (compression system evaluated by downstream benchmarks and perplexity). - Practical: Runs on 2× NVIDIA RTX PRO 6000 Blackwell GPUs (98 GB each); the barrier sweep and Hodge projection fit within standard research hardware at n ≤ 256 experts, though 256-expert Qwen models require ~196 GB GPU memory for the uncompressed checkpoint before compression. - Efficient: Plan-time is ~480 seconds for Qwen 3.5-35B (vs. ~25 s for REAP), a 19× overhead paid once offline; inference throughput of the compressed model matches REAP+Wanda to within 6% at 66%; the dead-expert ratio at 66% is 1–3 pp higher than REAM+Wanda on Qwen scales, a real cost acknowledged by the authors. - Effective: Achieves best C4 perplexity on every (model, rate) cell for the hybrid axis and best WikiText perplexity on every 66% cell; the +12.6 pp DS-Avg gain over STUN+Wanda on Qwen 3.5-35B at 66% is large and driven by real task performance (GSM8K, MMLU), not perplexity artifacts. - Scalable: Evaluated at up to 122B total parameters (Qwen 3.5-122B-A10B); the pairwise sweep scales as O(n²|D|d²) — manageable at n = 256 but not evaluated beyond this expert count; the authors defer multimodal and RL post-trained MoE checkpoints to future work. The method delivers a compression pipeline (not a library or CLI), with algorithmic pseudocode and full hyperparameter tables provided but no public code repository mentioned. Strengths - The Hodge-decomposition framing is mathematically precise and identifies a structural failure mode of all prior pairwise/triplet compressors through a clean inexpressibility argument (Remark 1, Proposition 2) — this is genuine novelty, not a relabeling. - The ablation isolating the Hodge decomposition from the triplet input (+5.74 pp over Triplet-Penalty on Qwen 3.5-35B at 66%) is the strongest evidence in the paper: the improvement comes from how triplet information is processed, not merely from having it. - Diagnostic measurements (ρharm ∈ [0.29, 0.62] across 104 layers of three production models, Fig. 2) provide evidence that the harmonic obstruction is pervasive in real MoE structures, not a pathological corner case, which directly motivates why the method should work. - The choice of unweighted Laplacian (barriers enter as signal, not as operator weights) is a non-obvious but well-justified design decision that keeps the harmonic projector topologically invariant across layers and model families. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly state: (1) HodgeCover cannot recover the last few perplexity points that fine-tuning or knowledge distillation would address; (2) plan-time on 35B-parameter models is ~480 seconds (vs. ~25 s for REAP), though amortized across compression rates; (3) evaluation is restricted to language MoE; multimodal and RL post-trained checkpoints are deferred; (4) the triangle cap |T| = 500 is set by wall-clock budget. The authors confront these limitations directly and without minimization, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The hyperparameter sensitivity gap is significant: p, qT, λe, λt, α are set by qualitative inspection on one layer of one model and applied universally. The paper provides only analytical boundary behavior (saturation at p → 100%, vanishing at p → 0%) without any empirical sweep. Given that the ablation shows +5.74 pp difference from topological structure, the question of how sensitive this is to the 20% threshold is material. - The triangle cap at |T| = 500 is set by compute budget and is not ablated across values. Appendix A.9 notes that |T|/C(n,3) → 0 at Qwen scale, and that the harmonic component vanishes as |T| → C(n,3) — but where the method degrades on the path between these regimes is not measured. - At 33% compression, STUN+Wanda beats HodgeCover+Wanda on WikiText for OLMoE and Qwen 3.5-35B (e.g., 8.86 vs. 9.18 for Qwen 3.5-35B), and the paper explains this as a C4 perplexity tradeoff, but the explanation for why HodgeCover+Wanda is worse at 33% on WikiText is not mechanistically explored. - The Triplet-Penalty ablation was not run on Qwen 3.5-122B-A10B because the 35B run "cleared the ablation gating threshold" — this is an incomplete ablation on the largest model, where results might differ given the very different ρharm profile. - The matched-control REAP+Wanda closes most of STUN+Wanda's gap; the margin of HodgeCover+Wanda over REAP+Wanda at 66% is 0.3–0.8 pp DS-Avg on the two Qwen scales — real but modest, and driven by a 19× plan-time overhead versus REAP. Bottom Line A practitioner running aggressive (≥66%) learning-free compression of large production MoE models — particularly Qwen-family models — will find HodgeCover+Wanda the current best option on the aggressive frontier, at the cost of a 19× plan-time overhead over REAP; a researcher in topological methods applied to neural network compression will find the Hodge-decomposition framing and the inexpressibility result the most durable contributions.

OProver: A Unified Framework for Agentic Formal Theorem Proving

arxiv.org · 📄 Open PDF
**TL;DR** - **What it does:** This paper introduces OProver, a unified framework for agentic formal theorem proving in Lean 4 that trains a policy to iteratively revise failed proof attempts using retrieved compiler-verified proofs and raw Lean 4 compiler diagnostics, paired with OProofs, a corpus of 1.77M Lean statements, 6.86M compiler-verified proofs, and 1.07M serialized multi-round agentic proving trajectories. - **Why it's important:** Prior provers treat retrieval and compiler feedback as inference-time heuristics grafted onto a fixed model trained only on finalized proofs, creating a train–inference mismatch that leaves the richest source of supervision—failure traces—unexploited. - **How it does it:** The policy conditions on a compact state (theorem statement, top-k retrieved proofs, most-recent failed attempt, raw compiler diagnostics) and is trained end-to-end through continued pretraining followed by an iterative loop alternating SFT on round-level repair examples and GSPO reinforcement learning on hard unsolved cases, with newly verified proofs and repair trajectories recirculated into the corpus and retrieval index each round. - **The good:** OProver-32B, a 32B dense model, achieves Pass@32 of 93.3% on MiniF2F, 58.2% on ProverBench, and 11.3% on PutnamBench—outperforming or matching LongCat-Flash-Prover (560B MoE, ~17× total parameters) on three of five benchmarks, with ablations confirming that iterative compiler feedback contributes the dominant performance share (e.g., removing it drops MathOlympiad from 22.8 to 16.5 for the 32B model). - **The missing:** The ablation table never isolates retrieval augmentation alone (with feedback intact), so the independent contribution of RAG cannot be measured against the full system—only its marginal value on top of a feedback-free baseline is reported. - **Read it or not:** Worth reading for practitioners working on LLM-based formal verification or agentic post-training pipelines; the co-evolution loop and compact-state formulation are clearly described and the corpus release (OProofs) provides a concrete artifact, but readers primarily interested in tactic-level or tree-search provers will find limited relevance.
Full summary
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces OProver, a unified framework for agentic formal theorem proving in Lean 4 that trains a policy to iteratively revise failed proof attempts using retrieved compiler-verified proofs and raw Lean 4 compiler diagnostics, paired with OProofs, a corpus of 1.77M Lean statements, 6.86M compiler-verified proofs, and 1.07M serialized multi-round agentic proving trajectories. - Why it's important: Prior provers treat retrieval and compiler feedback as inference-time heuristics grafted onto a fixed model trained only on finalized proofs, creating a train–inference mismatch that leaves the richest source of supervision—failure traces—unexploited. - How it does it: The policy conditions on a compact state (theorem statement, top-k retrieved proofs, most-recent failed attempt, raw compiler diagnostics) and is trained end-to-end through continued pretraining followed by an iterative loop alternating SFT on round-level repair examples and GSPO reinforcement learning on hard unsolved cases, with newly verified proofs and repair trajectories recirculated into the corpus and retrieval index each round. - The good: OProver-32B, a 32B dense model, achieves Pass@32 of 93.3% on MiniF2F, 58.2% on ProverBench, and 11.3% on PutnamBench—outperforming or matching LongCat-Flash-Prover (560B MoE, ~17× total parameters) on three of five benchmarks, with ablations confirming that iterative compiler feedback contributes the dominant performance share (e.g., removing it drops MathOlympiad from 22.8 to 16.5 for the 32B model). - The missing: The ablation table never isolates retrieval augmentation alone (with feedback intact), so the independent contribution of RAG cannot be measured against the full system—only its marginal value on top of a feedback-free baseline is reported. - Read it or not: Worth reading for practitioners working on LLM-based formal verification or agentic post-training pipelines; the co-evolution loop and compact-state formulation are clearly described and the corpus release (OProofs) provides a concrete artifact, but readers primarily interested in tactic-level or tree-search provers will find limited relevance. Problem & Motivation: Formal theorem proving in Lean 4 requires every proof step to pass mechanical kernel verification, creating a dense, semantically rich feedback signal at each failure. Despite this, most neural provers—including recent high-performing systems like Goedel-Prover-V2 and DeepSeek-Prover-V2—are trained exclusively on finalized compiler-verified proofs. When retrieval or compiler feedback is incorporated at all, it appears only at inference time as an external wrapper, not as part of the learned policy. This train–inference mismatch means the model is never optimized to interpret diagnostics or leverage retrieved lemma patterns. Additionally, existing Lean corpora (NuminaMath-LEAN, Lean-Workbook, Goedel-Pset, etc.) record only the final verified proof, discarding the failed attempts, retrieved context, and compiler messages that actually drove proof construction—the supervision needed to learn agentic self-correction. Key Idea: The core insight is that agentic theorem proving should be formulated as a bounded multi-round Markov decision process with a compact state X_t = (s, R_t, p_{t-1}, f_{t-1}), where s is the theorem statement, R_t is the top-k retrieved compiler-verified proofs, p_{t-1} is the previous proof attempt, and f_{t-1} is the raw Lean 4 compiler diagnostic—and that this exact state formulation should be used identically during rollout collection, SFT, and RL training so the training-time interface matches proving-time interaction perfectly. Crucially, rather than projecting compiler output into a hand-designed error taxonomy, OProver passes raw textual diagnostics directly to the policy, preserving fine-grained type-mismatch, goal, and identifier information that categorical encoding would lose. Methodology: OProver has three interlocking components. (1) OProofs construction proceeds via two branches: public Lean resources (NuminaMath-LEAN, Lean-Workbook, etc.) deduplicated and then agentically proved using existing open-source provers; and raw informal math statements mined from Common Crawl and GitHub via a FastText classifier, autoformalized into Lean 4 using CriticLean, then proved. Both branches retain only Lean-verified proofs. (2) Agentic proving at inference time runs up to R refinement rounds; on failure, raw compiler feedback f_{t-1} is fed back to the policy alongside the top-k retrieved proofs from retrieval memory M (using Qwen3-8B-Embedding, selected over Goedel-Prover-V2-8B via 66.7% GPT-4o judge preference on 1,000 retrieval queries). Successful proofs are immediately indexed back into M. (3) Training proceeds in two phases: a one-time continued pretraining on a 65B-token mixture (30% Lean/OProofs, 40% Nemotron-Math-4-Plus, 20% OpenCoder, 10% ProLong-64K) yielding OProver-Base; then iterative post-training alternating SFT (cross-entropy on target proof p_t only, sequence length 40,960) and GSPO RL (reward r_t = 0.8 + 0.2·1[format correct] if Lean-verified, else 0.0; advantages normalized group-relative across all n×R rounds per theorem; n=8 rollouts, R=4 for 8B, R=2 for 32B). DAPO-style group filtering excludes fully-solved and fully-failed groups. Each iteration re-indexes newly verified proofs into M and adds repair trajectories to the SFT pool. Key design decisions: compact state (only most-recent attempt, not full history) is justified by keeping context manageable while preserving the locally-relevant correction signal; raw compiler feedback rather than taxonomy encoding preserves fine-grained repair information; round-level decomposition of trajectories provides process-level supervision without consuming full interaction histories. Key Results: OProver-32B achieves Pass@32 (n=64) of 93.3% on MiniF2F (vs. 90.4% Goedel-Prover-V2-32B with self-correction, 90.2% LongCat-Flash-Prover with TIR), 58.2% on ProverBench (vs. 57.9% LongCat), and 11.3% on PutnamBench (vs. 10.4% LongCat), and ranks second on MathOlympiadBench (22.8% vs. 27.5% LongCat TIR) and ProofNet (33.2% vs. 36.1% LongCat TIR). Even OProver-8B exceeds Goedel-Prover-V2-32B across all five benchmarks. OProver-32B surpasses DeepSeek-Prover-V2-671B on all five benchmarks despite 21× fewer parameters. Ablation (Table 3): removing iterative feedback (keeping only round 1) drops OProver-32B by 4.9 points on MiniF2F, 6.3 on MathOlympiad, 7.4 on ProofNet, 6.2 on ProverBench, and 4.3 on PutnamBench—the dominant contribution. Further removing retrieval adds 0.5–1.7 point additional drops. Iterative post-training (Figure 6): OProver-8B improves 12.3 points (79.5→91.8) over 3 post-training rounds on MiniF2F; OProver-32B improves 8.6 points (84.7→93.3) over 2 rounds, with no saturation observed. Test-time scaling (Figure 4): BestPass(B) improves consistently from B=8 to B=256 across all benchmarks, with optimal refinement depth R=16 on easier benchmarks and R=8 on PutnamBench (where low per-chain success probability penalizes narrow sampling). Novelty Classification: NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK + NEW METHOD. The genuinely novel contribution is the unified co-evolution loop: training agentic feedback-conditioned repair as a learned policy (not inference-time wrapper) and coupling it with a corpus that continuously grows from the prover's own repair traces. The compact-state formulation and GSPO pooling across n×R rounds are concrete technical choices not seen in prior provers. OProofs is the first public Lean corpus to simultaneously include retrieved proof context, compiler feedback, and multi-round repair supervision (Table 1 shows all prior datasets lack at least one of these). The broader approach—iterative self-improvement via verified proofs and failure traces—is related to STP and Seed-Prover, but those systems iterate on statement–proof pairs without serialized multi-turn trajectories. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations in Table 3 cover the two core components (feedback and retrieval) across both model sizes and all five benchmarks—this is thorough by community standards. Figure 6 tracks all intermediate checkpoints across post-training iterations, establishing monotonic improvement. No error bars or significance tests are reported (standard in this community given the computational cost). Code and model weights are publicly released at github.com/multimodal-art-projection/OProver and huggingface.co/m-a-p/oprover. The retriever comparison uses GPT-4o as a pairwise judge over 1,000 queries—an appropriate but not infallible proxy. Autoformalization quality for Branch 2 (informal→Lean) is not independently validated; the corpus trusts CriticLean's output after compiler verification, which filters syntactic correctness but not semantic faithfulness. The baselines marked with † are taken from published reports rather than re-evaluated under OProver's protocol, which may conflate differences in evaluation setup. This is a preprint; results have not undergone peer review. Deployability (EMPIRICAL): - Practical: OProver-8B and -32B are dense models deployable on commodity GPU clusters; the Lean 4 verification server runs locally with a 240-second timeout per request, which is within reach of standard academic hardware. - Efficient: Multi-round inference at up to R=16 rounds multiplies generation cost by up to 16× over single-pass provers; at the optimal budget B=256 with R=8, total compute per theorem is substantial but bounded and comparable to best-of-N sampling used by competing systems. - Effective: Ablations confirm the method solves the stated problem (agentic repair of failed proofs), with compiler feedback providing 4.3–7.4 point Pass@32 gains over single-round generation across benchmarks. - Scalable: Test-time scaling shows consistent improvement from B=8 to B=256, with diminishing returns; the co-evolution loop has been run for 2–3 iterations and shows no saturation, but whether additional iterations continue yielding gains at comparable cost is untested. The paper delivers trained model weights and a software system (Lean server + retrieval pipeline), not just a paper-form result. Strengths: - The co-evolution loop design is well-motivated and concretely specified (Algorithm 1): each component (corpus, retrieval memory, SFT data, RL hard cases) maps to a specific routing rule, making the system reproducible. - OProofs fills a genuine gap: it is the only public Lean corpus providing all six supervision dimensions (formal statements, verified proofs, NL rationale, compiler feedback, retrieved proof context, multi-round repair) simultaneously. - The compact-state formulation (condition only on most-recent attempt + feedback, not full history) is a principled design choice that aligns training and inference exactly, avoiding the common failure where a model trained on finalized proofs encounters out-of-distribution multi-turn inputs at deployment. - The test-time compute analysis (§4.2) is unusually rigorous for the field: fixing total budget B and sweeping (R, k) allocation reveals the benchmark-dependent optimal depth, providing actionable guidance for practitioners. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors do not explicitly enumerate limitations in a dedicated section. The paper acknowledges that on PutnamBench, all methods—including OProver-32B—remain below 12% Pass@32, and that BestPass shows diminishing returns with budget. The paper notes that OProofs statistics cover only the Lean 4 theorem-proving component; auxiliary pretraining corpora are excluded from reported counts. These are factual scope statements rather than critical limitations directly confronted. The lack of an explicit limitations section is a mild credibility concern. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - The ablation table (Table 3) removes feedback first, then both feedback and RAG—but never tests RAG-only removal (with feedback intact). The marginal contribution of retrieval is thus measured only on top of an already-degraded no-feedback baseline, potentially understating retrieval's independent value. - Baseline comparisons are not apples-to-apples: OProver uses multi-round rollouts (each sample = a complete trajectory of up to R rounds) while baselines use whole-proof single-pass sampling or best-of-N. Section 4.2 provides a budget-controlled comparison, but the baselines' own BestPass curves are not shown—only OProver's scaling is characterized. - The autoformalization quality of Branch 2 (Common Crawl + GitHub → Lean 4 via CriticLean) is validated only by Lean compiler acceptance, not by semantic faithfulness to the original informal claim. Semantically incorrect but syntactically valid formalizations could degrade proof quality silently. - LLM-as-judge (GPT-4o) for retriever selection is a proxy metric; direct task performance (Pass@k with and without each retriever) would be a stronger validation. - No Pass@1 numbers are reported anywhere; the paper reports exclusively Pass@32, which masks the practical single-attempt performance relevant to many real use cases. - The GSPO hyperparameter choice (n=8 rollouts, R=4 for 8B, R=2 for 32B during RL) is stated but not ablated, so the sensitivity of RL gains to these settings is unknown. Bottom Line: This is a technically solid and well-executed system paper: the co-evolution design is coherent, the OProofs corpus fills a documented gap, the ablations are honest, and the SOTA results on three of five benchmarks at 32B dense scale are genuine—but practitioners should note it is an unreviewed preprint, the multi-round inference cost is non-trivial relative to single-pass baselines, and the ablation design leaves retrieval's independent contribution unclear.

Generative Recursive Reasoning

arxiv.org · 📄 Open PDF
**TL;DR** - **What it does:** This paper introduces GRAM (Generative Recursive reAsoning Models), a framework that replaces the deterministic latent-state update in Recursive Reasoning Models with a learned stochastic Gaussian perturbation, enabling probabilistic multi-trajectory inference trained via amortized variational inference. - **Why it's important:** All existing RRMs follow a single fixed latent trajectory, making them structurally incapable of representing multiple valid solutions or escaping local minima — a fundamental failure mode on any constraint satisfaction task where the solution space is multimodal. - **How it does it:** At each recursion step, GRAM computes a deterministic update u_t, then samples a state-dependent Gaussian noise ε_t ~ N(μ_θ(u_t), σ²_θ(u_t)I) and adds it as a residual, inducing a distribution over reasoning trajectories that is trained with an ELBO objective and exploited at inference via parallel trajectory sampling. - **The good:** GRAM with N=20 parallel samples at 16 iterations achieves 97.0% on Sudoku-Extreme and outperforms TRM at 320 iterations (90.5%), reaching the same accuracy ceiling with a parallelizable rather than sequential compute budget. - **The missing:** All evaluation targets toy-scale synthetic benchmarks (9×9 grids, 8–10 node graphs, 28×28 images); there is no evaluation on any real-world reasoning task where GRAM's design principles would need to compete with autoregressive systems under realistic conditions. - **Read it or not:** Worth reading for researchers designing latent-space or recursive reasoning architectures for combinatorial or constraint satisfaction tasks; practitioners focused on LLM-scale reasoning will find the experimental scope too restricted to act on.
Full summary
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces GRAM (Generative Recursive reAsoning Models), a framework that replaces the deterministic latent-state update in Recursive Reasoning Models with a learned stochastic Gaussian perturbation, enabling probabilistic multi-trajectory inference trained via amortized variational inference. - Why it's important: All existing RRMs follow a single fixed latent trajectory, making them structurally incapable of representing multiple valid solutions or escaping local minima — a fundamental failure mode on any constraint satisfaction task where the solution space is multimodal. - How it does it: At each recursion step, GRAM computes a deterministic update u_t, then samples a state-dependent Gaussian noise ε_t ~ N(μ_θ(u_t), σ²_θ(u_t)I) and adds it as a residual, inducing a distribution over reasoning trajectories that is trained with an ELBO objective and exploited at inference via parallel trajectory sampling. - The good: GRAM with N=20 parallel samples at 16 iterations achieves 97.0% on Sudoku-Extreme and outperforms TRM at 320 iterations (90.5%), reaching the same accuracy ceiling with a parallelizable rather than sequential compute budget. - The missing: All evaluation targets toy-scale synthetic benchmarks (9×9 grids, 8–10 node graphs, 28×28 images); there is no evaluation on any real-world reasoning task where GRAM's design principles would need to compete with autoregressive systems under realistic conditions. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers designing latent-space or recursive reasoning architectures for combinatorial or constraint satisfaction tasks; practitioners focused on LLM-scale reasoning will find the experimental scope too restricted to act on. Problem & Motivation Recursive Reasoning Models (RRMs) such as HRM and TRM use iterative latent-state refinement with shared transition functions, decoupling reasoning depth from parameter count and output length. This is appealing for constraint propagation, state tracking, and multi-step inference. However, all existing RRMs are deterministic: they follow a single fixed latent trajectory and converge to one prediction. This is structurally inadequate for problems with multiple valid solutions — such as N-Queens or graph coloring — where deterministic models exhibit mode collapse and coverage rates below 36% regardless of depth. More generally, any problem where a single refinement path can become trapped in a suboptimal attractor suffers from this single-trajectory design. The gap is the absence of principled multi-hypothesis exploration within the RRM paradigm. Key Idea The core insight is to reinterpret the deterministic latent update as the mean of a learned stochastic distribution: instead of z_t = u_t (a fixed next state), GRAM adds a state-dependent Gaussian perturbation ε_t ~ N(μ_θ(u_t), σ²_θ(u_t)I) to produce z_t = u_t + ε_t. This converts repeated recursive computation from a deterministic fixed point into a distribution over trajectories — a latent-variable generative model trained with an ELBO. The key distinguishing move is applying stochasticity at the level of the abstract high-level state (not output decoding, not random initialization), and training the noise distribution with a target-conditioned variational posterior so the model learns which directions to explore, not just random noise. Methodology GRAM maintains a hierarchical latent state z = (h, l): the high-level component h carries abstract reasoning state and is updated stochastically once per outer step; the low-level component l carries fine-grained computation and is refined K times deterministically per outer step (K=6 for Sudoku, K=4 otherwise; T=3 outer steps per supervision step). The full recursion is organized into N_sup=16 supervision steps applied sequentially, with the terminal state of each step passed as input to the next. Training maximizes an ELBO: L_ELBO = E_q[log p(y|z_T, x)] − KL(q_φ(ε|u, y) || p_θ(ε|u)). Because backpropagating through the full T_total = T × N_sup trajectory is prohibitive, gradients are propagated only through the final transition of each supervision step (truncated BPTT), a standard approximation validated empirically by showing that the surrogate objective tracks the full ELBO monotonically during training. At inference, GRAM samples N parallel trajectories from the learned prior and selects the best via majority voting or a Latent Process Reward Model (LPRM) — a value head v_ψ(z_t) trained jointly to predict trajectory accuracy from the latent state. Adaptive Computation Time (ACT) via a Q-learning halt head allows early termination. Three critical design decisions: (1) stochasticity injected only at the high level (ablation confirms low-level noise provides no benefit); (2) learned directional guidance μ_θ is essential in multi-solution settings (stochasticity-only collapses on N-Queens at 50.27%); (3) truncated ELBO surrogate consistently tracks full ELBO (Figure 8 confirms monotone co-decrease on both Sudoku and N-Queens). Key Results On Sudoku-Extreme, GRAM achieves 97.0% vs. TRM 87.4%, HRM 55.0%, Looped TF 61.3% (all at 16 supervision steps; GRAM 10M params vs HRM 27M, TRM 7M). On ARC-AGI-1: GRAM 52.0% vs TRM 44.6%, HRM 40.3%. On ARC-AGI-2: GRAM 11.1% vs TRM 7.8%, HRM 5.0%. Width scaling: N=20 parallel samples at 16 iterations outperforms all deterministic baselines at 320 iterations including TRM (97.0% vs 90.5%), with the advantage of parallelizability. On N-Queens 8×8: GRAM 99.7% accuracy and 90.3% solution coverage (20 samples) vs. best generative baseline MDLM 96.1%/87.2%; deterministic recursive baselines cover at most 36.1%. On 8-vertex Graph Coloring: GRAM 2.7 conflict edges vs AR 19.0 and TRM 109.3. Unconditional Sudoku generation: 99.05% validity with 10.9M params and 16 steps vs D3PM-Uniform-Big 91.33% with 55.1M params and 1,000 steps. Binarized MNIST: IS 2.04, FID 73.34 at 256 inference steps vs D3PM 1.86/74.03 at 1,000 steps. Baselines within the recursive model class are current and reproduced under identical settings. LLM comparisons (o3-mini-high, GPT-5.2, Grok-4-thinking, Gemini 3 Pro) are reported as reference points for benchmark difficulty, not controlled baselines. The surprising finding that Deepseek-R1 (671B parameters) scores 0% on Sudoku-Extreme — matching direct prediction baselines — underscores that these benchmarks probe a reasoning axis not addressed by pretraining scale alone. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW FORMULATION. The genuinely novel contribution is framing stochastic latent-state dynamics (previously used in VRNNs, DSSMs, and Dreamer-family world models for temporal observation modeling) as computation rather than perception: latent transitions define possible reasoning trajectories, supporting conditional and unconditional generation from a single compact model. Width-based inference-time scaling via parallel trajectory sampling within the RRM paradigm is novel. The underlying components (variational inference, hierarchical latent dynamics, truncated BPTT, ACT) are individually established; the contribution lies in their coherent integration and reinterpretation as a reasoning framework. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present and informative: Table 3a shows cumulative contribution of deep supervision, hierarchical recursion, and stochastic guidance; Table 3b isolates the roles of stochasticity vs. directional guidance. Error bars (mean ± std) are reported across runs in Table 1. The surrogate ELBO approximation is empirically validated by tracking both the training objective and the full ELBO throughout training (Figure 8), showing monotone co-decrease. Code is referenced at a website (ahn-ml.github.io/gram-website) but no explicit public repository or model release URL appears in the paper; this is a preprint (arXiv:2605.19376). Methodological concern: the KL coefficient β is tuned per task over a wide range (0.04–0.5) and no β sensitivity ablation is provided, indicating possible fragility. The ARC-AGI comparison with TRM is partially confounded by augmentation: TRM's published numbers use 1,000 augmentations per problem, while the paper's stated comparison is at a fixed epoch/hyperparameter budget; Appendix D.2 addresses this partially but the main table does not make augmentation regime explicit for all methods. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL - Practical: Trained on 8× NVIDIA RTX 4090 GPUs; training times range from 1 hour (N-Queens 8×8) to 5 days (ARC-AGI). Inference with N=20 parallel samples is feasible on consumer GPU hardware. - Efficient: GRAM (10M params) is more parameter-efficient than HRM (27M) and comparable to TRM (7M). Parallel sampling multiplies inference FLOPs by N, but this is offset by the ability to reduce sequential recursion depth; N=20 at 16 iters achieves what deterministic baselines need 320 iters to reach, and the N trajectories are fully parallelizable unlike sequential depth. - Effective: Achieves best accuracy and coverage among comparable recursive reasoning models on all five task families tested; the structural advantage on multi-solution tasks (mode collapse of deterministic baselines is irreversible regardless of depth) is well-established. However, all evidence is on small-scale toy benchmarks. - Scalable: The sequential nature of deep supervision — N_sup gradient updates per example — is explicitly acknowledged as a barrier to scaling toward larger foundation models. No experiments beyond toy scale are presented; scalability to real-world problem sizes is claimed but not evidenced. The paper delivers a model and training procedure; code availability is unclear from the paper text alone. Strengths - Framing stochastic latent-state transitions as a distribution over reasoning trajectories (rather than observation uncertainty) is a conceptually clean contribution that bridges two previously separate literatures: probabilistic SSMs and recursive reasoning architectures. - Width-based inference scaling via parallel trajectories is practically useful because it is embarrassingly parallelizable, unlike sequential depth scaling, and the paper provides a concrete mechanism (LPRM) for trajectory selection without external verifiers. - The ablation precisely characterizes the roles of each component: deterministic guidance alone (variance → 0) causes catastrophic overfitting to the target (0% on both tasks); stochasticity without learned direction works for unimodal tasks but collapses on multi-solution problems (50.27% N-Queens); the full combination is required for robust performance. - The unconditional generation capability emerging from the same architecture — with validity and quality competitive with diffusion baselines at a fraction of the parameter count and step count — is a non-obvious and empirically well-supported finding. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors directly state that the sequential nature of deep supervision limits training efficiency compared to Transformers, posing a significant barrier to scaling GRAM toward larger foundation models. They also note that multi-sample inference may increase compute and energy costs at scale, and that real-world deployment requires rigorous uncertainty calibration. These are confronted transparently rather than minimized, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - All benchmarks are synthetic toy problems (9×9 Sudoku, 8–10 node graphs, 8×8/10×10 N-Queens, 28×28 images); there is no evidence the framework transfers to any real-world reasoning task, language understanding, or planning problem where practical impact is claimed. - The KL coefficient β is tuned per task across a 12× range (0.04 to 0.5) with no sensitivity analysis; this suggests significant hyperparameter fragility that is uncharacterized. - The augmentation regime is inconsistent across the TRM vs. GRAM ARC-AGI comparison in Table 8: TRM's published numbers rely on 1,000 augmentations per test instance, making the main table comparison potentially misleading until Appendix D.2 partially unpacks it. - Parallel trajectory sampling is functionally similar to Best-of-N with a learned process reward model — a well-studied technique in the LLM literature. No comparison to BoN with task-specific verifiers (which would be trivial to implement for Sudoku and graph coloring) is provided, leaving open whether the latent-trajectory structure adds anything beyond what external verification already enables. - No wall-clock latency comparison is reported; it is possible that GRAM N=20 at 16 steps is slower in wall time than TRM at 320 steps despite equivalent accuracy, depending on GPU utilization and memory overhead. Bottom Line A researcher building compact recursive or latent-space reasoning systems for constraint satisfaction or multi-solution combinatorial problems gains a concrete and well-ablated design principle from reading this paper; practitioners focused on LLM-scale reasoning or real-world applications will find the experimental scope too restricted to draw transferable conclusions.

Teaching Language Models to Think in Code

arxiv.org · 📄 Open PDF
**TL;DR** - **What it does:** This paper introduces THINC (Thinking in Code), a training framework that restructures tool-integrated reasoning so that code itself serves as the primary reasoner — replacing the interleaved natural-language/code paradigm — through trajectory distillation, supervised fine-tuning, and reinforcement learning, producing THINC-4B which achieves 78.1% avg@16 across five competition-level math benchmarks. - **Why it's important:** Interleaved tool-integrated reasoning systems routinely let NL arithmetic errors propagate silently into code, use code only as a post-hoc verifier rather than a solver, and duplicate work between NL and code; these structural flaws corrupt final answers and degrade robustly under execution failures, limiting the ceiling of LLM mathematical reasoning. - **How it does it:** THINC constrains every trajectory to a single NL planning block followed by code-only reasoning blocks connected solely through interpreter execution outputs, then trains this format via 12.2k distilled trajectories (SFT) and three-stage GRPO/DAPO reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards. - **The good:** THINC-4B achieves 78.1% avg@16 across five competition benchmarks, outperforming all TIR baselines including the much larger rStar2-Agent-14B and ReTool-32B, surpassing Qwen3-235B-A22B-Thinking on four of five benchmarks, and maintaining 64–69% recovery@3 under consecutive code failures versus near-collapse for interleaved baselines. - **The missing:** All experiments are confined to 1.7B and 4B parameter models and competition-level mathematics; whether the code-centric format transfers to larger scales or other tool-integrated domains (e.g., web search, multi-tool agentic tasks) is entirely untested. - **Read it or not:** Read it if you work on mathematical reasoning, tool-integrated LLM training, or RL post-training — the structural diagnosis of interleaved TIR is sharp and the ablation against ASTER-4B (matched base, teacher, and RL pipeline) isolates the trajectory format as the source of gain.
Full summary
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces THINC (Thinking in Code), a training framework that restructures tool-integrated reasoning so that code itself serves as the primary reasoner — replacing the interleaved natural-language/code paradigm — through trajectory distillation, supervised fine-tuning, and reinforcement learning, producing THINC-4B which achieves 78.1% avg@16 across five competition-level math benchmarks. - Why it's important: Interleaved tool-integrated reasoning systems routinely let NL arithmetic errors propagate silently into code, use code only as a post-hoc verifier rather than a solver, and duplicate work between NL and code;
